properties.description: This mythical water bull was said to live in a lochan on St Kilda until it was killed by the islanders. It was said that any calf born with small or deformed ears was thought to be from a union between a Tarbh-uisge and a cow.

properties.name: Great Auk
properties.description: The last known Great Auk sighting in the UK took place on the little island of Stac-an-Armin. The unfortunate bird was beaten to death by three men who mistook the creature for a witch that had summoned a storm.

properties.name: Blocked Door
properties.description: Having just dropped anchor in the bay, the captain of a ship was surprised when a mermaid stuck her head out of the sea and asked if the anchor could be moved, as it had landed on the entrance to her home. The captain was happy to comply.

properties.name: Long Sea Beast
properties.description: This sea serpent was observed moving swiftly through the water, vanishing from view after a sailing boat moved too close to it.

properties.name: Howling Creature
properties.description: Heard but never seen, a creature was said to howl close to the reef when a storm was due.
properties.date: Weather Dependent: prior to a storm
properties.location_name: St Pierre du Bois (Guernsey) - Rocquaine Bay

properties.name: Cow Bone
properties.description: The church is home to a bone just under two metres in length, which is claimed to have come from a giant cow. The cow would provide free milk for the whole village until approached by a jealous witch who made the creature try to fill a perforated bucket - the cow dropped dead of exhaustion.

properties.name: Goat Man
properties.description: The occupant of a car reported seeing a thin bear-like creature standing on its hind legs (also described as a man-goat) that briefly became illuminated in their headlights. The creature stood six foot (1.8 metres) tall, had short legs, and crossed the road before disappearing from sight.

properties.name: Bluish Creature
properties.description: A skipper of a fishing boat reported seeing a creature which resembled an upturned boat with fins and a small hump. The visible part of the body was some 68 foot (21 metres) long, although the tail was not visible, and while resembling a whale, the skipper said the head was much flatter than a whale.

properties.name: Brown Creature
properties.description: A large crowd witnessed a huge, brown creature in the sea. Short fins could be seen emerging from the water and the creature, some 46 metres long, moved at around six miles per hour.

properties.name: Black Creature
properties.description: A large black thing was seen emerge from the sea and cross the road. It was blamed for causing nearby wooden gates to shake even though there was no wind.

properties.name: Talking Horse
properties.description: A man passing through the area spotted a saddled horse by the loch but decided against riding the animal. Shortly after, the horse passed the man and spoke to him, stating that the man would have drowned if he had tried to ride the horse, and also bestowed the date that the man would die.
properties.location_name: Strathcoil, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - Lake north of Aird a' Chaoil

properties.name: Grazing
properties.description: Faced with crossing a flooded stream by foot, one man spotted a black horse nearby and mounted it. Unfortunately, the horse was a kelpie, and dived into the deepest part of the stream, drowning the man. On another occasion, the kelpie took the form of an old man, only revealing its true form when bashed on the head (not a recommended procedure to determine whether someone is really a kelpie).
properties.location_name: Strichen (Aberdeenshire) - Deep pool near Braco Farm (likely to no longer exist)

properties.name: The Stronsa Beast
properties.description: This grey, seventeen metre long creature was first seen floating dead in the sea, before a storm washed it on the beach. It reportedly possessed six legs and had a mane along its back and tail. Another storm which hit the island soon after the discovery destroyed the corpse. The creature was finally declared to be a rotting basking shark carcass, though the body was five metres longer than the largest basking shark ever recorded.

properties.name: Python?
properties.description: There were reports of a large snake, presumed to be a python, observed in people's gardens along this road and the nearby wooded area. One witness observed the creature swimming in her pond.

properties.name: Black-Browed Albatross
properties.description: Named Albert, the Black-Browed Albatross spotted on the island was considered lost, as he was several thousand miles from his normal habitat.

properties.name: Ninety Foot Long Creature
properties.description: Two excise officers reportedly fired at a ninety foot (thirty metre) long monster as it charged towards them at some sixty miles per hour, resulting in the creature diving. Local fishermen also spotted the monster, and it was also claimed that a 100 tonne yacht was lifted out of the water when it encountered the beast.

properties.name: Crocodile
properties.description: In 2008, a witness reported seeing a one metre long crocodile in this body of water. The location was checked by a member of the Centre for Fortean Zoology, Gwilym Games. Seven years later, video footage caught a bird being attacked by something in the water, with various people claiming the creature more likely to be a terrapin or a pike rather than a croc.

properties.name: Long Necked Creature
properties.description: A witness spotted a creature with a three-foot (ninety centimetre) long neck at the base of a cliff. The creature swam off at tremendous speed. The witness was familiar with the usual objects mistaken for sea serpents.
properties.location_name: Tenby (Clackmannanshire) - Bottom of a cliff / off coast

properties.name: Bear
properties.description: Several witnesses reported seeing a bear on the outskirts of the nearby forest.

properties.name: Strange Animals
properties.description: Perhaps more legend than cryptozoological, but this wooded area is said to be home to strange water creatures and colourful non-native birdlife.

properties.name: Creature with Many Humps
properties.description: Witnessed by three women sitting on a lawn, this sixty-foot long sea creature with an estimated thirty humps was said to move at great speed.

properties.name: Wild Dogs?
properties.description: A pair of large dog-like creatures have been observed in this area and are thought to live in the nearby woodland. The disappearance of neighbourhood cats has been blamed on the creatures.

properties.name: Naked Mermaid?
properties.description: William Munro, a schoolmaster from Thurso, watched a naked woman combing her hair on a rock in the bay. After a few minutes she dived under the water and vanished.

properties.name: Wolf
properties.description: A taxi driver reported seeing a large, silver grey creature resembling a wolf eating something in the middle of the road. As the driver passed the creature, it looked up briefly before continuing to eat.
properties.location_name: Thurton (Norfolk) - A146 northbound, between Ferry Road and Hall Road

properties.name: Sea Serpent
properties.description: A student and a chaplain watched a large sea beast swim by while watching from this point.

properties.name: Lizard-like Creature
properties.description: A fishing boat reported being attacked by this creature, resembling a lizard but fifty foot (fifteen metres) in length with a long neck. The creature took a bite out of the boat before retreating into the deep.
properties.location_name: Tiree (Argyll and Bute) - Waters off the island

properties.name: Each-uisge
properties.description: A young woman invited an Each-uisge in the guise of an old woman into her shieling overnight only to awake during the night to find it eating her companion. On discovering the Each-uisge, the young woman fled, only managing to escape the clutches of the beast by crossing a flowing stream that the Each-uisge was unable to cross.

properties.name: Snake
properties.description: A boy reported seeing a large snake-like creature a few hundred metres off the coast.

properties.name: Captured Sea Serpent
properties.description: Though a good description of the creature is lacking, fishermen from this area chased and killed a sea monster, the body of which they brought back to land. The carcass was taken into a shop along Grey Street, and entry was charged at several shillings per view.
properties.location_name: Tynemouth (Durham) - Coastline off Cullercoats, and 57 Grey Street

properties.name: Drowned Beast
properties.description: A monster which made a nuisance of itself in the neighbourhood was killed by the hero Huw Gadarn, who drowned the creature. Another tale says that the hero involved was King Arthur. The area is also known for its fairies and was said to be the place where the legendary fairy cows first came from (before they vanished after one was almost butchered for being too old).
properties.location_name: Tywyn (formerly known as Towyn) (Gwynedd) - Llyn Barfog (aka Bearded Lake)

properties.name: Black Horse
properties.description: To pass the time, a young herder rolled several boulders into a lake - the splashing caused an Each-uisge to appear out of the disturbed water in the guise of black horse.
properties.location_name: Uig, Isle of Skye (Highland) - Unnamed lake

properties.name: Woman in the Sea
properties.description: Caught in a storm at sea, Lachlan McArthur watched a creature resembling 'a woman in upper parts' rise from the water, leaving him terrified.
properties.location_name: Uist (Outer Hebrides) - Sea south of islands, heading towards Skye

properties.name: Water Horse
properties.description: A tired traveller mounted a horse he found at the side of the loch only to find that the creature tried to drag him into the waters. The traveller managed to jump clear just before the horse entered the water - the horse disappeared in a blue haze.

properties.name: Wolf?
properties.description: Two witnesses encountered a 'strange animal' on multiple occasions. It was described as larger than a foxhound, with a big head, long jaw, and a very long tail that was bushy at the tip. and long black legs. One witness said it resembled wolves he had seen at the zoo.
properties.location_name: Vale of Lorton (Cumbria) - Low Fell range

properties.name: Each-Uisge
properties.description: A grieving widow was comforted by an Each-uisge which took the guise of man who appeared at her cottage after the death of her daughter. He used magic to keep the fire alight before vanishing in the morning.
properties.location_name: Vaterstein, Isle of Skye (Highland) - Exact location not known

properties.name: Non Sinking Church
properties.description: Legend said that building materials for the church were carried into position by a water kelpie. The creature said if anyone working for the church ever took their own life, the church would sink into a great lake which lay deep under the building. In the eighteenth century the minister of the parish committed suicide and some stories say many people gathered near the church to watch it being consumed into the ground. Needless to say, many people were also left disappointed when their expectations were not met.

properties.name: The Brentford Griffins
properties.description: A griffin given by King Charles II to Nell Gwynn made its home on the Brentwood Eyot, joined by another one many years later which was brought to the region by Sir Joseph Banks. The pair of mythological creatures are said by some to remain in the area.
properties.location_name: W5 (Greater London) - Brentwood Eyot and general area

properties.name: Wallaroo
properties.description: This medium-sized marsupial was spotted in Cornwall.

properties.name: Long Creature
properties.description: England's deepest lake is reportedly home to an unknown creature several metres in length.
properties.location_name: Wast Water (Cumbria) - Water of the lake

properties.name: Black Winged Creature
properties.description: Charles Appleby was found dead in his room, large claw marks on his arms and neck, the door and windows locked from the inside. Witnesses outside the building who could see into his room through a window said they could see the man fighting a shadowy bird like creature, though it was at least the same size as Appleby. Reports of the creature continued for a while afterwards, and one team of investigators who put down chalk dust during their stakeout recording finding large claw marks in the powder the following day.
properties.location_name: WC1 (Greater London) - Old Chambers, Lincoln's Inn (no longer standing)

properties.name: Glowing Owls
properties.description: Barn owls observed throughout Norfolk were reportedly glowing in the dark, as if they were large fireflies.

properties.name: Sea Horse
properties.description: A six metre long monster with the head and mane of a horse and large eyes was spotted by a team of fishermen.

properties.name: Long Neck
properties.description: Two crew members, one of whom being the captain, spotted a two metre long snakelike neck twice rise from the river, the monster's head briefly looking around before vanishing.
properties.location_name: Westcliff on Sea (Essex) - Mouth of the Thames

properties.name: Four Legged Beast
properties.description: The Captain of the HMS Caesar reported seeing a reptilian creature leaping out the sea, which he compared to a 'skinned chow dog'(!), though much larger in size.

properties.name: Long Necked Creature
properties.description: Three witnesses watched for several minutes a strange creature possessing a long neck swimming around in the mist covered sea.

properties.name: Like a Sea-Horse
properties.description: A creature which had the top half of a sea-horse but with the bottom part silver and covered in fins was spotted here by one witness reportedly hesitant in speaking of the sighting until they came across an earlier account describing a similar creature.

properties.name: Nygole
properties.description: A water horse known as a Nygole was found by a traveller returning from the kriegs at Skura. Mistaking the creature for a normal horse, the traveller mounted it, only realising his mistake after the Nygole set off at a fantastic speed. The traveller escaped the creature after driving his knife into its back.

properties.name: Cockatrice
properties.description: Taking up residence under the priory, this monster was finally killed (after many, many failed attempts) when a man named Green tricked it into attacking a large, mirrored sheet of polished steel - when it dropped to the floor semi-conscious, he penetrated its scales with a spear. The building is also reported haunted by a couple of nuns.

properties.name: Wolf
properties.description: Keith McDowell reported seeing an Alsatian sized creature, silver in colour with a black streak running along its back, with a bushy tail. The animal was circling calves in a field, before running off into the nearby country park.

properties.name: Sea Beastie
properties.description: The crew of a cargo ship reported having a strange fish follow their craft for half an hour. It was described as having a three metre long body, 1.2 metre long neck and an oval head.

properties.name: 'Ostrich'
properties.description: A rhea, though referred to as an ostrich in the local press, spotted on the line between Ipswich and Saxmundham forced a train to slow near Wickham Market.

properties.name: Smelly Creature
properties.description: A driver who had pulled over to wipe his windscreen spotted a strange creature standing around 140 centimetres tall, with a glowing, oblong shaped head. The beast emanated an acrid smell. The driver jumped back into his car and quickly drove off.
properties.location_name: Winterfold (Surrey) - Unknown road, near deserted mansion

properties.name: The Peel Street Monster
properties.description: After several attacks on children by an unknown creature, one seventeen year old boy managed to kill the 'monster'. No one could identify the creature, larger than a rat with bulging eyes, although another unknown entity was found dead within 24 hours and was named as a coatimundi, a mammal from the raccoon family.
properties.location_name: Wolverhampton (West Midlands) - Peel Street and Brickkiln Street

properties.name: Man Monkey
properties.description: This strange black creature with glowing white eyes leapt onto the back of a horse pulling a cart - when its owner tried to hit the mysterious creature, his whip passed straight through its body.

properties.name: Giant Eel
properties.description: A huge eel, some ten metres in length was chased into this cave system by angry fishermen.

properties.name: Water Cows
properties.description: A farmer managed to capture and breed a herd of the mythical water cows found in the loch. His herd returned to the loch when the mother of the cattle called out from the water - the roar caused nearby hills to shake.

properties.name: Trow
properties.description: A shipwrecked sailor made his way to Windhouse on Christmas Eve, only to find the family in the process of leaving. They explained to the sailor that every Christmas Eve, terrible things would happen, and a death would normally occur. The sailor offered to spend the night in the house, and sure enough, after sunset, the sailor encountered a huge Trow. The sailor grabbed an axe and gave chase, catching up with the creature outside and giving it a whack (or two). When the family returned home, the sailor took them to the spot, where the body had vanished, leaving only bright green holly.
properties.location_name: Yell (Shetland) - Area around Windhouse, exact spot said to be known to the local population.

properties.name: Serpent
properties.description: A serpent took up residence here and prevented people travelling to the St Ives market. The scrubland around the cairn was set ablaze, either killing the serpent or driving it away.
properties.location_name: Zennor (Cornwall) - Cairn which was known as 'Burning 'Mountain', on the road between Zennor and St Ives

properties.name: White Hare
properties.description: The manifestation of a white hare would warn the Kerr family of impending doom. The site known as Lover's Leap marked the spot where a Kerr woman was found dead (along with her lover) shortly after this magic white hare was spotted.
properties.location_name: Airdrie (Lanarkshire) - Area which was once known as Lover's Leap, on the road towards Faskine

properties.name: Curse
properties.description: One long, detailed curse resulted in the Erskines' losing their home (amongst other personal disasters), and now the castle remains empty.

properties.name: Cursed Bottles
properties.description: A collection of bottles on display in the pub are reportedly cursed - the last person to touch them died soon after, and now they are covered up to avoid further mishaps.
properties.location_name: Alnwick (Northumberland) - Ye Olde Cross public house

properties.name: Chained Oak
properties.description: A local legend says that the chain around the tree holds the branches in place, as when a branch falls, a member of the Earl of Shrewsbury's family dies. One version of the story says the curse began after the earl refused to give money to an old woman.
properties.location_name: Alton (Staffordshire) - The Old Oak (tree close to village)

properties.name: Family Curse
properties.description: A curse placed on the castle ensured its ultimate ruin - three owners went bankrupt, and a fourth owner and his family were drowned in the nearby loch when the ice cracked whilst they skated in winter.

properties.name: Granite Stones
properties.description: A legend says that five cylinder shaped stones mark the spot where the last of the Comyns family is buried. The stones vanish and reappear depending on how the House of Rothiemurchus is fairing, but if moved or taken by a human hand, that person is doomed to die. One story says the stones where stolen by a group of people who were later found dead in a car, the stones discovered standing upright nearby.

properties.name: Curse of Ballinacourty
properties.description: A mother whose son was hanged by a local sheriff for being 'lazy' cursed all who lived in the area to endure the heartbreak she felt.
properties.location_name: Ballinacourty (County Waterford) - Ballinacourty house (no longer standing), and general area

properties.name: No Ploughing
properties.description: A farmer who tried to plough this sacred land died after falling and banging his head. The two horses drawing the plough died the following day.

properties.name: The Laidly Worm
properties.description: This area was, for a brief time, home to a large slimy serpent - it turned out to be the daughter of the king, transformed by a curse from her evil stepmother. The king's son discovered the truth, saved his sister, and turned his mother into a toad. Another variant of the story says a dragon held the king's daughter hostage until she was saved by a knight who came from overseas.

properties.name: Animal Sickness
properties.description: A local story claimed that any disturbance to the fort would result in livestock death, although the farmer's wife disbelieved the tales and dug out an old well on the site. Cows soon fell ill, and calves died. The warning heeded, the woman filled in the well, and as the last stone put in place, a dreadful, disembodied laugh could be heard. The sick cows recovered.
properties.location_name: Bantry (County Cork) - Farm near old Danish fort

properties.name: Druid's Curse
properties.description: A sixty centimetre stone head, thought to be Celtic, was deemed to be cursed after several people who encountered it became bankrupt, ill or died.

properties.name: Gypsy Curse
properties.description: A local legend says that a community of gypsies were evicted from the land many years ago, enabling the construction of the stadium. St Andrews is one of several football grounds around the country that claims this gypsy curse is responsible for any bad luck they encounter.
properties.location_name: Birmingham (West Midlands) - St Andrews, Birmingham City Football Club

properties.name: Cursed Giant
properties.description: The chime was once a lush area, rich in vegetation and the hunting ground of a giant who feasted upon children. A holy man cursed the giant and the area, ensuring nothing would ever grow there again.
properties.location_name: Blackgang Chime (Isle of Wight) - Cave in the area (no longer present?)

properties.name: Woman in Black
properties.description: When the skull of a local girl was displayed here, several people reported seeing a spectral female figure standing by it. The skull's owner was convinced the item was cursed and died a week after finally giving it away.

properties.name: Cursed Stone
properties.description: When the waters are low, an inscribed stone can be seen in the river - it is believed cursed, and anyone who meddles with it is doomed. Adding to the treachery of the waters, a white horse is said to patrol the river, looking for people to drown.

properties.name: Smuggler Curse
properties.description: A curse was placed on this building after the vicar attempted to stop local smuggling activities. The building no longer stands, but the area (which now has shops built on top) is still said to be unlucky and haunted by the sounds of footsteps.
properties.location_name: Bognor Regis (Sussex) - The Old Vicarage, Sudley Road

properties.name: Sir Thomas Boleyn Drives Headless Horses
properties.description: The father of Anne Boleyn is said to be cursed with driving his coach over forty (or eleven) Norfolk bridges. One of the passing points is Breckles Hall, where the spectre once scared a poacher to death (another version states that the poacher was taken by the Devil because of his constant law breaking). Locals say that anyone who views the coach is dragged down to hell.

properties.name: Cedar of Lebanon
properties.description: A local legend said that Lady Margaret had thrown herself off a tower here, her blood spilling on the tree which resulted in it being cursed. If a branch fell from the tree, a member of the family residing at the hall was said to die soon after.
properties.location_name: Bretby (Derbyshire) - Tree on the grounds of Bretby Hospital (no longer standing)

properties.name: Cursed Cranium
properties.description: The cursed skull created havoc whenever removed from the property, so a solution-focused individual finally walled it up in a room of the hall.

properties.name: Cursed Skull
properties.description: When the skull was last removed from the hall (as a joke), it was blamed for spontaneous fires and family deaths. The skull was quickly returned and has not left since.
properties.location_name: Browsholme (Lancashire) - Browsholme Hall (referred to as Bowland Hall by Eric Maple)

properties.name: Maude Carew
properties.description: Cursed by a monk after Maude murdered the Duke of Gloucester in 1447, her shade is now said to appear once a year. The alleged ghost often brings dozens of people to the area in the hope of seeing her.
properties.location_name: Bury St Edmunds (Suffolk) - St Mary's Churchyard

properties.name: Petrified Sack of Corn
properties.description: The large rock on top of the hill was once a sack of corn owned by a selfish farmer. The farmer refused to give a handful of grain to a passing holy man, who turned the sack into stone as punishment. A curse is said to fall on anyone who tries to move the stone - this was experienced by Gentleman John Walls, who used four shire horses to move the rock to his front door at Corn Sack Farm after taking a liking to it. Misfortune soon befell him, and he moved it back soon after (only requiring one horse for the return trip).

properties.name: Open Window
properties.description: One story says a window in the upper part of the pub is cursed; if it is ever closed then ill fortune follows. Like so many other pubs, the site was also once home to a poltergeist, and is also said to be haunted by the ghosts of two Second World War airmen.

properties.name: Unlucky Art
properties.description: Since the installation of artist Gordon Young's sculpted granite 'Cursing Stone' inscribed with a 16th century curse in one of Carlisle's museums in 2001, misfortune has plagued the city - local farm stock were wiped out by foot-and-mouth disease, a devastating flood occurred, several factories have closed, a boy killed in a local bakery and Carlisle United football team dropped a league.

properties.name: Don't Talk
properties.description: Local legend says a witch was executed on this tree, cursing it while dying. Something untoward is supposed to happen to the loved ones of those who talk beneath the branches, while highly superstitious folk hold their breath while scampering past.

properties.name: Toppling Tower
properties.description: St. Andrew's church tower was cursed by a local witch. First it burned down, and when rebuild was struck by lightning and burned down again. To prevent this from happening a third time, a large square tower was built, only to be hit by a bomb during the Second World War.

properties.name: Mermaid Curse
properties.description: The mistress of the castle ordered the destruction of a black rock which stood just a short distance away, to prevent a mermaid sitting upon it and singing. As revenge, the mermaid cursed the family, ensuring they all died without leaving an heir for the estate.

properties.name: Bloody Handprint
properties.description: Knyvett, lord of the manor, was murdered by his son even though the finger of blame was pointed at the butler. Just before being hanged, the manservant cursed the descendants of Knyvett's family, vowing that they would not prosper while living in the hall. Knyvett's bloody handprint, left on a wall where he fell, could not be washed away so the stone was chipped away. More recently, the sound of footsteps and doors closing have been at night, and a couple dressed in Victorian clothing spotted.

properties.name: Lost Monk
properties.description: Seen by witnesses as he floats around the grounds of the abbey, this monk is thought to have been murdered in 1345. Phantom footsteps have also been reported on the stable yard cobbles and are thought to belong to a young gypsy girl who cursed the family after they mistreated her. Finally, a phantom cyclist, thought to be a Victorian woman, has been observed on the road just by the former abbey.
properties.location_name: Coombe (West Midlands) - Coombe Abbey (currently a hotel)

properties.name: Chair
properties.description: The artist John Heritage-peters lived at this site and threatened to haunt anyone who removed a chair from the gallery. The artist died in 1965 and subsequent house owners honoured his wish. When offered for sale in 1988, the house featured in the local press, complete with chair and associated folklore.
properties.location_name: Coventry (West Midlands) - Private residence, Kenilworth Road

properties.name: Duellist
properties.description: This phantom figure is reportedly a duellist who once fought (and lost) here. Another ghost, that of a woman, drifts around near the lake, and is thought by some to herald tragedy in the family. G Bernard Wood wrote that the priory was cursed by its last Prior, and that if anyone completed the decorating in an unfinished room upstairs, they would suffer a death in the family.

properties.name: Witch
properties.description: With her dying breath, a witch hanged from this tree cursed the area around it, ensuring nothing would ever grow.
properties.location_name: Craigie (Ayrshire) - Dead tree (exact area not known)

properties.name: Curse of Crowland
properties.description: The monks here embraced pagan ideals, and for their sins, the Devil appeared and told them that a new abbey would soon be built on the site, but all present would die before it became so. Soon after, a Viking raid levelled the building, and all the monks died.

properties.name: White Owl
properties.description: An unnaturally large white owl took to hunting over an ancient battlefield, leading locals to believe the creature was magical and should not be interfered with. A visitor to Raglan castle who laughed at the story and killed the owl, himself died a few hours later, choking to death at a dinner table.

properties.name: Witch's Leg
properties.description: Perhaps a recently created piece of folklore, one story says that a witch with a wooden leg was buried alive in the church; from the leg grew a tree and the church fell to ruins soon after.
properties.location_name: East Somerton (Norfolk) - Church of St Mary (ruins) and tree known as the Witch's Finger

properties.name: Cursed Building
properties.description: After several unconnected and violent deaths of people who worked at 71 Queen Victoria Street, the company which was based at the address closed.

properties.name: Curse of the Mummy
properties.description: The figure of an ancient priest was seen in the building after the family returned from a trip to Egypt - it was said that the ghost had accompanied a bone that had been illegally removed from a tomb by a family member. When the piece of skeleton was destroyed, the haunting stopped.

properties.name: Poor Curse
properties.description: Legend has it that James Anderson demolished several cottages to clear space for the construction of the hall. Left homeless and penniless, the villagers cursed the building, saying that the structure would not stand for long. Indeed, all trace of the hall has long been removed.
properties.location_name: Ettrick (Borders) - Ettrick Hall (no longer standing, was located top of Ettrick Water)

properties.name: Drifting Nun
properties.description: Appearing at dusk, the figure of a nun has been reported floating down this lane. Her skull is housed at Pennis Farm and it is said if the object is ever removed a great curse will befall the valley.

properties.name: Groom's Curse
properties.description: A groom was hanged from the holly tree after stealing a small amount of corn. Before dying the groom cursed the Mercer family, saying that they would never have a son inherit their family home. The curse is said to have lasted for several generations.
properties.location_name: Fossaway (Perth and Kinross) - Former Mercer family home (unknown whether building still stands) and holly tree near village green

properties.name: Friar's Curse
properties.description: Falling insane after penance took its toll, a friar cursed the church and anyone who stood in its shadow. Another tale reports it is the rock summit of Raggedstone Hill that casts the cursed shadow.
properties.location_name: Great Malvern (Hereford & Worcester) - Ragged Stone Hill

properties.name: Craven's Curse
properties.description: A curse placed on the Craven family by a pregnant servant girl kicked out of the household states that no earl will survive beyond their fifty-eighth year. The family sold the house to try to rid them of the ill fortune during the late twentieth century.

properties.name: Little John's Femur
properties.description: James Shuttleworth reportedly dug up the nearby grave containing the remains of Little John, removing a femur measuring seventy-two centimetres in length. However, he was struck by misfortune soon after, which vanished after he reburied the bone.

properties.name: Drowning Curse
properties.description: It is said that the body of water will take a life every seven years. There are also reports that a phantom ship is occasionally seen here.

properties.name: Luck of Hintlesham
properties.description: A cursed mannequin was once stored here - if removed, it was believed disaster would soon follow, and the owner would lose possession of the hall.

properties.name: Dafydd
properties.description: The skull of Dafydd Prince of Wales is housed in a box at the hall. A curse is said to befall the owners of the building if it is ever removed from the property.

properties.name: Glint of King Edmund's Spurs
properties.description: Discovered hiding under the bridge after the glint of his spurs attracted the attention of a pair of newlyweds, Edmund was taken and beheaded. The glint is still seen, and it is considered unlucky for newly wedded couples to cross the bridge.

properties.name: The Ex
properties.description: A piece of folklore says that Yolande of Dreux had a former boyfriend murdered so she could marry King Alexander III of Scotland. After the wedding, the boyfriend's phantom appeared and issued a curse that Yolande would be killed by misery (it is worth noting she did not die for another 45 years). A remarkably similar story is told of Jedburgh Castle.

properties.name: Howgill's Revenge
properties.description: The hall's owner, Magistrate Duckett, sentenced the Quaker Howgill to spend time in Appleby prison. The Quaker cursed Duckett, saying his name would vanish from history and his house would be home to owls and jackdaws. Sure enough, Duckett's children died without bearing children of their own, and his home was reduced to rubble, used by only birds.
properties.location_name: Kendal (Cumbria) - Grayrigg Hall (no longer standing, though some of the original buildings used by a local farm)

properties.name: De Clinton Curse
properties.description: Geoffrey De Clinton promised that if anyone ever tried to take away the land around the church they would be cursed by God - a series of disasters befell many people and companies which tried to redevelop the land during the late twentieth century.
properties.location_name: Kenilworth (Warwickshire) - Area around the former site of the abbey and the Church of St Mary (no longer standing)

properties.name: Dead Horse
properties.description: Several stones from the hill fort were taken to Penzance by horse, to repair the pier. Despite being healthy, the horses all died soon after.
properties.location_name: Kerris (Cornwall) - Kerris Roundago (Iron Age hill fort, no longer present)

properties.name: Reappearing Skulls
properties.description: Belonging to a married farming couple, the skulls came back to haunt the person who framed them for a crime that resulted in their execution. No matter how they were destroyed, the grinning skulls always returned to the hall, until the death of the man they cursed.

properties.name: Luck of Eden
properties.description: Stolen from fairies by a former butler, the fifteen centimetre tall cup was locked away deep within the house, before being moved to the V&A - a curse placed by the little folk in retaliation for the theft states that if the beaker breaks, so does the Eden Hall's luck. The hall was also reputedly haunted by an elderly lady, who may have been a maid, who had a habit of appearing as a reflection in a mirror.
properties.date: Still present (but in the Victoria and Albert museum)

properties.name: Falling Branches
properties.description: When a branch falls from a certain old elm tree near the manor house, a member of the Popham family (former owners of the house) is said to die. If the entire tree falls, a huge tragic event is believed to befall Littlecote.
properties.date: Marks a death in the Popham family
properties.location_name: Littlecote (Wiltshire) - Elm tree near the Manor House - current state unknown

properties.name: Returned Rock
properties.description: A small piece of rock was returned by an Australian tourist to the abbey, with the man claiming he had suffered a run of bad luck since removing it.

properties.name: Cursing Well
properties.description: Destroyed by the local clergy, the well which once stood here was used to curse one's enemies. The hex could be reversed if the pebble dropped into waters by the giver of the curse could be found and removed from the well.
properties.location_name: Llanelian-yn-Rhos (Clwyd) - St Ellian's Well (no longer present)

properties.name: Cursed Stairwell
properties.description: The staircase was made from a tree considered holy, and a series of disasters befell all those who were involved in cutting it down and moving to the building. The castle is also home to phantom music.
properties.location_name: Loch Nell (Argyll and Bute) - Loch Nell Castle

properties.name: Mr Anderton
properties.description: An unscrupulous landowner, Anderton refused to take rent money owing to him so he could reclaim some land he wanted, at the cost of a family living there - he was cursed to haunt that area for his sins.

properties.name: Unlucky Tree
properties.description: A tree is this park has its branches propped up - a legend says that if the branches ever touch the ground, ill fortune will befall the Lowther family.

properties.name: Brocade Shoes
properties.description: The remains of a woman found within the walls of this house were speculated to belong to the Spanish mistress of David Papillon. The woman died around 1715, but declared if anyone touched her brocade shoes, misfortune would descend. In 1903, Sir Frank Bellville removed the shoes; shortly after he fell and broke his skull. The shoes were returned to the hall, although after the building's destruction, were moved to Harborough Museum.

properties.name: Webb's Portrait
properties.description: A portrait of General Webb was at one time thought to be cursed, with ill luck befalling anyone who removed it.

properties.name: Workman
properties.description: The Alma was thought to be cursed by locals who were forced to move from their homes so they could be demolished for the construction of the cinema. A construction worker fell to his death during the building work, and many felt that it was his ghostly presence that haunted The Alma.
properties.location_name: Luton (Bedfordshire) - The Alma cinema (no longer standing)

properties.name: Francis Downes
properties.description: This building is reported to have had a skull bricked up within its walls, to prevent it from accidentally being removed and condemning the house's occupiers to misfortune or death. The macabre relic is thought to belong to Edward Barlow, a Catholic executed by Protestants in 1640.

properties.name: John Fothergill
properties.description: The portrait of John Fothergill that hangs above the bar is no longer touched - if removed from its spot a disaster befalls the handler or hotel.

properties.name: Lady Montague
properties.description: Lady Montague's wraith walks the gardens surrounding what is left of this once great house. The fortune of the building fell after it was cursed by a prioress after the owner ransacked the local priory. In 2021 two witnesses spotted a very tall, black clad figure gliding away from the ruins across the causeway. The figure appeared to be larger than a normal human and moved quicker than is possible.
properties.location_name: Midhurst (Sussex) - Ruins of Cowdray House & Park

properties.name: Cursed Castle
properties.description: During its construction, the owner of this building was cursed by a neighbour for being uncaring, and the castle was never finished. It is also said to be haunted by an aging man and a younger gentleman, who were caught on camera in 2003. (Image copyright 2006 Lawson Clout)

properties.name: Cursed Chest
properties.description: The chest is supposed to contain an evil presence that moves furniture around. Two old women who owned the chest prior to its delivery at the Stanbury are said to have been struck deaf when they first opened it and released the spirit hidden inside.

properties.name: White Lady
properties.description: Falling in love and becoming pregnant by the lord of the castle, a local girl committed suicide after being shunned by him after their love affair. Though her mother then cursed the evil lord (resulting in his death, and that of the estate's future heir), the young lass still returns to the site.

properties.name: Voices
properties.description: Probably the oldest public house in England, the building is home to spectral voices and other phantom noises. A model galleon, thick with dust, hangs from the ceiling and is said to be cursed - anyone who touches it dies within twelve months.
properties.location_name: Nottingham (Nottinghamshire) - Ye Olde Trip to Jerusalem public house

properties.name: Cursed Lock
properties.description: A young gypsy lad buried in the churchyard was hanged for stealing a horse - a crime his relatives claimed he did not commit. To stop their protests, the church door was locked to prevent them from visiting, but their leader cursed anyone who ever locked the door again. After several deaths occurring to people who performed this action, the key was thrown into the nearby river.

properties.name: Mother Ivey's Curse
properties.description: Mother Ivey (or Ivy), a local wise woman, cursed this land after pilchards that could have been used to feed starving villagers were used to fertilise the field. She reportedly said if the soil were broken, death would soon follow. Whoever digs up any of the field is reputed to suffer soon afterwards. The story was carried by the popular UK press once it emerged that Conservative leader David Cameron had booked a holiday on the 'cursed' land.
properties.location_name: Padstow (Cornwall) - Field near the Hellyer's farmhouse, Harlyn Bay

properties.name: Young Horseman
properties.description: Often heard and sometimes even seen, the horseman is thought to have been a young lad who stole a horse from his master to speed up the journey to meet his secret lover. In his haste, he galloped past the scene of an accident involving a cart - he could have saved the life of the driver who was trapped underneath, but lust took priority and the rider left the site without offering assistance. The victim of the accident later died but managed to curse the lad who rode on by; hence, the boy's ghost now roams.

properties.name: Cursed Wall
properties.description: Back in the sixteenth century, as the last monks were removed from their land, one brother cursed a nearby farmhouse. The brother said that if the building ever fell, the local community would fall with it. Work has been carried out to ensure the remaining wall of the ruined farmhouse has remained upright. A ghostly monk in white is also said to lurk in the area.
properties.location_name: Port Talbot (West Glamorgan) - Wall on the site of Tata Steel

properties.name: Underground Mutton
properties.description: The death of a nineteenth century quarry crane operator, blamed on a collapsing rabbit warren, gave birth to the belief that rabbits were unlucky. Even mentioning the word rabbit could bring frightful consequences; the creatures were referred to as 'underground mutton', 'furry things' or just 'bunnies'. Some employers would send their labourers back home if a rabbit had been seen en route to work.

properties.name: Cattle Killer
properties.description: Two stones once stood here and were moved to a ditch by a farmer who wanted to clear the land. Soon after the removal, the farmer's cattle died, the spring water ran red, and the farmhouse became home to a violent poltergeist which smashed mirrors and windows. Normality returned when the stones were replaced.
properties.location_name: Preston Brook (Cheshire) - Headless cross (a sandstone block) in a field near Newton crossroads (may have been moved for the M56 construction)

properties.name: Maud Toogood
properties.description: A hollow elm tree which was killed off by Dutch elm disease was replaced with an oak. One of the reasons for the replacement was that the shade of a local witch had taken up residence in the tree, and locals were worried that she may curse the area if the elm was fell.
properties.location_name: Ramsbury (Wiltshire) - Elm tree in village (now replaced with an oak)

properties.name: White Lady
properties.description: This pallid female wraith cursed the family with everlasting sorrow and misfortune. She is said to be quite tall and slender, with blue eyes and blonde hair. The figure was last seen in 1966, where the entity was said to be on her knees, crying, bathed in bluish-white light. A banshee has also been heard wailing at the castle; one man who heard the entity as he left the site died before his journey ended.
properties.location_name: Randalstown (County Antrim) - Shane's Castle (aka Shanes Castle)

properties.name: Lychgate
properties.description: Newly married couples avoided passing through the Lychgate as it was believed the marriage would fail, either by discontent or death.

properties.name: Seath Mor's Stones
properties.description: The stones resting on Seath Mor Sgorfhiaclach's grave are said to be cursed, with misfortune befalling anyone who touched them. An iron cage now covers the grave and stones; while some say it is to protect against people accidently invoking the wrath of the curse, the cage's placement may be due to the stones being stolen (and later recovered) in the 1980s.
properties.location_name: Rothiemurcus (Highland) - Churchyard (aka Old Doune Church Burial Ground)

properties.name: Tom
properties.description: Tom Busby murdered his father with a hammer - an event which resulted in the public house in which the crime was committed being named after him. Hanged for the crime, his shade now comes back complete with noose, and any who see it are doomed. The shade was once thought to be centred on Tom's favourite chair, though when the chair was removed to Thirsk Museum, Tom stayed pub-bound. The chair itself was thought to be cursed, anyone sitting on it would die soon after.
properties.location_name: Sand Hutton (North Yorkshire) - Busby Stoop Inn, road outside

properties.name: Lewes Dowrich
properties.description: Lewes died drunk, after falling from his horse on the nearby bridge. He was cursed to return home from the bridge, but only to move ten centimetres every four weeks. The ghost was seen in 1973, only a few metres short of its goal.

properties.name: Billy Callender
properties.description: Callender, a goalkeeper, hanged himself in the Old Stand in 1932, after his fiance died of a disease. His form is said to linger on. The ground was also subject to a curse in the 1970s from a psychic originally called in to lay the ghost of Callender.
properties.location_name: SE19 (Greater London) - Crystal Palace Football Ground (Selhurst Park)

properties.name: Molly
properties.description: After murdering her daughter by pouring boiling water over her, Molly the maid was herself hanged for the crime, and her skeleton placed inside this house. Her ghost appears in the 'Pink Room', where she committed her heinous crime. The skeleton itself is reputedly cursed and removing the bones would bring misfortune to the occupiers.

properties.name: Peasant Girl
properties.description: A knight took his own life after having his love rejected by a peasant girl. Lying in his own blood, the knight cursed her, and she died soon after. The peasant's ghost remains in the forest. Another entity reported to haunt the area is the wild hunt, complete with headless horseman (thought by some to be the aforementioned knight).
properties.location_name: Silverstone (Northamptonshire) - Whittlebury Forest (and site of the former woodland)

properties.name: Egyptian Curse
properties.description: Poltergeist activity broke out in the house shortly after the arrival of Egyptian and Syrian artefacts brought back from the Middle East by Owen Phibbs. Servants were driven away, while the house sometimes shook to the very foundations. Eventually the family moved away and left the house to rot.

properties.name: Pagan Help Required
properties.description: Cerridwen Dragonoak Connelly was called in to cleanse this stadium after it was believed the location was cursed or jinxed. After the blessing, the football team went on to their first win at their new home.

properties.name: Chair of Despair
properties.description: A chair in this public house has reported as being cursed after eight people who sat there died.

properties.name: No Mine
properties.description: When St Keverne lived here, he was shown little respect by the locals. The saint cursed the area with 'No metal will run within the sound of St. Keverne's bells'. Hence, no mines were ever discovered within the area.

properties.name: Four Poster Bed
properties.description: A four poster bed in the castle is reported to be cursed - any child who sleeps in it never wakes up.

properties.name: Do Not Move
properties.description: This stone is said to be cursed; when it was last moved to straighten the nearby road, one of the workmen is said to have dropped dead.

properties.name: Shakespeare's Grave
properties.description: The tomb of Shakespeare has the following curse written upon it: Good frend for Jesus sake forebeare, To digg the dust encloased heare; Bleste be the man that spares thes stones, And curst be he that moves my bones.

properties.name: Kentish Longtails
properties.description: One legend says that Thomas Becket was riding through the land when a member of the de Broc family cut off his horse's tail. This must have mightily upset the archbishop, as Becket cursed the population of Strood to be born with tails.

properties.name: Mummified Feline
properties.description: If removed, this cat is said to bring ill fortune to the building and the occupiers. When the cat was first discovered (before the mill became a hotel), it was sold to a nearby shop. The shop burned down, so the cat came back has stayed ever since.

properties.name: Cursed Widow
properties.description: Cursed by her husband prior to his execution (the woman's testimony placed him there, enabling her to run off with another man), Lord Ferrer's widow died in a fire so intense only part of her thumb was recovered. Her spirit is said to be looking for the rest of the body.

properties.name: Seat of Defeat
properties.description: The museum houses a cursed wooden chair which brings death to any who sit upon it, and is even said to bring misfortune if touched.

properties.name: Hob of the Hurst
properties.description: This entity helped the family with their chores, until given a gift - this freed the spirit, and it became hostile and antagonistic. As if this was not bad enough, the Barcroft family was once cursed by one of their sons who was imprisoned in the basement, who declared that the family name should die out.
properties.location_name: Todmorden (West Yorkshire) - Barcroft Hall, Walk Mill

properties.name: Homely Skull
properties.description: The home of another screaming skull, the building would echo with the sounds of cries and moans if the morbid item were removed.

properties.name: Cursed Motorway
properties.description: A week after a motorway construction team disturbed the graves of eighteen men, some two metres tall, two of the men suffered fatal heart attacks and a night watchman dropped dead at his post. People protesting the construction of the motorway pronounced the incidents 'the curse of the giants'.
properties.location_name: Twyford (Hampshire) - Twyford Down (now area of the M3)

properties.name: Field of the Dead
properties.description: Anyone who plants a spade in the earth at this location in Burra Fiord shall be struck by tragedy (according to folklore). One woman who dug there lost her best cow. When she dug there a second time, her husband died.

properties.name: Soothsayer Curse
properties.description: Before being boiled alive in a cauldron of tar, a local soothsayer predicted the downfall of the family that owned the castle. His every word came true...
properties.location_name: Urray (Highland) - Brahan Castle, former site of

properties.name: Fire
properties.description: Despite dire warnings from locals, Mr Hocart of Belval had workers cut this former monolith into stone for his new house. The day the building work had finished, a fire broke out, killing two servants. Two ships which carried spare stone to the UK sank within the year, and even though Hocart moved into a second house to Alderney, the fire curse followed him, destroying that dwelling also. Hocart finally returned to Guernsey, but just prior to landing, some rigging fell and landed on his head, killing him instantly.
properties.location_name: Vale (Guernsey) - L'Ancresse Bay - large stone (no longer standing)

properties.name: Roman Coins
properties.description: One story says misfortunes befell the family of a man who found a cache of Roman coins near the river and kept them for himself.
properties.location_name: Watton-at-Stone (Hertfordshire) - Close to the River Beane

properties.name: Marcus Beck
properties.description: Before being stolen in January 2001, the portrait of Beck was considered cursed. People who fell asleep under the painting quickly became ill, and in some cases died. It was also said that if the shutters were not closed on the painting at night, a patient would unexpectedly die.

properties.name: Crop Curse
properties.description: Robert Rosier was burnt to death in this field in 1556 by a former friend; since that day, no one has been able to raise a successful harvest in that pasture.
properties.location_name: Wetherden (Suffolk) - Field between Wetherden and Woolpit

properties.name: Bad Luck Stone
properties.description: After the decommissioning lightship 'Guillemot', it became a maritime museum that housed a stone that caused ill-luck to anyone who touched it (taken from a fairy fort by a lorry driver who refused to return it. One woman who held the stone injured her leg, while another man who touched it slipped off the ship into the water.
properties.location_name: Wexford (County Wexford) - Lightship Guillemot Museum (no longer open, cut up for scrap)

properties.name: Cursed Hoard
properties.description: Somewhere in the village, a fabulous treasure is hidden; searching for it will do little good, however, as anyone who finds it will die.

properties.name: Sir Laurence Tanfield
properties.description: A former landowner in the area, Sir Laurence returned after dying and can be seen driving a coach pulled by four black horses. It is said that bad luck falls on any witnesses.

properties.name: Blue Lady
properties.description: A painting of a blue woman (who died after being banished from the property) that hanged in the manor house was thought to have been cursed, with the destruction of the property a promised result of any movement of the work.

properties.name: William the Giant
properties.description: These two boulders were thrown into position by William, the local giant and wizard. A farmer who tried to move one using six horses failed to do so, and all the horses died shortly after their attempt - it became known that to mess with the stones was unlucky.
properties.location_name: Wroot (Lincolnshire) - Thumb Stone and Little Finger Stone (no longer present?)

properties.name: Cheated Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil is said to have built the bridge for an old lady - he expected her soul as a reward but ended up with that of a dog instead. Crossing the bridge at night is said to risk the Devil pushing you off as revenge for the old woman's treachery. Another legend says that a ghost that haunted a nearby house was banished to a cavern under the bridge where it now works.

properties.name: Laid by the Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: It was said Old Nick created this old road in a single night. He was said to appear to anyone using the road at midnight, taking the form of a man on a white horse (although he had the horns and feet of a cow). If the person encountering him had a good soul, they could pass unheeded. If they had led an evil life, the Devil would beat them to a bloodied pulp.
properties.location_name: Acton Burnell (Shropshire) - Devil's Causeway, between village and Cardington

properties.name: Demonic Frogs
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil and three imps are said to live here, manifesting in the form of frogs. While the imps are happy to be seen, Old Nick takes the form of a larger frog and spends most of the time hiding away.

properties.name: The Devil Helps Thrice
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This church is one of three in the area (Bloxham and King's Sutton being the other two) that were paid for by three brothers. One of their workers who helped construct this tower never took any pay, never slept and never ate - he vanished as soon as the tower was built. The brothers were convinced that they were helped by Old Nick.

properties.name: Mowing Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A witch by the name of Maggie Ramsey was said to have summoned the devil to assist her in a mowing contest, although he remained invisible to anyone without second sight.
properties.location_name: Airdrie (Lanarkshire) - Unidentified farmer's field, North Burn

properties.name: Summoned Entity
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A local myth states that walking around the church thirteen times anticlockwise will summon the Devil.

properties.name: Devil's Bang
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil reportedly helped the owner of the castle perform alchemy on the site, before a chemical mishap blew them both to hell.

properties.name: Old Nick
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Legend has it that if one runs around the cave known locally as 'Devil's Grave' widdershins three times, the Devil is raised! Another version says that running around it three times restores your virginity.
properties.location_name: Alderley Edge (Cheshire) - Devil's Grave, Stormy Point

properties.name: John Euerafriad
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: John Euerafriad sold his soul to the Devil (for what we do not know), whether he was buried within the church or not. As normal, with this type of deal, he was buried under the church wall (neither in nor out of the church) which denied the Devil his due.

properties.name: Devil's Trackway
properties.description: This causeway was said to have been constructed by the devil. There are also reports of buried treasure in the area.

properties.name: Blind George
properties.description: Said to still be playing his fiddle in tunnels under the village, Blind George ventured into the Devil's Hole cave with his trusted dog - the hound ran out, and was found with its hair burnt away, and George was never seen again.
properties.location_name: Anstey (Hertfordshire) - Fields around the village, and Devil's Hole cave

properties.name: Rock Stack
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Several rocks along this stretch are named after the Devil, who was probably blamed for making the coast so dangerous.

properties.name: Furrow
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Between five and six miles long, this ancient ditch is said to have been created by the Devil and two inhuman helpers in a single night.

properties.name: Devil's Apron String
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: While on a mission to fill the loch with stones, the Devil, carrying the boulders in his apron, had a mishap; the apron string broke, the rocks scattering themselves over the landscape. The Devil gave up and returned home.

properties.name: Dropped Mortar
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: During the construction of the churches at Bloxham, Adderbury and King's Sutton, the Devil was thought to have lent a hand. During the work he slipped and dropped some of the mortar he was carrying, thus creating Crouch Hill.

properties.name: Blown Cross
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: It was believed that the villagers tried to erect a massive cross on the hill, though the Devil visited every night and blew it down. Exasperated, the villagers carved the cross into stone, preventing further destruction. It is now thought the cross was the base of a Roman hillfort.

properties.name: Tatter Foal
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil was said to occasional visit this area in the form of a rough looking horse.

properties.name: Leaping Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: For many years, the church tower only had three pinnacles - the loss of the fourth was blamed on the Devil who tried to jump over the tower while giving chase to a local wizard named Minterne.

properties.name: Devil's Hand
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A stone doorway is said to display the handprint of Satan. Another version of the story says a skeletal ghost left the mark after trying to grab a tailor who stayed the night in the church as a wager.

properties.name: Digging Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: As Old Nick dug out the nearby dyke, Hangour Hill was created with the discarded earth.

properties.name: Golden Coffin
properties.description: A golden coffin is reputed to be buried in the area. Another legend says that if you walk around the hill seven times while swearing, the Devil will appear to grant you a single wish.
properties.location_name: Berwick St John (Wiltshire) - Winklebury Camp, also known as Winkelbury Camp

properties.name: Pact
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A witch once claimed (likely under duress) that she met the Devil in human form along this road, where she made a deal with him. As a side note, the Devil is thought to have tried to hamper the construction of the bridge here.

properties.name: Hoof Marks
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A standing stone at this site was said to feature marks where the Devil had jumped and landed upon it (by mistake, as he was aiming for a building some half mile away).

properties.name: Caught in the Tread
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Lea Stone was created when the Devil picked the rock from his boot and tossed it aside.
properties.location_name: Bishops Castle (Shropshire) - Lea Stone, located near the castle ruins

properties.name: Meeting Point
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: It is a curious local legend that states that once a year all the ghosts in the UK meet at the stones, one assumes for their AGM. Another story says that if the stones ever sink into the earth, England shall be ruined; the Devil occasionally sits on the stones to speed up the process.

properties.name: The Devil Helps Thrice
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This church is one of three in the area (King's Sutton and Adderbury being the other two) that were paid for by three brothers. One of their workers who helped construct this tower never took any pay, never slept and never ate - he vanished as soon as the tower was built. The brothers were convinced that they were helped by Old Nick.

properties.name: Round in Circles
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The building was constructed in a circular shape after Old Nick appeared night after night and pulled the partly constructed four walls down. The shape confused the Devil, and he left the area.

properties.name: Devil's Bolts
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: These large standing stones are said to have been thrown by Old Nick at Aldborough, an old Christian settlement that once stood here. After failing to hit his target, the Devil hanged himself from the largest stone!
properties.location_name: Boroughbridge (North Yorkshire) - Standing Stones, in a field near Aldborough

properties.name: The Devil and the Cobbler
properties.description: Built by a cobbler who made a deal with Old Nick and went back on his word, the house became haunted by the man and the devil. A miller who lived nearby was losing money due to his proximity to the location and asked Parson Corker to banish both entities. The parson brought some friends, set up his magic circle and after a mighty battle (involving a storm which levelled most trees in the area) banished the Devil. The ghost proved more difficult to banish, and in the end the house was demolished.
properties.location_name: Bosava (?) (Cornwall) - House no longer standing, location not known

properties.name: Devil's Breath
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The winds which whip and whistle around the Stump are said to be caused by the Devil. He was once cornered by St Botolph; the saint preached so intensely, the only thing the Devil could do was huff and puff, and the gasps have not yet subsided.
properties.location_name: Boston (Lincolnshire) - Boston Stump (tower of St Botolph's parish church)

properties.name: Evil Awaits
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: It was said that the churchyard here was unused for a while due to the belief that the first body interred would belong to the Devil. The body of a servant under the employ of a visitor to the area was buried here, after which others believed the site was ready to be used for their loved ones.

properties.name: Dropped Rocks
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: As the name of the place suggests, this ancient monument was created when the Devil dropped an apron full of stones while on route to one of his many building projects.

properties.name: Dropped Stones
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The three stones here were taken from a church by the Devil to extend hell. They proved too heavy, and he dropped them where they now stand. Another legend says that if anyone tried to move the Webb Stone, farmland would become less productive, while a third story says that any girl who baked a cake on All Saint's Eve and left it on the stone would dream of their future husband.
properties.location_name: Bradley (Staffordshire) - Webb Stone, also known as the Wanderer Stone

properties.name: Near Miss
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil tried to throw a ten metre tall rock at Lenton Priory from his cave at Castleton. He missed, and the stone landed on Stapleford Hill where it remains.

properties.name: Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Local legend says that on the day Cromwell died, the park was laid to waste as the Devil passed though it to collect the soul due to him. Now, once a year, Old Nick rides out and returns to the park to ravage it once more.

properties.name: Nick's Hole
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A local legend said that this deep (naturally occurring) pit was dug out by the devil.

properties.name: Footprint
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The footprint in a slab of stone is now on display within the church. It is said to belong to a Catholic who, after arguing with a Protestant, declared 'if my doctrine be untrue, may my foot sink in stone' - upon which it did. Another version of the story says it was the Devil who left his footprint in the stone.

properties.name: Undone Work
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The old church here had to be constructed away from the planned site after the workmen constantly found all their previous day's work undone. The Devil was blamed and after moving the building site, no more delays were experienced.

properties.name: Old Nick in Waiting
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The builder of this church is said to have sold his soul to finish the construction. The Devil is said to still wait for the soul due to him, while the old man's shade lurks protected within the church grounds. There is one report that says the builder now takes the form of a clothed skeletal figure and appears once per year.

properties.name: Visiting Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A large, scorched patch on some flagstones in the building is explained away by a story which involves a group of students silly enough to try to summon the Devil - as he climbed out of the ground, the pupils panicked and hit Old Nick with books, stones and anything they could grab, until he disappeared back to Hell.

properties.name: Moving Bricks
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The church was going to be built on another location, but night after night the Devil appeared and moved the building materials, until the construction finally relocated to where the church now stands. Another version of the story allocates the Devil a pig to perform the task.

properties.name: Fiddler
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Old Nick visits the area to play his fiddle on moonlit nights.

properties.name: Devil's Footprints
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Prints left by the hoofs of the Devil can still be seen in the rock, after Old Nick danced with joy after discovering a group of men playing cards on the Sabbath.
properties.location_name: Cadair Idris (Gwynedd) - Rock of the Evil One (crag)

properties.name: Evocation via Tree
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Running around the tree known as Big Bellied Oak seven times (or twelve) at midnight was said to bring forth the Devil.

properties.name: Travellers' Rest
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil sometimes beckons road weary travellers into his kitchen - it is not known what happens to them once inside.

properties.name: Scot's Building
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The site of an Iron Age hill fort, the rocky remains were once said to have been constructed by local wizard Michael Scot. Some legends replace the builder with the Devil.
properties.location_name: Carrock Fell (Cumbria) - Stones on top of the fell

properties.name: Devil's Chimney
properties.description: This limestone outcrop is said to lead straight to hell.

properties.name: Concealed Treasure
properties.description: This stone, made of sand and gravel, is said to be unmovable, which makes acquiring the treasure buried beneath pretty damn hard to reach.
properties.location_name: Chertsey (Surrey) - Large stone on St Anne's Hill, was known as the Devil's Stone

properties.name: Bent by the Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil, resting against the church, was so taken by the beauty of a bride that he spun around to have a better view, warping the spire in the process. Another story says the first couple to be married in the church were so innocent that they caused the spire to warp. The damage will not be undone until another innocent couple are wed on the site. More recently, people have reported the sensation of being followed as they walk around the church.

properties.name: Failed Suicide
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil is said to have once tried to take his own life on Chrome Hill but failed.

properties.name: Fleeing Nick
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Local witch Mother Ludlam had her cauldron stolen by the Devil. As the Devil ran, every step he took created a great mound of earth.
properties.location_name: Churt (Surrey) - Devil's Jumps (three hills in the area)

properties.name: Cloven Feet
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Cock fighting and other forms of gambling would take place in this secluded area. One story said that during one game of cards, a mysterious man in black sat and joined. One player was mortified when, bending over to pick up a dropped card, he noticed the man in black had cloven feet.

properties.name: Home of the Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil once decided that his new home would be here, but a cunning schoolteacher convincing Old Nick that he should try to make a rope of sand which could be used in the river. As the rope touched the water and dissolved, the Devil left in frustration.
properties.location_name: Cockerham (Lancashire) - River Cocker and surrounding area

properties.name: Rotating Stone
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Old Nick appears at midnight every night to turn the stone around.
properties.location_name: Colwall (Hereford & Worcester) - Stone in the centre of the village

properties.name: Thrown
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Accidently created by the Devil as he tried to hurl boulders at Corfe Castle, these stones fell in a rough circle shape.
properties.location_name: Corfe Castle (Dorset) - Rempstone Stone Circle (private property)

properties.name: The Devil's Chair
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Named after a belief that the Devil would once sit here, the stones are also called Granny's Armchair (which is not quite as frightening).

properties.name: Failed Roadblock
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil threw these stones into position while trying to block the road.

properties.name: White Stag
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Work on the church was hampered by the Devil stealing the construction materials each night. A wise man told the builders the only way to prevent this from happening was to find a new spot for the church, and that the area would be indicated by a white stag. Sure enough, after searching the local area, a white stag was found; the church was successfully built on the spot of the sighting with no further interruptions.

properties.name: Moving Away
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The church was due to be built in the middle of the village, but the Devil thought otherwise and moved the building materials to the outskirts.

properties.name: The Devil's Shovel Full
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This barrow was created by Old Nick after he lost his shoe and was forced to drop a shovel full of earth with which he intended to dam the Derwent in Derby.

properties.name: Thrown Missile
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil, who may have been secretly involved in constructing the church, was angered by a local man. The Devil fled to the Isle of May and threw a stone at the church. The projectile fell short and remains in place to this day.
properties.location_name: Crail (Fife) - Blue Stone (outside the parish church)

properties.name: Footballing Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Old Nick once popped up and started playing football on the Sabbath with a group of local lads - with his first kick of the ball it turned into a burning sphere, causing all the other players to flee!

properties.name: Lost Bell
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Having stolen the church bell, the Devil dropped it on the common, where it sunk into the earth. The bell is said to still be buried there today.

properties.name: Devil in Disguise
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: During a violent storm, a ghostly monk appeared during Mass in the church. At the same time, the roof shook with such force that it was feared it would collapse. Some say the monk was the devil in disguise, and he returned a short time later, left scorch marks on the north door, and stole the bell.

properties.name: Lost Souls
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Deep pools of water located along the river, the Hell's Kettles are said to contain the souls of sinners. Some report that these people (and animals in some cases) can be seen floating in the pools when the waters are clear.
properties.location_name: Darlington (Durham) - Hell's Kettles, along the River Tees

properties.name: The Devil does a Deal
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
