properties.description: It was at this spot that Jan Reynolds was approached by a cloaked stranger who bought his soul from him in return for seven years of good luck. Jan agreed, and seven years later the Devil came and took his due, while Jan sat in Widecombe church.

properties.name: Petrified Parson
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A priest and his clerk found themselves lost in the area and sought shelter in an old house where they found several men eating and drinking to excess. The two visitors joined in, only to soon realise that their host was Old Nick. They tried to escape but failed. One legend says that they were found dead on the rock that now bears their names, while other stories say that the pair were turned into the stone.

properties.name: Broken Apron
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil decided to sabotage the construction of Durham Cathedral; he visited Castle Eden and found an inferior piece of limestone that could added to the cathedral foundations. Placing the stone in his apron pocket, the Devil took to the skies, only for his apron to snap and the stone to fall. The Devil's scratch marks can still be seen on the rock, where he tried to catch it but and failed.

properties.name: Devil's Print
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: An ancient hillfort, Devil's Dyke is also rumoured to be a popular place with those who practice the dark arts; it was here a large footprint was found in the mud, twice the size of a human's, with only four toes. Legend has it that the Devil tried to dig a large trench across the land to flood all the churches in the region. He was foiled by either St Dunstan or a local man who made all the cockerels in the area crow, fooling the Devil into thinking dawn was arriving.
properties.location_name: Devil's Dyke (Sussex) - Hillfort, and general location

properties.name: Black Tom of Scothill
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Black Tom is the name of the bell in the church and is tolled the number of the year (ie, in 2007 it was tolled 2007 times) to ensure Old Nick does not visit the village during the following twelve months. The last peal must occur on the stroke of midnight to ensure success.

properties.name: Floating Stone
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The stone was said to float in the air on Halloween's night, the Devil sitting upon it, surrounded by witches, until dawn breaks the following morn.
properties.location_name: Dollar (Clackmannanshire) - Deil's Cradle, found on a glen which was known as Burngrens

properties.name: Meeting Place
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil would stand on this site and preach to his followers below.
properties.location_name: Drymen (Stirling) - Finnich Glen, aka Devil's Pulpit

properties.name: Relocation
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Wayne Anthony, in Haunted Derbyshire and the Peak District, writes that St Alkmund's was going to be constructed next to Duffield castle, but every night for a week the building materials were moved by the Devil to the current site where it was eventually erected.

properties.name: Doom Park
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A circular piece of ground, surrounded by a low wall, was said to have been left untouched by human hands as it belonged to the Devil. It was officially known as the Dome Park, but as it was believed that only evil people were buried there, it acquired the name Doom Park.

properties.name: Missing Steeple
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Night after night, during the construction of the church, the Devil appeared and pulled down the steeple. The builders finally gave up building the tower, and the bells now stand in a little bell house.

properties.name: Dropped
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A large stone in the village was once believed unmovable and was said to have been dropped by the Devil as he constructed Stonehenge.
properties.location_name: East Knoyle (Wiltshire) - Large stone (exact location unknown)

properties.name: Dropped in a Huff
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Angered after hearing the bells of East Worlington church, the Devil dropped this stone - some say it cannot be moved by man or beast. Four crosses decorate the stone.

properties.name: Lady Elizabeth Hatton
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A bloody local legend says that Elizabeth was found here in 1626, her four limbs ripped from her body, but her heart still beating. She was last spotted dancing with a tall dark figure, said to be the devil.

properties.name: Countless Stones
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A baker attempted to count the stones here, to prove that it was possible to number the so-called 'countless stones'. The baker placed a loaf of bread on each rock, and when complete, counted each one he took back. The Devil had other plans, however, and consumed several loaves before the baker had finished.

properties.name: Entrance to Hell
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This hole is said to be bottomless and used by Old Nick to travel between this world and his own.

properties.name: Built by the Devil
properties.description: A local giant made a wager with Old Nick that that a bridge could not be built across the River Barle overnight. Old Nick rolled up his sleeves and promptly proved him wrong. The Devil then took ownership of the bridge, refusing to let anyone cross safely until a priest drove him away.
properties.location_name: Exmoor (Devon) - Tarr Bridge (also known as Tarr Steps), River Barle

properties.name: Devil's Footprints
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil's Footprints ran for one hundred miles across Devon, appearing overnight after a light snowfall. One of the locations they were reported was on this farm, the tracks being recorded on the roof and second floor window. The house is also reputed to be haunted by a blue lady, while a fawn coloured large cat was seen several times over an eight year period.
properties.date: 8/9 February 1855 (footprints), ghost sighting unknown, ABC between 1982-1990

properties.name: Disguised Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil is said to haunt these rocks, disguising himself as a beautiful woman to lure young men to their deaths. Another version of the story drops the Devil from the story, with the female entity capable of beguiling men to fall from the crags.
properties.location_name: Fair Snape Fell (Lancashire) - Exact area unknown

properties.name: Fishy Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: While changing the landscape to make it more hazardous for sailors, the Devil dropped his hammer into the sea. Reaching in to retrieve the tool, the Devil pulled out a haddock, creating marks on the fish which can still be seen today.

properties.name: Moving Stones
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The construction of the church started in Broke Wood, but every night the work which was done was undone and the building materials moved to where the church now stands. Another version of the story says the Devil moved the stones to prevent any work being done but failed.

properties.name: White Oxen
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: On the stroke of midnight, the Devil appears and tries to pull this stone structure down using eight white oxen. A white hare or rabbit also joins them, offering advice. A second variation of the story says the Devil uses four oxen, and a black hound with glowing red eyes watches them. Another legend says that water poured into the cavities on the top stone will quickly vanish, drunk by an unknown horror.

properties.name: Devil's Stone
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil became vexed with the occupants of Gilling village and lobbed a stone at them. As is tradition, he missed, and the stone, complete with his finger marks, landed and remained on the moor.
properties.date: Unknown is stone is still present

properties.name: Hobgoblin Builders
properties.description: Warlock Sir Hugo de Giffard is said to have made a pact with the Devil, and in return had an army of Hobgoblins build this castle for him. A staircase in the hall was filled with earth as it was believed it led straight to hell.
properties.date: Still present, although only subterranean 'Goblin Hall remains'

properties.name: Unlucky Strike
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil tried to throw several lumps of earth at Stevenage - he missed the town, but one of the shots knocked down the steeple at Graveley church.

properties.name: The Infidel's Bible
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A copy of The Infidel's Bible, a black magic book, was accidentally left in the mill and forgotten about. Within days the mill stopped working, and for three years no-one could figure out why. After that time, the book was rediscovered in the mill and burnt - almost immediately, the mill started turning once more.

properties.name: Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Anyone who runs around this ancient work seven times will bump into Old Nick at the end of their journey.
properties.location_name: Great Kimble (Buckinghamshire) - Chequers Park - Cymbeline's Mount (or Castle)

properties.name: Built in a Night
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil agreed to build a bridge between the two locations over the river Monnow, but on condition that he would have the soul of the first creature to cross. Before using the bridge for the first time, a local woman threw a bone for her dog across the bridge - the canine ran to fetch it, and thus cheated the Devil of his human soul.
properties.location_name: Grosmont (Gwent) - Bridge between Grosmont and Kentchurch

properties.name: Jack o'Kent
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A local folk hero who constantly tricked the Devil, Jack was finally laid to rest under the churchyard wall to prevent Satan from taking his soul.

properties.name: Moved
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: During construction, the Devil moved the stones each night to where the church now stands.

properties.name: Devil with his Pack
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: When boars lived wild, the Devil would periodically come out hunting with his hell hounds. They patrolled this area, inducing nightmares in any witness careless enough to watch them.
properties.location_name: Halesowen (West Midlands) - Between town and Lickey Hills

properties.name: Dropped Hot Rock
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Fleeing across the area with a stone lodged in his foot, the Devil shook his leg while passing over the hill. The stone fell upon the hill, with the Devil's footprint remaining visible.

properties.name: Built by the Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Local legend says that this church was erected by Old Nick himself, though his motives are unclear.

properties.name: The Devil's Bridge
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: In a rare moment of generosity, the Devil built the bridge for a local shoemaker who had shared a drink with him.
properties.location_name: Hebdon (North Yorkshire) - Dibble's Bridge, Yorkshire Dales National Park

properties.name: The Devil and his Mum
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A cave by the loch was once home to the devil and his mother, although Old Nick did kill his mum after she slept in and failed to make his porridge for breakfast.

properties.name: Devil Speaking
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Children would once poke pins into a boundary stone before running around it several times. After this they thought they could hear the Devil speaking from deep underground.

properties.name: Dancing Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Local folklore says that drinking from the spring will result in the devil appearing and break into dance on a rock. One village resident has commented after sipping the water, Old Nick did not appear.
properties.location_name: Hemswell (Lincolnshire) - Spring in wooded area north of village

properties.name: Impact Crater
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: One legend says that the punchbowl was created after the Devil leapt into Surrey to avoid sunrise. Another story says Old Nick accidently created the punchbowl while throwing clods of earth at Thor, while a third tale states that the Isle of Wight was created after a giant scooped out the punchbowl and threw the earth at a rival (who he missed).

properties.name: Devil's Stone
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This rock is all that remains after the Devil and the locals threw stones at each other.

properties.name: Devil Playing Cards
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Old Nick once paid a visit to the public house, dealing several of the locals into a game of cards. Before the hand was over one sharp eyed witness noticed the Devil's cloven foot. The Devil quickly vanished, leaving only a strong wind in his wake. (Image copyright 2006 Lawson Clout)

properties.name: Drunk Dry
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Having picked up a local witch in his black coach, the Devil paused outside the church and drank the well dry. The witch was said to continue to haunt the area, occasionally being spotted flying over the nearby moors.

properties.name: Satan's Hoof Print
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A single footprint belonging to Old Nick was said to be visible down this aging alleyway, although the route has now been resurfaced.

properties.name: The Devil Helps Thrice
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This church is one of three in the area (Bloxham and Adderbury being the other two) that were paid for by three brothers. One of their workers who helped construct this tower never took any pay, never slept and never ate - he vanished as soon as the tower was built. The brothers were convinced that they were helped by Old Nick.

properties.name: Summoning
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: There is said to be a tree somewhere in this area where the skeleton of a murdered man is intertwined amongst its roots. Running around the tree seven (or nine) times is said to summon Old Nick.
properties.location_name: Kingston by Sea (Sussex) - Unknown tree in the area

properties.name: The Devil's Garden
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This area of dirt and stone was known as the Devil's Garden by locals as nothing would ever take root there.
properties.location_name: Kington (Hereford & Worcester) - Area of barren land

properties.name: Apron Leakage
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This stone circle was created while the Devil used his apron to carry rocks - the strings snapped, the stones fell, and the devil just let them lie.

properties.name: Built for a Soul
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil built this bridge, in return for the soul of the first who crossed it. Alas for him, a local woman let her dog run across it first, and so Satan left with his new pet. A more recent story concludes by saying that when a new bridge was constructed in 1932, Satan was so upset that he delayed the opening by creating heavy rain, hail, and snowstorms.

properties.name: Rock Hit
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Old Nick appears to have taken a liking to Kirkby Lonsdale - he once threw a large boulder at a church here, scoring a direct hit. The rock created the depression now known as the Devil's Punch Bowl, and the church is said to be intact but buried deep underground.

properties.name: Feast for a King
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The meal is set out by the Devil to lure the living to him; the last person to sit down for the feast was the village parson, who escaped by making Old Nick and the food vanish when he asked for salt.

properties.name: Mustard Grinders
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: According to local legend, anyone who places an ear in an opening to the rock can hear a low grumbling that was thought to be employees of the Devil crushing mustard seeds. Jingling Annas also haunts the bridge - her hands reduced to stumps by the manacles she was forced to wear.
properties.location_name: Kirkby Stephen (Cumbria) - Skinkrith Bridge and River Eden

properties.name: Dead Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: It was written that 'some say the Devil's dead and buried in Kirkcaldy', even though he complained that his toes were cold. To shut the Devil up, a large house was built at the end of the town to ensure his toes were warmed.

properties.name: Cornerless Buildings
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: To prevent the Devil hiding and causing chaos, several buildings in the area were constructed without corners.
properties.location_name: Kirkcudbright (Dumfries and Galloway) - Various buildings in the area

properties.name: Debt Collection
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: It is said a local laird haunts this building, after selling his soul to the Devil for enough money to repay his large debts.
properties.location_name: Lagg, Isle of Arran (Ayrshire) - Lagg Inn

properties.name: Relocated Church
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Legend says that this church was supposed to be built on top of a local hill, but the Devil insisted on moving the building materials overnight.

properties.name: Human Sacrifice
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: How the area of land gained its name was unknown even to nineteenth century folklorists, so it was conjectured that it was an area of human sacrifice.
properties.location_name: Leicester (Leicestershire) - Area of land north-east of the Hoston Stone, Humberstone (was known as Hell-hole Furlong)

properties.name: Walking Backwards
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A local piece of folklore claims that walking backwards around the structure twelve times results in the Devil appearing.
properties.location_name: Leixlip (County Kildare) - Conolly's Folly (aka Conolly's Obelisk)

properties.name: Relocation
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: During construction, the Devil moved the stones each night to where the church now stands. The church was also said to have been the site of a fairy funeral, witnessed by a fisherman returning home late at night and drawn to the church after seeing the lights on.

properties.name: Knight
properties.description: A knight in gold armour and a silver coffin are said to be concealed under this fort. The fort was once believed to have created by Old Nick, one of the mounds of dirt he left behind while digging the nearby 'Devil's Dyke'.
properties.location_name: Lewes (Sussex) - Mount Caburn - iron age hillfort

properties.name: Finger Lickin' Good
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: There are six holes in the door to the well. Legend says if you walk around the well seven times and place your finger in one, good people are awarded with feeling the breath of the Devil on their digit, while bad people have their finger bitten off.
properties.location_name: Lincoln (Lincolnshire) - Queen Anne's Well, cathedral area

properties.name: Devil's Cheese
properties.description: An old piece of folklore says the Devil would make his cheese using this stack of stones. The tor was created when Saint Tue engaged in a stone tossing contest with Uther, one of the local giants. One of Uther's stones missed the target; Saint Tue picked the rock up and an angel carried it to the top of the tor. Most of the giants promptly converted to Christianity.

properties.name: The Demons Below See
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This old well has the words 'Qui non dat quod habet, Daemon infra vide 1414' carved above it. It was said that any traveller passing would have no choice but to drink the waters and give a donation to charity, otherwise the Devil (chained under the well) would laugh.

properties.name: Footprints
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Up to his nightly no-good antics, the Devil entered this church, scaled the tower and tried to steal the bells. The vicar witnessed this and managed to drive the Devil away - the Horned One jumped from the bell tower, leaving an impression of his foot on a stone in the graveyard.

properties.name: Devil's Home
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Old Nick once set up home in a cave here but was driven away by clergy after his presence grew too much for the locals to tolerate.

properties.name: Dancing Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: On the eve of Midsummer, the Devil would emerge and spend time dancing with the local fairies around a clump of trees.
properties.location_name: Llangattock (Powys) - Field north of Llangattock Park

properties.name: Stolen by the Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil stole the tower of the church but Saint Cyfelach caught him and forced Old Nick to drop it a short distance away.
properties.location_name: Llangyfelach (West Glamorgan) - Church with separate tower

properties.name: Tall Man
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: One of several churches in Wales where the Devil was once said to have appeared in the form of a man with an evil looking face (or the head of a dog), with his appearance causing illness to witnesses.

properties.name: Devil's Hideout
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Contained within the branches of this old, withered oak tree, Old Nick sometimes hides. The troublesome Devil would leap onto the backs of men as they passed, draining their energy and not letting go until they reached home or somewhere safe.
properties.location_name: Llanrhos (Clwyd) - The Devil's Tree (old oak tree)

properties.name: Tall Man
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: One of several churches in Wales where the Devil was once said to have appeared in the form of a man with an evil looking face (or the head of a dog), with his appearance causing illness to witnesses.

properties.name: Devil's Coach Marks
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: During the winter months, when the Devil visits Scotland, the wheels on his coach leave visible marks across frozen Lochs.
properties.location_name: Lochs in Scotland (various districts) (Highland) - No exact location given

properties.name: Petrified Witch
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This large column of rock is reportedly Maggie the Witch, who failed the Devil on so many occasions he finally hit her with lightning and turned her to stone. Another version of the story says Maggie deliberately antagonised the devil, resulting in her petrification.

properties.name: Divot Tosser
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Local cunning man Adam Donald visited this farm after the devil moved in, throwing divots at the family and causing kitchen utensils to dance (what would now be seen as poltergeist activity). Donald's use of the arcane arts ensued peace returned to the household.

properties.name: Hill Creation
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Upset by the building of the Evesham Abbey, Old Nick threw a lump of dirt at it, but the prayers of Saint Egwin deflected the missile. The projectile ended up many miles away, becoming Meon Hill.

properties.name: Escape Route
properties.description: While combing her hair nearby, Saint Menfre was confronted by the Devil. She threw her comb at him, striking with such force he dug Lundy Hole in his haste to return to hell.

properties.name: Frozen Nick
properties.description: The area is said to become so cold at night that the Devil died of exposure and is buried under a hill. Phantom highwaymen are also said to ride around the countryside.

properties.name: Dancer
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: For the heinous crime of leading a troupe of dancers on the Sabbath, Jack was turned to stone by the Devil.

properties.name: Winner
properties.description: A group of miners playing cards invited a passing stranger to join their game. He sat with them, quickly winning all hands, although the game quickly came to an end when the miners fled, having realised their guest had cloven feet.

properties.name: Dancing with the Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: According to one legend, a particularly good looking stranger asked a local girl at the ballroom to dance with him. She did so, but halfway through the tune the girl collapsed to the floor and the man quickly left. Upon recovering from her swoon, she told her friends she had looked down at his feet during the dance and spotted cloven hooves!

properties.name: South of the River
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The villagers of March wanted their church in the village, but the Devil felt so passionately about his fenland he moved the construction materials across the river.

properties.name: Devil's Jumps
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The small clumps of trees between these points are where the Devil bounced along the ground after being kicked by the Abbot of Forde. Some say that these trees move to a nearby stream to drink late at night, while others tell of the Devil returning after dark to swing from tree to tree.
properties.location_name: Marshwood (Dorset) - Clumps of trees between village and Thorncombe

properties.name: Dropped Church
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil tried to steal the bell tower, but found it too heavy and dropped it after several steps - church and tower remain separated to this day.
properties.location_name: Marston Moretaine (Bedfordshire) - St Mary the Virgin church

properties.name: Moved Stones
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil tried to prevent the church from being built by moving stones at night, but he ultimately failed.

properties.name: Tongs
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This convent holds the tongs used by St Dunstan to grab the Devil by the nose - the fallen angel was disguised in woman's clothing, but St Dustan noticed his cloven feet. After the attack, Old Nick ran off to the springs at Tunbridge Wells.

properties.name: Burnt Flag
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Samuel Bamford wrote that the school flag had a hole in the shape of a burnt hoofprint. Students had summoned the Devil and had to be saved by the school's headmaster who manged to exorcise the fallen angel, although the ritual damaged some of the building.

properties.name: Sinking Church
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A church is said to have once stood here, but the Devil appears and dragged it underground - all that remains are fifty-five stones that form a circle.

properties.name: Petrified Quoits
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: According to legend, King Arthur stood on Hel Tor, the Devil on Blackingstone, and a game of quoits ensued. They threw discs at each other until Arthur finally won - the Devil, disgusted with the outcome, turned the last two quoits into stone.
properties.location_name: Moretonhampstead (Devon) - Hel Tor and Blackingstone hills

properties.name: St Catherine's Throw
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: To settle a dispute over who should control the island, Saint Catherine and the Devil agreed to a stone tossing contest. Saint Catherine's throw was straight and true, landing some seven miles away. The Devil's throw fell short and, true to his word, he left the island. Other versions of the story replace Saint Catherine with a neighbourhood giant, but with the same outcome. Either way, the thrown boulder remains for all to see. It is now thought the two stones are part of a Neolithic long barrow, but of course, I'm sure that's what the Devil wants you to believe.

properties.name: Wild Humphrey
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Wild Humphrey Kynaston, local highwayman and friend to the poor, used this cave as a hideout. His horse could make jumps so daring the creature was said to either be the Devil himself, or alternatively, the Devil had helped the horse.

properties.name: Devil's Hoof
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A hoof print is visible in a stone by the gate. The Devil was once so upset by the sound of the church bells, he climbed the steeple with the intent of stealing them; he slipped and fell, leaving the single piece of evidence behind.

properties.name: The Pot o' Pittenyoul
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Pot o' Pittenyoul was a small, rocky pool along the stream. A local legend said that one of the worn stones was a seat used by the devil.
properties.location_name: Newmill, Keith (Moray) - Stream by the hamlet

properties.name: Moved Foundations
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The church here was intended to be built closer to Thornton-le-Moor but the Devil moved the stones to where the church was finally constructed.

properties.name: Downed Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Old Nick is said to have died from the cold as he passed through this area.

properties.name: Relocated Church
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The church once stood in Mary's Grove until the Devil appeared one night and moved the structure to its present location.

properties.name: Sir Rowland
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Sir Rowland Alston made a deal with the Devil, though when Old Nick came for Alston's soul, the man fled to the nearby church for help. It proved futile, and Alston was dragged to hell. Scratch marks can be seen in the church porch where Alston tried to hold on as the Devil dragged him away. The scene is re-enacted once a century.

properties.name: The Passenger
properties.description: The pond is said to be bottomless; occasionally a phantom coach pulled by headless horses led by a decapitated driver passes through the village and dives into the pool, their passenger none other than the Devil on his way home.

properties.name: The Devil Takes His Due
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil is said to appear and burn down an old mill, containing a miller, his daughter, and a visitor.

properties.name: Dropped by Old Nick
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The church once stood in the middle of the village, but the Devil tried to run off with it. He dropped the building where it currently stands when he heard the ringing of the bells at a nearby abbey.

properties.name: Pleased to Meet you...
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: An unknown bush in the village can be used to summon the Devil, by dancing three (or thirteen) times around it, and/or saying (or not saying) a spell.

properties.name: Relocation
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The villagers were going to build their church at Crownhill Castle, but the Devil created so much havoc during the laying of the foundations the building was relocated to where it now stands.

properties.name: Dr Foster
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: These stones where thrown into position when Dr Foster and the Devil had a hurling contest. Another story says it was the Devil and Saint Dubricius, while a third says it was a giant and Old Nick.
properties.location_name: Porlock (Somerset) - Whitstones (aka Whit Stones), northeast of common

properties.name: Thrown
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: These stones came into being when Old Nick threw it from Portland Pike, during a game of Quoits.

properties.name: Cheap Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Old Nick once popped into this public house for a quick pint during a storm. He resembled a normal man, apart from his cloven feet that were spotted by the landlady. While the Devil did pay for his drink, the money turned into old leaves the moment he left.

properties.name: Grave
properties.description: A giant's grave is said to be within this wooded area. The area is said to take its name from a rock that sits on the edge of a nearby cliff, thrown there by the Devil during a competition.

properties.name: Tall Man
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: One of several churches in Wales where the Devil was once said to have appeared in the form of a man with an evil looking face (or the head of a dog), with his appearance causing illness to witnesses.

properties.name: Devil's Lock
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A piece of nineteenth century folklore said the Devil in the form of a man would sit on the lock gate at midnight, and only heaven could help anyone who tried to use his service during this time.

properties.name: Croydon Hill Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: An old lane on this hill was once reportedly home to a creature with horns. A local lad, who once dressed up as the creature to play a joke on strangers who used the lane, disappeared without trace; it is known thought his ghostly screams can be heard on stormy nights, as the real Devil takes him.

properties.name: The Devil's Burning Nose
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: After being attacked by Saint Dunstan in Mayfield, Old Nick ran here and placed his flaming nose into the waters - they turned red and have remained so ever since.

properties.name: The Rudston Monolith
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The tallest monolith in the UK, one tale states the stone was thrown by the Devil with the intent of knocking down the nearby church. Another tale states that the rock was dropped from the sky, killing one or two people intent of desecrating the graves.

properties.name: The Old Brick Mover
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil was said to have moved the bricks from the church's original build site just as quick as the builders could lay them. The builders finally gave up and constructed the church where it stands today.

properties.name: Failed Bridge
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil once tried to build a bridge between Seascale and the Isle of Man - his apron split and all but one of the stones were lost at sea. The remaining rock remains, with two white seams showing where the apron strings were once wrapped.

properties.name: Wishing Stone
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil is said to be responsible for the creation of this wishing stone.

properties.name: Don't Upset the Tramp
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A tramp (or angel or witch, depending on the source) looking for a place to rest was turned away from every house in the neighbourhood, until he came to a farm on the hillside where the occupants of which gave him food and shelter. He thanked his guests and punished the other households by flooding the valley. The lake also once had two large rocks on its banks which were known as the Mermaid Stones, said to have been thrown there by the Devil and a giant.
properties.location_name: Semerwater (aka Simmer Water, aka Simmerwater) (North Yorkshire) - Lake

properties.name: Another Thrown Stone
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This large stone was thrown into position by the Devil (as he chilled at Bul Barrow).
properties.location_name: Shaftesbury (Dorset) - The Popple Stone, Doncliffe Hall Farm (private property)

properties.name: Cloven Hoof Card Player
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The story goes that young people would meet at the barn to play cards on a Sunday. One day they were joined by a stranger who sat in for a couple of games. He dropped a card, and as he bent down to pick it up, other players spotted that he had a cloven hoof. None of the card players ever played on a Sunday again.
properties.location_name: Shaftesbury (Dorset) - Three hundred year old barn (likely no longer standing), French Mill Lane

properties.name: Turn it Over
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Once a year the villagers turn a large stone over that stands by an oak. It is said that if they fail to do so, disaster and ill-fortune will strike the region - one legend says the last time the village forgot to rotate the stone was just before the Second World War began. Some believe the Devil himself is trapped under the stone.

properties.name: Devil or Other
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Sidney Oldall Addy, in a book about the local area, assumed that the woods took the name from a monster, water sprite, a goblin or the Devil.

properties.name: Hell Hole
properties.description: An entrance to the lower world was said to be located somewhere in Sheffield.
properties.location_name: Sheffield (South Yorkshire) - Somewhere between Neepsend Bicarage and Rutland Road

properties.name: Angry Old Nick
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This rocky outcrop is named after Old Nick after he accidentally dropped stones here instead of Hell's Gutter. A local legend says the Devil hates the area so much that he continues to bring heavy boulders to the area, hoping the county will sink beneath the sea.

properties.name: Devil's Room
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The local priest offered to play cards with the first owner of the house, who responded, 'I'll rather play cards with the devil'. When the devil turned up at the house to take the owner up on his offer, the room where the horned one waited was promptly bricked up (and remains so to this day). The property is also haunted by several ghosts, including horses that are heard galloping but never seen.

properties.name: Onion
properties.description: A local giant named Onion tossed a stone that landed on the common, a mile away, leaving the impressions of his fingers upon it. Others have said that the stone was thrown by the Devil.
properties.location_name: Silchester (Hampshire) - Stone on the common, Hampshire/Berkshire Border

properties.name: Devil's Treasure
properties.description: A local legend states that the door handle of the church is all that remains of a treasure chest discovered by three (or two) men at the bottom of Callow Pit. As the men tried to take the chest away with them, Old Nick appeared and pulled at the other end - the men managed to escape with only one handle from the chest.
properties.location_name: Southwood (Norfolk) - Old church and Callow Pit

properties.name: Devil's Headstone
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A local legend speaks of the 'Devils Gravestone'; from the nearby path you could see the image of Old Nick upon a headstone, but when approached, nothing can be seen. A witness who would walk past the illusion on the way to school in the 1960s said that the headstone was moved in the 1980s, destroying the pareidolia.

properties.name: Knox
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: John Knox was banished from St Andrews after he raised the Devil. The event was supposedly witnessed by Knox's servant Richard Bannatyne, who became insane and died shortly after.

properties.name: Moving Bricks
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The church was going to be built over a mile away, but the workmen awoke the day the construction was due to start and found their materials had been moved closer to the sea. The men spent the day moving everything back to the original spot, but after the materials were once again moved the following night, the church was constructed where it now stands. The jury is divided on whether God, the Devil or fairy folk were responsible.

properties.name: Devil's Print
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The area was reputedly the meeting place for witches and for summoning the devil. A footprint left by the devil is said to be still visible on a rock.

properties.name: Dando
properties.description: Dando was a monk and a bit of a rogue - he enjoyed hunting on a Sunday. One Sabbath he was out hunting for hare when the Devil appeared and grabbed all the game Dando had hanging from his horse. Old Nick ran, Dando in hot pursuit - unfortunately, the monk lost his balance on the bank of the Tiddy and fell in. The waters turned to steam, and Dando was taken to hell.
properties.location_name: St Germans (Cornwall) - Erth Hill, near the rivers of Tiddy and Lynher

properties.name: Mowing Contest
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil and the village smith had a hay mowing contest in the village. Unbeknown to Old Nick, the smith planted obstacles (most likely iron stakes) in the Devil's half of the field, ensuring the Devil's scythe quickly became blunt. Enraged, Old Nick threw his whetstone at the smith - it missed and landed in a field where it remained for some time before being removed.
properties.location_name: St Mabyn (Cornwall) - Exact location not known

properties.name: Sea Sounds
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A wooden devil figurehead from a ship once stood here, and although the first was stolen and burned, a modern replacement exist. Although some modern tales relate the crashing of the sea from the holes to legends, there is little to suggest people ever thought the noise was related to the Devil.

properties.name: Moved
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: During construction, the Devil moved the stones each night to where the church now stands.

properties.name: New Shoes for the Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This inn was once a forge, where the Devil was caught making new shoes for himself.
properties.location_name: St Nicholas (South Glamorgan) - Dusty Forge Inn

properties.name: Wedding Party
properties.description: Dressed as a fiddler, the Devil convinced all the guests from a wedding to dance on a Sunday - for the sin, the entire party were turned to stone. Another creation story for the site says a passing giant dropped the contents of his shovel that formed the circle of stones.

properties.name: The Devil's Game
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Playing Quoits on Beacon Hill, some two and a half miles away, the Devil was informed he was not playing the game correctly. In a rage, he threw the stones to where they now stand.

properties.name: Devil's Gold
properties.description: The stone was once thought to have gold concealed underneath, hidden by the Devil. However, when moved to make way for a new road, nothing was found.
properties.location_name: Staple Fitzpaine (Somerset) - Devil's Stone, near the church

properties.name: Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil is reputed to cross the bridge once a year - if he looks you in the eye, he'll take your soul!

properties.name: Construction Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The foundation stones of the church were moved around the village by Old Nick nine times before the rest of the building could be constructed. Another version of the story blames fairies.

properties.name: Devil's Bath
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The hot springs found in the region are said to be used by the Devil to wash both himself and his dirty linen.
properties.location_name: Strathpeffer (Highland) - Hot springs in the area

properties.name: Missed
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Agglestone came into being when the Devil, angered by Corfe Castle obstructing his view from Needles Rock (in the Isle of Wight), removed his nightcap and threw it in an to knock over the towers. He failed.
properties.location_name: Studland (Dorset) - Agglestone (aka Devil's Anvil, aka Devil's Nightcap)

properties.name: Harry
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The cliffs around this area are known as Old Nick's Ground, while the sea stack made from chalk is called Old Harry (Harry being another name for the Devil). Another rock stack that stood here was said to be the Devil's Wife (or Daughter), but it collapsed during a storm in the 1890s; the remains of the stack is just visible during low spring tides. The caves in the cliff are said to still be warm as Harry likes to spend time there.

properties.name: Whistle
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: An old belief from this area of Norfolk was by running around the church at midnight and whistling through the keyhole, the Devil would be seen.

properties.name: Sunbathing Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The steps were created by Old Nick, and he uses them so he can catch some rays on the nicer days of summer.
properties.location_name: Tarr Steps (Somerset) - By the River Barle

properties.name: St Michael saves the Day
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The church was to be built at the base of the hill, but the Devil came every night and moved the foundation stones to the top. Even though St Michael appeared and scared Old Nick away, the church was built where the Devil wanted it to be.

properties.name: Drinker
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: When the Devil hears the clock strike twelve, he emerges and drinks from the nearby stream.

properties.name: John Alcock
properties.description: Highwayman John Alcock stashed much of his ill-gotten gains in a cave under this hill, in a chest protected by three locks and guarded by a cockerel. The bird will only let treasure hunters pass if they possessed one of Alcock's bones. Another legend says the Devil created the hill; out picking nuts on Devil's Nutting Day (21 September), Old Nick stumbled across the Virgin Mary who ordered him to drop the nuts. The Devil did, thus creating the hill.
properties.location_name: Temple Grafton (Warwickshire) - Alcocks Arbour (aka Rolls Wood Hill), Croft Lane

properties.name: Broken Tooth
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This mountain is so named after Old Nick took a bite from it - he broke a tooth in the process, which formed the Rock of Cashel.

properties.name: Concealed House
properties.description: A local story states that a mansion lies buried under the hill, filled with wealth beyond dreams. Another story says that the hill was created by the Devil, created by mud falling from his shoe. Walking around the hill seven times at midnight is said to summon him.

properties.name: A Quick Drink
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This mere has an erratic water level; perhaps previous generations attributed the sudden disappearance of water to the Devil having a quick drink while passing by.

properties.name: The Devil's Holes
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The name 'Grime's Graves' is thought to have come about in the sixteenth century, Grime being a corruption of Grim, a pagan god that Christians associated with the devil.
properties.location_name: Thetford (Norfolk) - Grime's Graves, prehistoric flint mine

properties.name: Jumping Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil is sometimes thought to exercise by jumping over the trees located between these two locations.
properties.location_name: Thornecombe (Dorset) - Trees between village and Birdsmore Gate

properties.name: Moved Again
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The village folk wanted their church in a hollow by their homes, but the Devil had other plans, and moved it to the hill.

properties.name: Contest
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil and the giant Jack o'Kent had a stone throwing contest - the Devil's stone landed here, while Jack's landed way over the River Severn, winning him the game.
properties.location_name: Tidenham (Gloucestershire) - The Broadstone, between village and Woolastaston

properties.name: Take it Down
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The Devil became upset at the building of this church, so during the night would undo the construction work. It is unclear who or what motivated the Devil to cease his actions. Another story says a visitor to the site watched a monk dressed in black walk through the church and vanish at the pulpit.

properties.name: Knight Meeting with the Devil
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Local legend recalls a knight who wished to build a house a mile away from the field. The devil came each night and pulled the house down, until the knight agreed to erect the building closer to the field.

properties.name: A Woman Scorned
properties.description: Scorned by a knight, a woman invited the warrior and his new love to meet here. The woman murdered the couple, and as a storm developed, the Devil appeared and took the murderess into a nearby hole (presumably to hell).

properties.name: Kings
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: There are seven barrows at Five Knolls and legend has them contain former kings. During a seventeenth century witch trial, local woman Elizabeth Pratt claimed to have met the Devil at the site.

properties.name: Stone Thief
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The tower's shortness is explained away by the story that Old Nick turned up each night during construction and knocked down the day's work. The builders finally grew tired of the conflict and put the roof on at the height is now.

properties.name: Quoits
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The capstone of this dolmen was thrown into position by the Devil whilst playing quoits.

properties.name: Daffyd Ddu
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Former vicar and black magician, Daffyd made a pact with the Devil to take his soul 'whether buried in the churchyard or out'. Needless to say, the wizard cheated Old Nick by ensuring his body was buried under the boundary wall.

properties.name: Thor the Rock Thrower
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The god Thor would sit on nearby Treyford Hill. One day the Devil came along and, in a fit of boredom, started to hop from one barrow to the next. This childishness annoyed Thor, who scared Old Nick off by throwing a rock at him.
properties.location_name: Treyford (Sussex) - Devil's Jumps (set of five barrows)

properties.name: Marshy Bells
properties.description: The Devil once appeared in the village, stealing the church bells and sinking them into the nearby marsh. Bubbles in the marsh were said to be the result of the bells continuing to sink into the bottomless pit.
properties.location_name: Tunstall (Norfolk) - Church, and marsh known as Bell Hole (and Hell Hole)

properties.name: Devil Summoning
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: To run around this stone circle three times at midnight will bring forth the devil.

properties.name: The Devil Can't Hide
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The round cottages found in the village were built thusly so the devil could not conceal himself in the corners. A cross was also placed in all the roofs, guaranteeing that he could not carry them away either.
properties.location_name: Veryan in Roseland (Cornwall) - Round cottages in the village

properties.name: Moving Head
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This house was built out of stones from the ruined Catley Abbey. A carved stone head which was incorporated into the building was said to move every night, until the house was demolished.
properties.location_name: Walcot (near Digby) (Lincolnshire) - Stone house (no longer standing)

properties.name: Devil Summoning
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Anyone reciting the Lord's Prayer backwards whilst walking around the large cross in the churchyard will call upon the Devil.

properties.name: Dropped Tower
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Attempting to steal the bell tower, the Devil dropped it shortly after flying off. The tower landed a few dozen metres away, where it still stands.

properties.name: Satan Takes a Town
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Whitstable was founded after the Devil tried to steal the buildings of Canterbury - he was caught, and dropped the stolen houses eight miles away, where they stayed.

properties.name: Storm
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A terrible storm descended on the village during a church service - the sky turned black, a ball of fire entered the church, and parts of the tower broke away. Several people died, including a man by the name of Jan Reynolds who some claimed was taken away by the Devil (possibly on horseback) as punishment for falling asleep (or playing cards) during the sermon (or previously selling his soul).

properties.name: Thrown Stone
properties.description: Battlegore was the landing site of a stone thrown by a giant from West Quantoxhead, competing with the Devil. Old Nick's rock fell short, resulting in his banishment from the area.

properties.name: Drop and Run
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: An old folk tale states that by dropping a pin through the keyhole of the church and running around the building seven times, the Devil will be met.

properties.name: Clawed Stone
properties.description: The stone was finally buried by a farmer, who dug a hole next to the boulder and pushed it in. A failed previous attempt had resulted in the Devil appearing who scratched the stone with his claws. The stone was believed to have been placed on top of treasure, however when the stone was finally moved to one said, no treasure (or Devil) was found.
properties.date: Moved to the side of the field in 1970
properties.location_name: Winceby (Lincolnshire) - Boulder near area known as Slash Hollow

properties.name: Frozen Figures
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The stones are said to be either children (or maidens) who were petrified for dancing on the Sabbath, or the Devil, his wife, and his children.

properties.name: First and Last
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The peninsula was the cause of many shipwrecks and locals would say it was the place that 'God made last and the Devil will take first'. A well on the point was said to cure poor eyesight.

properties.name: Devil's Grannie
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: It was said that the Devil hanged his grandmother from this crag on the fort.
properties.location_name: Wooler (Northumberland) - Hanging crag on Doddington Hill Fort

properties.name: Changed Site
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: It was said that the Devil did not want this church to be built on a nearby hill, so spent every night undoing the construction work which had been performed the day before and moving the materials to the site the church now stands.

properties.name: Raising Miller
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: Dancing or walking around the tomb seven times will summon the miller who is buried within, according to one legend. Others say that it will be the Devil who appears when the rite is complete. More recently, two witnesses encountered a large black dog during a rainy evening; the creature silently watched the pair causing the couple to quickly leave.
properties.location_name: Worthing (Sussex) - The Miller's Tomb, Highdown Hill

properties.name: A Man of Wealth & Taste
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: A legend says that if you walk around the Devils Kneading Trough seven times then drink the water from the natural spring, you will see the devil.

properties.name: Chain
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: The chains heard rattling somewhere underground in this area were said to belong to the devil. One story said an underground tunnel existed in the area.

properties.name: The Good Musician
properties.description: A small band of musicians were performing around the city when they found themselves joined by another unannounced member. He played the violin particularly well, and as the band completed a song standing by Beedham's Court, their new-found violinist vanished, leaving only the strong smell of brimstone.
properties.location_name: York (North Yorkshire) - Passageway once known as Beedham's Court, off Skeldergate

properties.name: Sir Dafydd Llwyd
properties.type:original_category: The Devil and All His Works
properties.description: This local wizard had made a pact with Satan, and in return for power Llyyd's soul would belong to Old Nick whether 'buried inside a churchyard or out'. On his deathbed, the wily wizard asked his friends to bury him under the churchyard wall, thus preventing the Devil taking his due.

properties.name: Dragon Slayer
properties.description: The village is named after a local hero who slew a dragon but died as the beast released a final breath of flame. In another version of the tale, the hero survives and finds a brood of hatchlings in the dragon's cave, which is subsequently blocked up.
properties.location_name: Aller, Curry Rivel (Somerset) - Exact area unknown

properties.name: Dragon Stone
properties.description: A farmer watched in horror as his horses and plough were sucked underground in the middle of a field - a few seconds later a large dragon emerged and flew off. The stone that remains today is said to cover the dragon's treasure. Some believe the dragon was in fact Satan.
properties.location_name: Anwick (Lincolnshire) - Drake Stone, currently in the churchyard

properties.name: Banished Wyrms
properties.description: Whilst visiting Britain, St Paul, was supposed to have banished forever all snakes, dragons, and thunderstorms. One out of three is not too bad...

properties.name: Wybrant Gwiber
properties.description: A bandit from a nearby village tried to kill the Wybrant Gwiber but failed. The bandit's throat was torn out and his body thrown into the river. What happened to the dragon is not known.

properties.name: Imprints
properties.description: A large snake-like creature wrapped itself around Bignor hill and squeezed so tightly that it left imprints of its coils.

properties.name: Hording Lizard
properties.description: A dragon was said to reside in a tumulus, protecting its hoard.
properties.location_name: Bilsdale (North Yorkshire) - Exact location not known

properties.name: Limbless Worm
properties.description: A creature, said by legend to be a long, hostile worm, but likely to have been a boar, inhabited an oak wood and would attack man and beast. It was slain by a member of the Pollard family.

properties.name: Duel
properties.description: The area was held to ransom by a fire breather who demanded a pail of milk once a day. The knight who engaged the dragon in battle barely made it out of the conflict with his life, and though the dragon lay slain, the victor died shortly after.

properties.name: Piers the Dragon Killer
properties.description: Piers Shonks is a celebrated local dragon slayer, whose tomb in the village church is decorated with his battle. One story says he died after Satan came to seek revenge for the death of his pet, another that Satan tried to claim his soul many years later when Piers lay on his deathbed; the Devil said that he would take Piers' soul whether buried inside a church or not, so the man was buried in the north wall of the local church, denying the Devil of his prize.
properties.location_name: Brent Pelham (Hertfordshire) - Exact location unknown, but buried in St Mary church

properties.name: George and the Dragon
properties.description: The village of Brinsop claims to be the location where St George had his famous battle with a dragon. The creature lived in Duck's Pool Meadow but was finally slain at Lower Stanks meadow. An old stone carving in the church depicts the final battle.
properties.location_name: Brinsop (Hereford & Worcester) - Lower Stanks and Duck's Pool Meadow near the church

properties.name: Dragon Slaying
properties.description: The local church gives the account of how a local dragon that was immune to arrows was finally chased into the marshes where it vanished. A virtually identical tale exists from nearby Wormingford.

properties.name: Sheep Eater
properties.description: A dragon terrorised the neighbourhood around the hill after demanding a sacrifice of sheep - the locals had given him milk instead. Before long, a dependable knight of yore came along, covered his armour with birdlime (a sticky substance made from bark) and powered glass, and the engaged the creature. The knight may have won the fight, but his two hunting dogs were killed, and he later died of dragon-inflected injuries.
properties.location_name: Burley (Hampshire) - Hill known as Burley Beacon

properties.name: Slain Dragon
properties.description: A member of the Buslingthorpe family was said to have killed a dragon in this area and was awarded a large amount of land for the act of bravery.

properties.name: Carantoc's Dragon
properties.description: Saint Carantoc, while searching for his altar, met with King Arthur who voiced concerns about a dragon terrorizing the county. Carantoc agreed to help the King (in return for the location of the altar), and tamed the dragon using his stole. Locals wanted to kill the creature, but the saint set it free after making the dragon promise never to hurt anyone again.

properties.name: One Eyed Dragon
properties.description: Sir Hugh Bardolfe takes credit for slaying this dragon, noted for having a single eye located in its forehead. Man and beast fought during a storm, and when the dragon was blinded by a flash of lightning, Sir Hugh struck a wart on one of its legs, killing it.

properties.name: Winged Serpent
properties.description: A serpent dwelt in a well which took the creature's name and would fly between the hole and Cawthorn Park.
properties.location_name: Cawthorne (South Yorkshire) - Between Serpent's Well and Cawthorn Park

properties.name: Firey Creatures
properties.description: Breathing fire as they travelled, the dragons in this region are said to have an interest in Bronze Age burial mounds, possibly protecting them from treasure hunters.

properties.name: Sea Dragon
properties.description: The third abbot of Saint Martin of Tournai documented the arrival of a five headed dragon in Christchurch. The dragon was said to have emerged from the sea and taken flight towards the town, destroying the church and many of the surrounding houses (but not those occupied by the abbot and his entourage). The dean of the destroyed church tried to escape by boat, but the vessel was also incinerated (although the dean escaped harm).
properties.location_name: Christchurch (Dorset) - Skies above and off coast

properties.name: Wormstall
properties.description: The dragon which took up residence in this area was killed by a knight; the lashing of the dying dragon's tail is said to have carved out a hollow in a field known as Wormstall.
properties.location_name: Churchstanton (Somerset) - Area where Stapley Farm now stands

properties.name: Multi-headed Dragon
properties.description: Two men from the village engaged a double-headed winged lizard that terrorised the area, winning the battle by forcing the creature to eat burning pitch. Carvings on the benches in the Church of the Holy Ghost show some of the battle.

properties.name: White Worm
properties.description: A local blacksmith eventually killed this beast by constructing a suit of armour covered with retractable spikes. The creature swallowed the smith whole who then wriggled so violently that the monster's intestines were ripped to shreds.

properties.name: Smith's Wyrm
properties.description: John Smith, a local hero, was rewarded with a large amount of land after killing a dragon that was trying to make a home in the neighbourhood. Smith left a large quantity of milk for the creature to consume. The wyrm drunk so much milk that kinks appeared in its scales, enabling Smith to decapitate it with an axe.

properties.name: Vortigern's Tower
properties.description: Every time construction started on Vortigern's Tower, it collapsed. Vortigern asked the druids why this was, and they recommended sacrificing a boy to settle the earth. A young lad was brought, but he told Vortigern that the tower fell because two mighty dragons fought beneath the ground in a stone chest. This boy grew up to become Merlin.
properties.location_name: Dinas Emrys (Gwynedd) - Thought to be location where the spring can be found

properties.name: Flying Beast
properties.description: To guard the treasure buried under both hills, this beast flies between the two locations each night.
properties.location_name: Dolbury (aka Dolebury) (Devon) - Exe valley between Dolbury Hill & Cadbury Hill

properties.name: Water Monster
properties.description: Once home to a strange water creature which could paralyse with its gaze, a legend tells of a brave farmer who took its head off with an axe blow as it rested. The monster was buried on the mountainside.

properties.name: Buried Wyrm
properties.description: A dragon is mentioned in a document dating to 772 as being buried in a prehistoric tumulus.

properties.name: Saint Serf's Victory
properties.description: Saint Serf (or Servanus) killed a local dragon using his bishop's staff.
properties.location_name: Dunning (Perth and Kinross) - Area around the iron age fort

properties.name: Great Worm
properties.description: This dragon was discovered hiding amongst the tombs of the churchyard; it was finally slain by Sir James Tyrell. Some reports say the dragon lived in the nearby woods.

properties.name: Griffin
properties.description: The village blacksmith killed a griffin that threatened the safety of this community.

properties.name: Wyrm
properties.description: The Lambton worm, sounding more like a very large snake, took up residence here and terrorised the locals - on returning from the crusades, a young and noble member of the Lambton fought the creature and won; but only by wearing spiked armour that impaled the creature as it wrapped itself around him.
properties.location_name: Fatfield (Durham) - Lambton Castle and grounds, Worm Hill

properties.name: Ghostly Dragon
properties.description: Many years ago, before the construction of the road and railway, a large lizard creature was said to reside here. It possessed qualities more like a ghost than a physical creature.

properties.name: Hissing Creature
properties.description: A worm was reputed to live in this area, rushing out to hiss at anyone who passed its lair.

properties.name: Slay Lie
properties.description: Local lord Seigneur de Hambye killed a dragon in the marshes close to here but was then murdered by his squire. The squire took credit for killing the monster, and claimed the beast had killed de Hambye, but not before his former master requested that the squire marry his widow. Fortunately, the squire would sleep talk, and it was not too long before the truth slipped from his lips in the marital bed. He was hanged for murder. Seigneur de Hambye was buried under La Hougue Bie.

properties.name: Wealthy Dragon
properties.description: It is thought that the wyrm which established its home in the hill brought with him a great deal of treasure.
properties.location_name: Gunnarton Fell (Northumberland) - Gunnarton Castle, aka Money Hill

properties.name: Dragon's Lair
properties.description: Living off maidens and the odd monk, the dragon was finally slain when a man wearing heat proof armour stabbed him through the neck. As a reward, he married the surviving maiden discovered in the lair.

properties.name: Dropper of Fire
properties.description: A large rock on the outskirts of this town is said to have been dropped by a huge dragon as it passed overhead.

properties.name: Basking Dragon
properties.description: The nine foot long dragon with 'eyes as big as a sheep's' once spent a few days basking in a field in this area, before taking leave (chased away by concerned villagers). The creature caused no damage and remained a popular local character until quite recently. Another version of the tale is not so dragon-friendly; the creature lived in a cave and would venture out at night to eat cattle and sheep - it only stopped when the cave roof collapsed, trapping the creature within.

properties.name: Long Wyrm
properties.description: This wood was once the home of a long dragon, which caused some concern prior to being hacked apart by a forester.

properties.name: Poisonous Dragon
properties.description: This wooded area was home to a nine foot long dragon in the seventeenth century - it killed men with its poison, but did not eat them, preferring rabbits and smaller creatures. It was coloured black, with a red belly. Even today, there are reports of another unknown creature that travels in the branches of the trees, rarely descending to the forest floor. A nineteenth century story has a headless phantom, named as Squire Paulett by some, which would leap on to the back of any horses being ridden through the forest. The entity would hold on tight and ride pillion, vanishing when its victim reached the edge of the forest.

properties.name: Goraidh Crobhan
properties.description: Crobhan killed the dragon that had levelled most of the island by having the beast chase him while he travelled with three horses. The hero ensured that the dragon ate the three horses during the pursuit, resulting in the dragon slowing down and tiring. Still being chased, Crobhan leapt across a series of barrels in which he had earlier hammered iron stakes - the dragon failed the jump, impaling itself on the stakes, ensuring Crobhan had an easy kill.
properties.location_name: Islay (Argyll and Bute) - Dragon den close to Ballygrant

properties.name: Terror of Mull
properties.description: A fearsome dragon terrorised the island until a hero appeared and killed the beast with the inventive use of barrels with big spikes attached to them. This story and the dragon tale told of Islay are strikingly similar.
properties.location_name: Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - General area

properties.name: Ormroyd
properties.description: A shepherd called Ormroyd and his faithful dog fought a dragon which lived here; the pair managed to slay the creature, though died soon after.

properties.name: Blue Ben
properties.description: The dragon known as Blue Ben built the nearby causeway so he could reach the sea - this would keep him cool on days he felt particularly fiery. Ben met his end when he slipped on the rocks and suffocated in the mud below. His skull (actually a fossil ichthyosaur) is on display in the local museum.

properties.name: Dead Dragon
properties.description: A stone in the area marks the place where a local man named Martin killed a dragon which had eaten nine sisters who had gone to fetch water for their aging father.

properties.name: Spit Fire
properties.description: Seen flying and spitting fire, the dragon passed over the town many times during the month.

properties.name: Linton Wyrm
properties.description: This dragon was dispatched by Somerville de Lariston; the brave knight covered his lance with burning pitch, piecing the dragon's armour and cooking it from the inside.

properties.name: Dragon Duel
properties.description: A black dragon from Suffolk and a spotted red dragon from Essex met at this location. After fighting for an hour, the Essex dragon won, and both dragons disappeared back to where they came.
properties.location_name: Little Cornard (Suffolk) - Location known locally as Sharpfight Meadow, but now called Shalford Meadow

properties.name: Church Dragon
properties.description: This dragon, which made its home in the church tower, was killed by a local lad. The boy made a dummy dragon out of oak and covered it in steel spikes - the real dragon attacked the model, impaling itself on the spikes and dying of its injuries.

properties.name: Angry Gwiber
properties.description: A dragon killed many local people until they had the idea of covering a large stone with spikes and covering the structure with red cloth. The colour angered the dragon, who attacked the stone until the spikes had mortally wounded it.
properties.location_name: Llanrhaeadr ym Mochnant (Powys) - Red Pillar or Pillar of the Viper

properties.name: Warty Dragon
properties.description: This wyrm is said to have found the water from the wells so refreshing he set up home here. The creature was described as having a black tongue, long tail, warty skin, and very sharp claws. It was finally killed by Sir Guy of Warwick.

properties.name: Dragon of Aller
properties.description: The church is home to a spear that was used to kill a local dragon which lived in Athelney Fens - the creature snacked on the local livestock until a local hero bore arms against the beast.

properties.name: Dragon's Home
properties.description: A local legend says that a dragon once moved to the vault here after local people prevented the creature from entering its former home in a hill.
properties.location_name: Ludham (Norfolk) - Ruins of St Benet's Abbey

properties.name: Dragon's Den
properties.description: A Saracen arrived in the area and sought permission from the Earl to kill the dragon which was poisoning the area. The Earl agreed and the Saracen was successful in defeating the beast. What happened to the Saracen is unclear, but the Earl gained possession of the dragon's gold.

properties.name: Knuckler
properties.description: The dragon, known as Knuckler, which lived here only came out of its hole to eat the local population and their livestock. It was killed by a heroic knight who is now buried in the local church.
properties.location_name: Lyminster (Sussex) - Knucker Hole, and Arun valley (hunting ground)

properties.name: Dragon's Mine
properties.description: A winged lizard set up its lair in an old tin mine and its hissing heard for miles around. The dragon was killed in the pit, but no one appears to have wanted to take credit.

properties.name: The Mordiford Dragon
