properties.description: The name of this barrow implies it was once believed to be haunted by goblins or other fairy folk.
properties.location_name: Crossways (Dorset) - Buck Barrow, Knighton Heath Wood

properties.name: Little Workers
properties.description: A farmer met a man who said that he could complete the construction of an unfinished road out of the village if the farmer promised not to watch. The farmer agreed but sneaked a peek, spotting dozens of little men working on the road. In an instant, the little men vanished. It was said that the road could never be finished, although after a quick look on Google Maps, this author was unable to spot an unfinished road.
properties.location_name: Crowle (Lincolnshire) - Unfinished road (now likely to be finished)

properties.name: Dancing Sheppard
properties.description: A youth tending to his sheep was convinced to dance by a small group of fairies - when he did, he was transported to fairyland, but returned to the mountain once he stopped his jig.

properties.name: Tiny Soldiers
properties.description: A young shepherd spotted small, uniformed fairies dancing to a harp on the mountainside. He joined them and was instantly transported to fairyland. He was told he could eat or drink anything, but not to touch the water in a certain well. As expected, the shepherd did so, and was instantly teleported back to the mountain.

properties.name: Fairy Circle
properties.description: A child was warned to stay away from a meadow containing three rings made with grass that looked like 'weaved around the edges', as fairies had created them. Anyone foolish enough to enter the rings could never escape.
properties.location_name: Cwm (Carmarthenshire) (Dyfed) - Unknown meadow in the area

properties.name: Red Fairies
properties.description: The red haired fairies in this area lived underground and would steal cattle from neighbouring farms. It has been speculated that these fairies were actually normal bandits who have since gained mythical status.
properties.location_name: Cwm Dugoed (Gwynedd) - Woodland near the valley

properties.name: Tylwyth Teg
properties.description: The Tylwyth Teg, a race of fairies believed to be the souls of druids, are thought to reside in this valley.
properties.location_name: Cwm y Llan (Gwynedd) - General area of the valley

properties.name: Fairy Assault
properties.description: The farm owners would leave milk and bread out for the local fairy to ensure it did not create mischief. On one occasional, a servant drank the milk and eat the bread, after left water and crusts for the entity. A short time later the servant was grabbed by unseen hands and flung around, and a voice warned never to commit the offense again.
properties.location_name: Cwmcarn (was known as Abergwyddon up to 1817) (Gwent) - Trwyn Farm

properties.name: Malekin
properties.description: Malekin would converse with the family of Sir Osberni de Bradewelle, sharing people's secrets and humouring householders in the voice of a one year old child. The entity would speak in both English and Latin, and while Malekin could be heard and felt, he only appeared once; manifesting as a child dressed in a white tunic to a woman who agreed not to try and touch him. Malekin claimed that he had been born in Lavenham, removed from a field after being left there by his mother, and he had a hat which made him invisible.
properties.location_name: Dagworth (Suffolk) - The house of Sir Osberni de Bradewelle

properties.name: Pixies
properties.description: Several of these small creatures are said to loiter around the area.

properties.name: Pixie
properties.description: This wee fellow, standing forty-five centimetres in height and wearing blue and red clothing, quickly vanished once realising that he had been spotted. The area is also said to be where the Devil and his hellhounds hunt.

properties.name: Little People
properties.description: The cave was shared with the pixies by humans when hiding during the Civil War.

properties.name: Small Man in Red
properties.description: A little man dressed in red was spotted by children around the area, mysteriously vanishing from the sight of some of the witnesses while remaining visible to others. The local press at the time claimed the entity could have been an escaped monkey, although other people suspected a leprechaun.

properties.name: Swift Dancers
properties.description: Four witnesses came across a group of fifteen or sixteen strange dancers in this field. Dressed in red, the dancers waved red handkerchiefs.
properties.location_name: Denbigh (Clwyd) - Field by Lanelwyd House (likely to be no longer standing)

properties.name: Leprechaun
properties.description: A leprechaun who lived in the fort was caught by a local farmer who tried (unsuccessfully) to discover where the entity had hidden its treasure.
properties.location_name: Derryragh (County Cavan) - Fort on Derryragh hill

properties.name: Cow Protectors
properties.description: Farmer Hanafin left a cow unmilked on his land to ensure the local fairies could feed their children. When Hanafin fell into financial difficulty and the bailiffs tried to take his cattle as payment, the fairies fought back; the bailiffs were almost killed by an unseen force, and the cattle ran back to pasture.
properties.location_name: Dingle (County Kerry) - Fairy fort just outside of town

properties.name: Fiddling
properties.description: While camping in a small piece of woodland, a couple of women clearly heard fiddles being played. The following morning, they asked the rest of the camp whether anyone else had listened. No one else had heard the music, although one man said he had previously heard the music, and that fairies were responsible.
properties.location_name: Ditton Priors (Shropshire) - Between village and Clee Hills

properties.name: Brownie
properties.description: Resembling a hairy man, the brownie who worked at the farm was happy with a small bundle of hay on which he slept during the day. Unfortunately, during a harsh winter, the farmer's wife grew concerned about the brownie's wellbeing and offered him a blanket, unaware that it would be considered an insult. The brownie left.

properties.name: Farming Fairies
properties.description: The little people would visit the farm and assist in the gathering of crops. In return, farmer David Wright would leave the best fleeces for them during the sheering season. The helpfulness of the fairies ended when David died - his son took over the running of the farm, but mistreated the fairies, who in turn ensured the farm animals sickened and died. The son died soon after, and the farm eventually becoming a ruin.

properties.name: Female Spirit
properties.description: Taking the form of a spirit, but considered to be fairy in nature, a beautiful woman would occasionally manifest, formed from the mist of the well. One person who summoned the entity was grabbed by her and dragged to the bottom of the well.
properties.location_name: Dollar (Clackmannanshire) - Maiden Well, Glenqueich (north of Castle Campbell)

properties.name: Bridge Builders
properties.description: Water spirits would cross this body of water in seashells but found the method very tiresome. A team of kelpies started building a bridge made of gold and had almost finished their endeavour when a passing man blessed them on their work. The workmen vanished and the bridge collapsed, turning into a sandbank.

properties.name: Donn of the Sandhills
properties.description: Donn was said to be one of the most powerful fairy kings, but he was forgotten about as he lacked a bard to sing his praises.
properties.location_name: Dough (County Clare) - Close to the site of the old castle

properties.name: Petrified Saddle
properties.description: A vicar was left perplexed after discovering that someone was riding his horse without permission. The vicar asked around the neighbourhood but was unable to uncover the identity of the culprit. A few days after the unauthorised borrowing began, the vicar watched as a little man in a green jacket dismounted the horse and remove the saddle. The little man turned around, realised that he had been seen, and promptly turned invisible. The little man's saddle, left on a fence, turned to stone, and it was from this stone that the road took its name.

properties.name: Secret Ball
properties.description: A postman decided to have a quick nap close to a rock known to be the haunt of fairies. The rock opened and a fairy appeared, inviting the postman to a ball. The postman fled and escaped to Tobermory, chased by fairies who probably did not like taking no for an answer.
properties.location_name: Drimfin (near Tobermory) (Argyll and Bute) - Slope near a rock

properties.name: Grindylow
properties.description: Likely a different name for the entity known as Jenny Greenteeth, this fairy-like creature would wait for passing children before dragging them into the water to drown.
properties.location_name: Droylsden (Greater Manchester) - Pits in the area

properties.name: Lilac Balls of Light
properties.description: Some locals say that these balls of light, lilac in colour, are fairies; others say they are the manifestation of a lady who lost her way in a bad snowstorm in the middle ages and consequently died - she now tries to prevent her fate from happening to others.

properties.name: Taken by Fairies?
properties.description: A pedlar by the name of Scobie who once lived here was said to make his living by trading with fairies. On the evening prior to his disappearance, on neighbour spotted him and said he looked 'fay-like'. A few months later his body was discovered in a glen which was then named Scobie's Wood.

properties.name: Music
properties.description: Created by fairies, this music emerges from the cave and floats across the waters of Loch Eribol.

properties.name: Capped Dogs
properties.description: A man and companion spotted some small fairy creatures, resembling small dogs wearing red caps.
properties.date: Early nineteenth century, on a moonlit night
properties.location_name: East Baldwin (Isle of Man) - Small glen in the valley

properties.name: Colt Pixie
properties.description: An equine pixie, this fairy entity would play mild pranks and call out to horses, the distraction causing the creatures to become lost.
properties.location_name: East Boldre (Hampshire) - Cold Pixie's Cave (Bronze Age barrow), Beaulieu Heath

properties.name: Moved Church
properties.description: A church was in the process of being constructed at the base of this hill when local fairies took exception. They moved all the stones to Lewcombe, where the church was finally built.

properties.name: Albino Dwarf
properties.description: Seen by the novelist Pamela Frankau when at school here, this little creature ran across her bedroom floor and escaped via the landing, fading from view.

properties.name: Gateway
properties.description: According to one story, dating back to the Seventeenth Century, a magical fairy gateway leading to France, Holland, and the fairy kingdom opened on the hill, thought which though only those with second sight could see and use the mythical entrance. A young lad claimed to have access to the gate and would act as a drummer to the many fairies who used the portal. The lad claimed to be needed on a certain date by the little people, and despite all physical efforts to force him to miss the meeting, the lad slipped away from his wardens and was never seen again.

properties.name: Centaur
properties.description: Robert Ogilvie Crombie claimed to have encountered several mystical creatures here, including a centaur, a faun named Kurmos, and Pan.

properties.name: Vanishing Piper
properties.description: Believing the fairies within the mound would teach him new tunes, a piper entered to seek enlightenment. He was never seen again.
properties.location_name: Ednam (Borders) - Nearby mound known as the Piper's Grave (aka Pict's Knowe)

properties.name: Butter Flingers
properties.description: The local elves were said to create mischief by throwing their freshly made butter at doors and gates.

properties.name: Hobthrush
properties.description: Hobthrush, a brownie-like entity, tirelessly worked here until, after someone spotted his hat was a bit worse for wear, made him a new one. Offended, Hobthrush left and never returned.

properties.name: Brown Man
properties.description: A stocky dwarf like figure, the brown man looks after the local wildlife, and has been known to kill anyone who persistently harms the natural balance.

properties.name: Awfshots
properties.description: Awfshots, flint arrowheads used by ancient people, were traditionally believed to have been used by fairies. Farmers would touch their cattle with the arrowheads for protection. Emily Bronte refers to the objects in her work.
properties.location_name: Esk Valley (North Yorkshire) - General area, including Goathland and Danby

properties.name: Brownie
properties.description: This woodland was the home of a brownie, although little more is known about the creature.

properties.name: Shellycoat
properties.description: Shellycoat was the name given to a playful water spirit which dressed in a shell covered jacket. He normally carried out his antics while hiding in the river, trying to lure the gullible forever closer to the cold waters.

properties.name: Fairy Abduction
properties.description: The lady of the house was taken away by a strange man dressed in green, who appeared from nowhere. A few years later a witness reported seeing her chained at the bottom of a local cavern, though no one was able to climb down and release her. The house also may be haunted - one story told was of a group of fishermen who fled the house one night after encountering a ghost.

properties.name: Fairy Fort
properties.description: This Iron Age Hillfort was constructed by fairies to protect themselves from earth spirits.

properties.name: Harassed by Fairies
properties.description: Several abandoned watermills on the island were associated with myths, the most prevalent one being that fairies had driven the owners away.
properties.location_name: Fetlar (Shetland) - Unnamed disused watermills on the island, unlikely to remain

properties.name: Helpful Horse
properties.description: This boggart took the form of a horse. The entity would plough fields and work around the farm in return for a warm place to spend the night (or a pie, depending on the story).

properties.name: Tiny Horses
properties.description: Author Herbie Brennan and a friend visited this site late at night and spotted several small white horses galloping along the earthwork. He described them as being no taller than a cocker spaniel and around 25 in number.

properties.name: Little Folk
properties.description: The area around the lake is reportedly home to boggarts and hobs. There are also stories of ghostly Roundheads moving around the area, some on horseback and others wearily walking holding pikes.

properties.name: Fairy Stone
properties.description: The village is said to have taken its name from four stones, either holy or Roman in nature, which marked the four corners of the village. One of the stones was said to act as a message drop between fairies and had a small recess in which small pieces of paper could be left.
properties.location_name: Fourstones (Northumberland) - Exact area no longer known, was on the south edge of the village

properties.name: Cauldron
properties.description: The cauldron in the church is said to have been taken from fairies that once lived nearby, though other accounts say it belonged to a white witch. Earthen mounds near the pond are thought to have been created by the Devil and are unlucky to disturb.

properties.name: Water Fairies
properties.description: Once a clay pit, the hole was left to fill with water. Some say that it has become bottomless, and that fairy folk live within the water waiting to drown those who are too curious. Others say it the Devil who pushes people in.

properties.name: Lavina
properties.description: Lavina, a female elf, can be heard singing on the site at sunrise.

properties.name: Witches
properties.description: A piece of local folklore says, 'There will always be nine witches from the bottom of Orcop to the top of Garway, as long as water flows'. Depending on the story, the witches either helped a man recover his wife who had been kidnapped by fairies, or had brought her back from the dead.

properties.name: Hay Maker
properties.description: A dab hand around the farm, this hob left when (like so many of his other helpful friends throughout Yorkshire) the owners tried to give him new clothing to replace the rags that he wore.

properties.name: Cliodhna
properties.description: Cliodhna, the Banshee Queen, met Keevan of the Curling Locks during a feast at Tir Tairngire. She fell in love with the mortal and they escaped in a ship, landing in Glandore. Keevan set off inland to hunt, leaving Cliodhna to sleep on the beach. Depending on the storyteller, Cliodhna either drowned while sleeping or fairies tracked her down and returned the queen to Tir Tairngire. Cliodhna is still heard when the conditions are right, a deep and hollow roar emerging from the caves along the cliffs of Glandore.

properties.name: Fairy King
properties.description: Local legend has it that St Collen visited the head of the fairy royal family on this hill. A woman, named by some to be Queen Guinevere, is also said to manifest over the Tor, and possesses golden hair that illuminates the area.

properties.name: Falin
properties.description: This demonic creature, whose head is twice the size of its body, is so evil that anything which crosses an area where it has walked is doomed to die soon after.

properties.name: Brownie
properties.description: A Brownie left this residence in disgust after the homeowner left some new clothes out for the entity to wear.
properties.location_name: Glen Devon (Perth and Kinross) - Unknown residence

properties.name: Urisk
properties.description: Evidence that shows this part fairy, part human hybrid still to be in the area occasionally turns up. A barrow known locally as the 'Mound of the Dead' is thought to be an entrance to Hell.

properties.name: Goblin
properties.description: This pesky little fellow his said to be half as high as the average person, and twice as vindictive to any trespassers caught on 'his' land.

properties.name: Wife Seeker
properties.description: The home of a beautiful male water spirit, the creature sometimes emerges from the pool looking for a young maiden to kidnap and take as his bride.
properties.location_name: Glen Roy (Isle of Man) - Nikkesen's Pool

properties.name: Mother Stealing Fairies
properties.description: The population of little people located here were constantly trying to kidnap mothers who had recently given birth, to act as nannies for their own fairy children.

properties.name: Sewing Fairies
properties.description: The tailor who owned this shop left the mayor's clothing unfinished on Saturday and found it completed on the Monday morning. He used this event to advertise the shop, claiming that the little folk had done the work, though it soon transpired that an assistant had worked on the Sunday to avoid going to church.
properties.location_name: Gloucester (Gloucestershire) - Exact location unknown, old tailor's shop

properties.name: Fairies
properties.description: A letter in the press mentioned that six people had seen fairies in the area.

properties.name: Tom's Fairies
properties.description: Artist Tom Charman took inspiration from the fairies and elementals that he encountered in the forest. He described the entities as ranging from a few inches to two and a half feet (5cm - 76cm) in hight, and they would be smartly dressed. The fairies could create items by simply thinking about them and also created self-portraits using stone.
properties.location_name: Godshill (Hampshire) - The Ridge, and general area of the New Forest

properties.name: Cro Sith
properties.description: Dun and hornless fairy cattle were once thought to live along the coast of Bernera and under the nearby waters.

properties.name: Hyter Sprites
properties.description: Hyter Sprites were said to be a beneficent type of fairy, though their homes were avoided after dusk. Hyter Sprites were once believed to live in this part of Great Melton.
properties.location_name: Great Melton (Norfolk) - Area once known as Blow Hill, aka Coldblow Hill

properties.name: Army of Small People
properties.description: The fairies here are said to ensure that no one ever builds a roof on the nearby St Trinian's Church. A witness watched a long line of little people dancing down the road, coming from the direction of the church.
properties.location_name: Greeba (Isle of Man) - Greeba Castle, road outside of, & St Trinian's Church

properties.name: Raura Peens
properties.description: Raura Peens, the last fairy of the area, may have appeared to a local man at these stones during the nineteenth century. The fairy tried to lure the witness to her realm (or at least a cave under the area) but failed. Another story said the stones were once home to a giant named Heywood.

properties.name: Dancing Folk
properties.description: Knee high fairies were once said to use the steps as a stage, dancing along them between sunset and sunrise.

properties.name: Fairy Holes
properties.description: The marks found along the channel within the burn were said to have been created by the little people.
properties.location_name: Gunnerton (Northamptonshire) - Burn on north side of village

properties.name: Pixie Place
properties.description: The hill is still reportedly a home for fairies and other little folk, whose music can be heard as it drifts around the area.

properties.name: Fresh Flowers on Grave
properties.description: Like many nineteenth century suicides, Mary 'Kitty' Jay was buried at a crossroads. Fresh flowers are reported to be found at the grave, though no one has ever seen them placed, leading some to believe the site is tended by fairies. A floating female figure is also reported to have been seen by the graveside, as has a man draped in a dark blanket.
properties.location_name: Haytor (Devon) - Road between Heatree Cross and Hound Tor Inn

properties.name: Hyter Sprite
properties.description: Said to resemble little people, this area was another home to the fairies known as Hyter Sprites.
properties.location_name: Hempnall (Norfolk) - Area around Hempnall and Topcroft

properties.name: Ringing Road
properties.description: A dip in the road would ring whenever a cart moved across it. Two men excavated the spot and discovered a secret doorway. They decided to stop for dinner, but as they left the spot, a storm blew up and filled the hole.

properties.name: Tiny Pilot
properties.description: Either an early UFO or fairy sighting (or maybe both), a brother and sister watched a thirty centimetre long biplane land nearby and a tiny occupant dressed in flying gear waved at them. The aircraft then took off.

properties.name: Poached Faries
properties.description: A local folktale says two poachers fled the area after finding their sack filled with fairies they had unintentionally caught instead of rabbits.

properties.name: Fairy Tunnels
properties.description: The rabbits and fairies in this village are said to live side by side in the warrens.

properties.name: Ruined Twice
properties.description: Fairies advised the people building a church here to place it at the top of the hill and not at the bottom. The builders ignored the advice and twice found their church in ruins just before completion. They moved their third attempt to the top of the hill, where it remains.
properties.location_name: Holme on the Wolds (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Church

properties.name: Kindly Fairies
properties.description: A woman who lived at the farm returned from her daughter's funeral to find her home had been tidied and a fire burning in the hearth. The woman believed that the little folk had been at work.

properties.name: Boggart
properties.description: Two people were attacked within weeks of each other by a creature they reported as being ice cold, hairy, with glowing eyes. The area is said to have reports going back hundreds of years of a Boggart creeping around, trying to kill the unwary. This creature should not be confused with the white shuck-like dog, which also haunts the neighbourhood.
properties.location_name: Horbury (West Yorkshire) - General area around the village

properties.name: Short Fellow
properties.description: Sabine Baring-Gould's wife encountered a two foot (sixty centimetre) tall figure dressed in green sitting on a hedge. The woman ran away after the figure began to make strange faces at her.

properties.name: Angry Little Men
properties.description: Seen by a witness throwing themselves off the cliff edge in a mad dance, the witness said that these small men looked 'primitive', with long dark hair.

properties.name: Boggart
properties.description: A nature spirit has been reported haunting the hall.

properties.name: Home
properties.description: This large piece of granitic rock was once believed to be the home to fairies. A nineteenth century owner of the land removed part of the stone so his plough could pass over it, but after which his fortune vanished, and he died in a poorhouse.

properties.name: Broken Staff
properties.description: A farmer ploughing the field heard a fairy cry 'I've broken my staff!'. The farmer offered to fix the staff, and having done so, found bread and butter left out for him the following day.

properties.name: Hob Headless
properties.description: This brownie-related entity lived along the road until exorcised. He was cast under a stone where he had to remain for ninety-nine years and a day. It was said if anyone sat on the stone, they would never be able to stand again. Needless to say, the ninety-nine years has now expired but the entity appears still to be quiet.
properties.location_name: Hurworth-on-Teas (Durham) - Road between village and Neasham

properties.name: Jeanie
properties.description: A farmer was dared to approach the home of the fairy known as Jeanie. Sitting upon his horse, the farmer called out to her. Somewhat displeased at the interruption, she called back and chased him away. As the farmer crossed a stream to safety, she cut his horse in two.

properties.name: Hound Eater
properties.description: Renowned for its dislike of canines (it is blamed for tearing apart stray local hounds), this Cu Sith (sometimes described as a human-wolf hybrid for dramatic effect) has been seen during both day and night.
properties.location_name: Hynish, Tiree (Argyll and Bute) - General area

properties.name: Agile Green Folk
properties.description: Described as fast moving little folk in green, the fairies that lived in this area were once found in the single village bath - they scarpered when discovered.
properties.location_name: Ilkley (West Yorkshire) - Area near the well

properties.name: Red Capped Fairies
properties.description: A group of fairies dancing around in the moonlight ran away after being told they should talk to a priest to have their question of 'will they go to heaven' answered.
properties.location_name: Inchigeela (County Cork) - Close to a stream?

properties.name: Singing
properties.description: Fairy song was said to have been heard coming from this fort.

properties.name: Lile Hob of Blea Moor
properties.description: This pixie-like creature would loiter on the roadside and leap upon passing carts to blag a free ride. The Hob vanished when a shepherd stumbled upon three little silver armbands left in a field, one of which he lost and the others he sold.
properties.location_name: Ingleton (North Yorkshire) - Road between Ingleton and Hawes

properties.name: Fiddler in a Timeslip
properties.description: One story told here is of a fiddler who fell asleep on the hill. He awoke in fairyland where he had to play for the queen. After several hours he was permitted to leave and was returned to an area near the River Ness. The fiddler soon discovered that one hundred years had passed since entering the fairy kingdom.

properties.name: The Call
properties.description: A strange spell is occasionally cast upon mortals by the fairy folk that causes them to believe they must leave the island, be it by land, sea or death.
properties.location_name: Iona, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - General area

properties.name: Marie Emily Fornario
properties.description: Ghost lights are seen hovering around the grave of Marie, and her sad figure has been reported moving around moorland in the region.
properties.location_name: Iona, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - Reilig Odhrain

properties.name: Fairy Music
properties.description: From this grassy knoll sweet music has been heard by both locals and visitors to the area.
properties.location_name: Iona, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - Sithean Mor - Fairy Mound

properties.name: Green Woman
properties.description: A green-clad fairy woman lived on the island and would be left milk and food by the locals.
properties.location_name: Iona, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - Unidentified cave on island

properties.name: Brownie
properties.description: A brownie took a liking to the MacDougalls and, in return for a small amount of warm milk which is left for him in a hollow in a cave, would keep an eye on the cattle which grazed on the island. If his gift were neglected, a cow would be found stranded (or dead) on one of the rocky outcrops.
properties.location_name: Isle of Insh (Argyll and Bute) - General area

properties.name: Fairy Ship
properties.description: Two children on the beach suddenly found a boy dressed in green standing beside them. The boy pointed to his ship a few feet from shore, which contained a small girl and a tiny dog. After the green people shared some small biscuits, they set sail, saying that others of their kind were coming.

properties.name: How do they Smell?
properties.description: The little people on this island are renowned for only having one nostril.
properties.location_name: Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - General area

properties.name: Fairy Hill
properties.description: It was said that if any milk was spilt close to the hill, the fairies reap the benefit.
properties.location_name: Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - Small hill near Duart Castle

properties.name: Gruagach
properties.description: Not much is known about the hairy fairy that once made its home on this island
properties.location_name: Isle of Skye (Highland) - Small island east of Skye, was known as Troda?

properties.name: Dancing Dwarfs
properties.description: The little people here made their homes in the Roman ruins scattered around.

properties.name: Fairy Music
properties.description: Two children, a sister and brother, were hunting for birds in the snow. Music that emerged from deep underground terrified the pair; they ran home, the girl losing her shoes in her haste to escape.
properties.location_name: Kennavarra, Tiree (Argyll and Bute) - Kennavarra Hill

properties.name: Midnight Visitors
properties.description: A witness watched as a group of fairies entered this farmhouse at midnight. The fairies silently glided around a room and left again.

properties.name: Changeling
properties.description: A fairy changeling took up residence in Gylen Castle, escaping into the sea when identified by an Irish visitor.

properties.name: Clothes Washing
properties.description: Reports of fairies frequenting this area date from the seventeenth century, with three men and one woman claiming to have stumbled upon the fairies making merry at midnight. One twentieth century author wrote that the little folk washed their clothes at the site, their wooden rollers making so much noise that they could be heard a mile away.

properties.name: Shining Cup
properties.description: The castle was home to a shining cup, stolen from a fairy party by a serving boy. The cup would fill with whatever liquid the bearer wished. The magical item was lost after a boat sunk while carrying it.

properties.name: Bodach Beag Chill-a-Chreunain
properties.description: A fairy-like being, the Bodach Beag Chill-a-Chreunain took the form of a little man surrounded by shadow.
properties.location_name: Kilchrenan (Argyll and Bute) - High road to Nant Woods

properties.name: Mushroom Givers
properties.description: A witness who watched a troupe of fairies dancing a circle returned the following morning to find a ring of mushrooms in the same place, given to him for not disturbing their dance.

properties.name: Fairies in Retreat
properties.description: Locals say that when the fairies were last seen here, they shouted out 'Hey for Ireland!'.
properties.location_name: Kirk Bride (Isle of Man) - Lough Goayr

properties.name: King's Home
properties.description: The King of the pixies once lived in this area, but the ringing of the church bells drove him away.

properties.name: Fairy Music
properties.description: The finest music in all Scottish fairydom can be heard here.
properties.location_name: Kyle of Lochalsh (Highland) - Clann Mhic Crumein

properties.name: Donn Dumhach
properties.description: The fairy king known as Donn Dumhach was once believed to live amongst the sandhills.
properties.location_name: Lahinch (County Clare) - Sandhills around Dough Castle

properties.name: Fairy Fairs
properties.description: Only visible from the sea, flickering lights on the cliff side indicate where the little people are celebrating and partying.

properties.name: Boggles
properties.description: These northern fairies would play merry havoc near the rivers, frightening horses and riders alike.

properties.name: Tron Tree
properties.description: The Tron Tree stood in the centre of the community and was a favourite haunt to the elves, until chopped down by a local man. The man's sow was found dead the following day.
properties.location_name: Largs (Ayrshire) - Tree in town centre (tree no longer standing)

properties.name: Water Nymph
properties.description: While on holiday, one lady observed a pale skinned, red headed woman moving across the park, although the way the entity's green and blue clothing flowed, appeared to be moving through water. The woman smiled before vanishing.
properties.location_name: Laugharne (Dyfed) - Laugharne Park, close to the estuary

properties.name: Gnomes
properties.description: For several weeks, explosions were heard in the Highfields area, although no source could be found. One man, rushing to investigate a loud bang, spotted several, grotesque gnome-like entities dematerialising.

properties.name: Gold Guardians
properties.description: This hill was formed when a giant buried his treasure, leaving a troupe of warrior fairies to protect it. They have been called into action at least once, appearing as a would-be treasure hunter arrived armed with pick and shovel.

properties.name: Little Folk
properties.description: The second highest peak in Dorset is said to be home to an extended family of fairies. In recent years, there have been reports of a large black cat living in the area. In the late 1970s, a woman and her two children all witnessed a headless horseman emerging from a fog bank.
properties.date: Cat and fairies unknown, headless horseman during late 1970s

properties.name: Fairy Riders
properties.description: Beneath the castle a company of fairies on horseback wait for the day they are needed. The site is also reputed to be home to a phantom woman who wanders around carrying a candle.

properties.name: Water Sprite
properties.description: This malicious little entity is reported to lure the unwary into the water to drown.

properties.name: Boy playing Flute
properties.description: This figure was seen and heard on the lanes, in trees and behind bushes in this village - the music he produced described as enchanting, but with an underlying danger. The same area is haunted by a pale white calf.

properties.name: Abstinent Fairies
properties.description: Fairies told a drunken blacksmith if he ceased his drinking, he would benefit. The smith sobered up, and a few months later, was visited by a strange man who needed new shoes for his horse. After being tied to the smith's building, the horse bolted, pulling down masonry as it fled. The horse's owner also vanished. While repairing the damage, the blacksmith discovered three brass kettles filled with money.

properties.name: Home
properties.description: The hill, another home to fairies, also claimed to house the grave of a giant - anyone who laid within the hill would be granted incredible strength.

properties.name: Relocated
properties.description: The church was supposed to have been built another field, but other entities had different ideas; every night the previous day's building progress was deconstructed and relocated to the site where the church now stands.

properties.name: Invisible Hands
properties.description: The local landlord, Richard the Tailor, was said to keep the company of fairies. A hunter who upset Richard found himself whisked away from the village by unseen hands which pushed and pulled the man until he reached Newport. Although exhausted, the invisible force then motivated him to walk the twenty miles back to the village.

properties.name: Fiddling Fairies
properties.description: An innocent shepherd here was caught up in a fairy dance - he could not stop 'strutting his funky stuff' until the following day, when his master found him in a field. The location is also a contender for the resting place of the Holy Grail.

properties.name: Dancing Fairies
properties.description: Witnesses here watched a troupe of fairies dancing in the fields behind the village church. Once the merriment ceased, the fairies headed off towards the wood.

properties.name: Dancing Fairies
properties.description: While lying in this barn, John Rosser heard music and watched a company of fairies enter and dance around. The fairies danced until morning and then vanished.
properties.location_name: Llanhilleth (Gwent) - Barn named Ysgubor y Llan (likely no longer standing)

properties.name: Fairy Funeral
properties.description: Not only did Isaac William Thomas witness a fairy funeral, but he also reached across the bier and removed the veil which he kept. This fine fairy cloth was supposed to be very light and could be folded down to next to nothing.

properties.name: Visited Fairyland
properties.description: James Davies (aka Iago ap Dewi) was said to have disappeared for seven years, during which he visited the fairy kingdom, although he never admitted it.
properties.location_name: Llanllawddog (Dyfed) - Cottage in Llangwyly (woodland), unlikely to be still standing

properties.name: Dancing Place of the Goblin
properties.description: Under a certain yew-tree in the centre of the woodland a fairy circle was known as 'The Dancing Place of the Goblin'. Two lads once took a nap in the circle, and upon awaking, one had vanished. The missing lad reappeared a year later - he was grabbed out of the clutches of dancing goblins and saved.
properties.location_name: Llanwrin (Powys) - Tree in woodland near Mathafarn (woodland may no longer exists)

properties.name: Entrance
properties.description: Locals say that the hill contains a doorway into the fairy realm. A blind fiddler once accidently wandered in, and his fiddle could be heard playing deep beneath the village.

properties.name: Fairy Cattle
properties.description: Said to be a fairy lake, one farmer who found a young bull on the edge of the water and took it home had his entire herd taken away from him - a little man came out from the lake and led the animals into its water.

properties.name: Bull
properties.description: The waters are home to a large bull that breathes fire from its nose, though others say the creature is more 'Loch Ness Monster' in nature.

properties.name: One Good Turn
properties.description: A shepherd who would spend the summer months in this cabin gave his shirt to a woman, so she had something to wrap around her baby. The following morning, and every morning from that day on, he found a piece of silver left in an old clog in the cabin.
properties.location_name: Llyn Glas (Gwynedd) - Cabin by the lake

properties.name: Fairy Bride
properties.description: A fairy married a mortal man with her father's blessing, on the condition that the man would never let iron touch her flesh. They lived together for many years, and had several children, but one day her knee was struck by a stirrup and she vanished back to the fairy realm. While she was not allowed to walk human lands again, she was permitted to float on the lake while balanced on a piece of turf, which she did to continue to talk to her husband.
properties.location_name: Llyn y Dywarchen (Gwynedd) - Lake and a farm close by (likely no longer present)

properties.name: Porridge Eaters
properties.description: A shepherd who used the bothy made tiny bowls and spoons for the fairies who also lived here, having found his own porridge bowl pecked at. The fairies moved away after they brought a guest that the shepherd did not feed.
properties.location_name: Loch Ericht (Perth and Kinross) - Bothy at the southern end of the loch

properties.name: Husband
properties.description: A widow was told that her late husband was working as a 'baggage horse' for the fairies within this cairn.
properties.location_name: Loch Errochty (Perth and Kinross) - Mound known as Cam na Sleabkack (aka Slevach Cairn) (may no longer exist)

properties.name: Bridge
properties.description: Fairies were in the process of building a bridge across the loch when a traveller wished them 'God speed'. The fairies stopped working and left their bridge unfinished.

properties.name: Frost
properties.description: A traveller passing through the area heard two Urisk calling out to each other, one shouting 'Frost', the other responding 'Kick frost'.
properties.location_name: Loch Sloy (Argyll and Bute) - Glen located south of the loch

properties.name: Water Cows
properties.description: Fairy cattle would constantly graze both on the land surrounding the loch, and in the still waters. Another legend suggests it was home to 'fish without fins', interpreted by some as serpentine monsters. An Each Uisge once lived here but was driven out and moved to Loch Earn instead.
properties.location_name: Loch Tay (Perth and Kinross) - General area

properties.name: Mary's Urisk
properties.description: A voyeuristic Urisk would visit this area and watch a lady named Mary as she worked her spinning wheel. A man who wanted to see the creature for himself donned Mary's clothing but failed to fool the Urisk (that declared 'I see your beard').

properties.name: Bean-nighe
properties.description: The Bean-nighe is a Scottish spirit, an ill bringer who would be seen washing the clothes of those soon to die. This creature has long breasts, slung over her shoulders, and if she were grabbed from behind, the Bean-nighe would say whom the clothes belonged; if yours, she could change fate. The owner of the castle, upon seeing the creature, tried to grab her but failed - he died in battle the following day. As if this entity were not enough to contend with, the area is haunted by a headless horseman.
properties.location_name: Lochbuie, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - River by Moy Castle

properties.name: Dancing Fairies
properties.description: The King Stone was once a place where fairies danced around. Legend says that the monolith was once a King who was petrified by a witch (though which ruler is unclear). There are so many other smaller stones that it is said to be impossible to count them all. Another set of nearby standing stones known as the Whispering Knights are thought to be the witch's aids, who conspired against the king but were turned to stone anyway. Finally, a grey female figure would appear on misty days, twisting and moving strangely within the stone circle.
properties.date: Weather Dependent: Mist. Grey figure seen pre-1937
properties.location_name: Long Compton (actually in Warwickshire) (Oxfordshire) - The Rollright Stones - the King Stone and the Whispering Knights

properties.name: Blinded
properties.description: A Netherwitton man was charged with raising a fairy child and given an ointment with which he was to wash the child's eyes. Curiosity overtook the man and he washed one of his own eyes with the ointment. While visiting the Longhorsley fair, the man spotted the fairy child's parents and greeted them. Surprised to have been seen by the man, the fairies asked which eye could see them. The man pointed to the eye and one of the fairies blew into it, permanently blinding him.

properties.name: Dancing Fairies
properties.description: A group of children encountered between eight and ten fairies dancing to music. One of the little people charged towards the children, striking one girl with a plant - the children ran home, and the girl who was hit later collapsed, only waking when a priest visited her.
properties.location_name: Lough Derg (County Donegal) - Exact location not known

properties.name: Submerged City
properties.description: Humanity struck a deal with the fairies - they could build a city here, on the condition the nearby earth forts were left untouched. Humanity grew greedy and built upon the forts, at which point the fairies flooded the city, creating the lough.

properties.name: Vicious Little Folk
properties.description: The well in the village is reported to be protected by violent fairies, which are also capable of summoning storms.

properties.name: Cave Dwellers
properties.description: The fairy folk here would hide in a cave close by. They were a helpful sort and rewarded any mortal who assisted them in repairs or tracking down lost items.
properties.location_name: Lulsley (Warwickshire) - Osebury Rock (aka Oseberrow Rock, aka Rosebury Rock)

properties.name: Fairies
properties.description: The site of an Iron Age hill fort was said to be the haunt of fairies.

properties.name: Protective Fairy
properties.description: Locals knew the good fairy which lived here would cure the sick. It was also known to go on missions to locate and retrieve children stolen by other fairies with lesser morals. The stones found on the site are believed to have curative properties.

properties.name: Fairy Tree
properties.description: A knarred and knotted tree was thought to be home to a group of fairies from Ireland, and ill luck fell upon anyone who picked up deadwood from under it.
properties.location_name: Maesteg (Mid Glamorgan) - Exact location not known

properties.name: Rollicking Bill
properties.description: This fairy fellow has been seen skipping over the marsh at twilight.

properties.name: Ring
properties.description: When certain, unknown, conditions are met, a fairy ring in this area is reputed to become active. If anyone steps into it during this period, they vanish to an unknown place and never return.
properties.location_name: Malew (Isle of Man) - Ronaldsway, exact area not known

properties.name: Janet
properties.description: Janet, or sometimes Jennet, was a fairy queen who inhabited a cave next to the waterfall found here.
properties.location_name: Malham (North Yorkshire) - Janet's Foss, Gordale Beck (aka Gordale Scar)

properties.name: Small Pipes
properties.description: Two small clay pipes are reported to have been discovered here, thought to have been used by fairies and the local pixie folk.
properties.location_name: Mam Tor (Derbyshire) - General area of the barrows

properties.name: Jenny Greenteeth
properties.description: Parents would warn their children to stay away from the drain, otherwise Jenny Greenteeth would emerge and drown them.
properties.location_name: Manchester (Greater Manchester) - Open drain at Shooters Brook (no longer present), Store Street

properties.name: Reconstructing Building
properties.description: It is said that the church here was once pulled down to make way for a new building - after the last brick was removed and night fell, the church rebuilt itself; the builders took the hint, and the church has remained standing ever since. It was said that the hobman, a type of fairy, was responsible.
properties.location_name: Marske by the Sea (North Yorkshire) - Church

properties.name: Glowing Lights
properties.description: Sharing a bedroom with his brother, this witness awoke to see several small bright lights enter the room and dance around before landing on his brother's bed. The lights changed to small people who walked to the head of the bed. They were all dressed in very colourful clothes, singing and playing musical instruments. They finally disappeared the way they had entered.

properties.name: Liannanshee
properties.description: A phantom woman, said to be fairy in nature, would be seen washing clothing in this river, while holding a candle in one hand.
properties.date: Weather Dependent: Prior to bad conditions

properties.name: Secret Meeting
properties.description: The little people are reported to gather here once a year for secret planning sessions.

properties.name: Hunters
properties.description: The fairies that frequent this remote area dress in green and often carry hunting weapons - the last one seen possessed a whip. It was once said that under the site was a lost city which sunk for some unnamed reason - church bells could be heard on still nights.

properties.name: Fairy Queen
properties.description: The fairy queen kidnapped the Scottish hero Thomas the Rhymer in this area, returning him from the fairy kingdom in the same place seven (or three) years later. Thomas is reported to still live under the hills, preparing for the day his country needs him once more. The latter part of the same tale is also said about King Arthur.

properties.name: Elf Home
properties.description: As with Beaumont Water near Cheviot Hills, this small body of water was once thought to be the home of the fairies, the circular pebbles within considered elf plates.
properties.location_name: Melrose (Borders) - Elwin stream (aka Allan stream) which feeds into the Tweed

properties.name: Fairies
properties.description: It is believed that the hill is frequented by fairies, which probably live beneath it.

properties.name: Lamenting Woman
properties.description: Driven from her land by the onset of farming, a fairy maiden would return on some nights, crying over the land which once belonged to her and her people.

properties.name: Hooting Goblin
properties.description: Recognised by his extremely loud call, normally heard around midnight, this fellow has never been seen.

properties.name: Lost Child
properties.description: It was at the nearby river that a woman once found a fairy child that she took home. The little creature, with red eyes, cried constantly, and would not stop until returned to the place it was found. If your ear is placed by the ground, you can hear fairy music within the hill.

properties.name: People in Green
properties.description: Tiny people dressed in green would be briefly seen by locals visiting the site during the summer months. If alone. to catch a glance of the figures would be considered lucky, but if in a larger party, any witnesses to the fairies would be struck with ill-fortune. When the hill was being cut by railway engineers, the banks would collapse at night. Fairies were blamed for the disruption and navvies requested additional money in dealing with the paranormal problem (which was granted).

properties.name: Pitchfork Hurlers
properties.description: The fairies found here are full of mischief; the critters once chased a visitor to the village, who managed to seek shelter within the grange just before a pitchfork embedded itself in the door.
properties.location_name: Middridge (Durham) - Middridge Grange and surrounding area

properties.name: Fairy Islands
properties.description: Green fairy islands could be seen off the coast here, thought to be heavily populated and connected to the mainland via a secret undersea network.

properties.name: The Hob
properties.description: Like many of his siblings, this hob helped keep the area clean and tidy. The creature left when a man offered him a hooded cloak to wear during winter.

properties.name: Changeling
properties.description: A changeling child was whipped and left at a crossroads after being substituted for a healthy human baby.
properties.location_name: Minane (County Cork) - Exact location not known

properties.name: Gifts
properties.description: The well took its name from the cheese which travellers would leave for the resident fairies.

properties.name: Cat People
properties.description: It was said around this time that several little people climbed the trees of the forest, possessing catlike attributes for better balance.
properties.location_name: Minstead (Hampshire) - General area in the New Forest

properties.name: Glowing Light
properties.description: The eight hundred year old tree known as Old Knobbley is supposed to have been a refuge for witches hiding from Matthew Hopkins. In 2022, a glowing ball of light was recorded as it flew around a person swinging on a tree rope.

properties.name: Hard Working Brownie
properties.description: A helpful brownie would help with the harvest each year, much to the delight to the farmer. However, when he finally decided to reward the entity with milk and bread, the creature left him.

properties.name: Land of Ever Day
properties.description: While following the sound of music, a fisherman ran his boat ashore in the isle of fairies. After conversing with the tiny islanders (who sang their home was Ever Day) and partaking of their food, the fisherman tried to escape with pockets full of fairy gold; the fairies beat him up and left the man on the shore of Morecambe.

properties.name: Helpful Folk
properties.description: Once upon a time, the stream dried up and the lack of water caused hardship to local farmers. Farmers who had been respectful to the fairies were helped by them - their crops grew and animals flourished.

properties.name: John Kelleher's Leprechaun
properties.description: John Kelleher captured a leprechaun under a rock on the mountain and agreed to let the creature go on the condition that it gave him a purse of coins. The little man told Kelleher that if he dug where he stood, he would find the purse. Kelleher let the leprechaun go, stuck a stick in the ground to mark the position and went home to find a shovel. Returning a short time later, the man found hundreds of identical sticks all stuck in the earth around the area.
properties.location_name: Mushera (mountain) (County Cork) - Rock near the summit

properties.name: Fairy Forts
properties.description: A member of parliament made headlines after he declared a dip in the road, which had cost forty thousand Euros to repair, had been caused by fairies who lived in forts which the road navigated around.
properties.location_name: N22 (County Kerry) - Dip in road close to Kerry Way, Curraglass

properties.name: Fairy Home
properties.description: The hill was said to be a major home for fairies - they could be heard inside, and several times there have been reports of the little people entering or leaving the place.
properties.location_name: Nafferton Slack (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Hill

properties.name: Floating Island
properties.description: An island once floated around this body of water, acting as a meeting place between a fairy and her human husband, after she was prevented by other members of her race to walk on land.
properties.location_name: Nantlle Valley (Gwynedd) - Lake of the Turf Sod

properties.name: Fairy Tree
properties.description: The two trees here belong to the fairies - damaging them, or even just taking a clipping, would result in bad luck befalling the perpetrator.
properties.location_name: Navan (County Meath) - Two hawthorn trees on the Hill of Tara

properties.name: Partying Fairies
properties.description: Local man Davy Hanlon met a troupe of fairies upon this bridge. They whisked him away to a distant wine cellar where Davy drank with the little people. They allowed him to keep a silver goblet and returned him home, although he became bedridden for many months after his encounter.
properties.location_name: New Ross (County Wexford) - Maudlin's Bridge area

properties.name: Elf Meet
properties.description: The site was also home to a mighty oak tree in which fairies lived. When a storm damaged the tree, a fallen branch was used by locals as firewood, although those who burnt the wood had their homes set alight by the upset fairies.
properties.location_name: Newcastle (Gwent) - Oak tree on former site of castle

properties.name: Fairy Court
properties.description: This hill was once believed to be the setting for the fairy court.
properties.location_name: Newcastle upon Tyne (Tyne and Wear) - Small grassy mounds on Fawdon Hill (area now likely built over)

properties.name: Not a Banshee?
properties.description: Residents who lived close to the lough reported hearing the wail of a banshee which lasted for several nights. When the cries ceased and no one died, the locals doubted whether the sounds came from a banshee... A little man in green was also spotted in the area. Matching the description of a leprechaun, a witness tried to grab him for his gold, but the little man vanished.
properties.location_name: Newmarket on Fergus (County Clare) - Lough Gaish

properties.name: Summoning the Fairies
properties.description: Running around this barrow nine times is said to bring forth the fairies.
properties.location_name: Newport Pagnell (Buckinghamshire) - Barrow in the area

properties.name: Wild Edric
properties.description: A county hero, Wild Edric is said to emerge from the lead mines where he lives and charge off towards England's enemies when required. Once flesh and blood, it is thought his fairy wife may have had a hand in making him and his troops immortal.

properties.name: Small Trolls
properties.description: This cairn was used by trows who would only leave it by moonlight to go hunting.

properties.name: Sixty Gnomes in Cars
properties.description: Appearing to several children, these friendly gnomes sat two per car and playfully chased the children around the park. The wee fellows looked 'like Noddy', but with white beards. The cars moved without sound.

properties.name: Glaistig
properties.description: The Glaistig is a fairy-like guardian entity that follows a family, only becoming apparent when good or bad news is heard (that is when she bursts into song, cheers, or cries). Dunstaffgne's Glaistig would also be heard stomping around and on one occasion, pulled the sheets off a guest's bed. The creature has not been heard since the castle burnt down.
properties.location_name: Oban (Argyll and Bute) - Dunstaffgne (or Dunstaffnage) Castle

properties.name: Dancing Girls
properties.description: Two men out for a walk reported seeing a score of small child-like figures dancing on the slopes of the hill. The figures danced in a perfect circle while holding their joined hands in the air before suddenly vanishing.
properties.location_name: Okeford Fitzpaine (Dorset) - Okeford Hill (aka Oxeford Hill)

properties.name: Faery Queen
properties.description: The Faery Queen would be seen just before dawn broke, travelling in her coach and six.
properties.location_name: Old Cambus (Borders) - Fracture in a rock face, was known as the Fairy Road, and surrounding area

properties.name: Fairy in Long Green Coat and a Golden Belt
properties.description: As a reward for keeping a clean home, fairies would bring one house owner gifts. However, he bragged about the fact to a group of friends, and the little folk were never seen again.

properties.name: Dancers
properties.description: This solitary spot was a place for the fairies to meet and dance to their little hearts' content.
properties.location_name: Otterburn (Northumberland) - Dowcraig Top, around a mile north of village

properties.name: Hob
properties.description: This member of the fairy family was said to be live in the caves and would occasionally leap around upon the hills in the area. He was also reported to churn butter for the local innkeeper in return for a slice of bread and butter. On one occasion, the innkeeper forgot to leave the food out for Hob, resulting in the entity leaving the area in disgust.
properties.location_name: Over Silton (North Yorkshire) - Carlhow Hill, Hobthrush Hall (cave) and surrounding area

properties.name: The Picktree Brag
properties.description: A goblin which enjoyed playing tricks on the locals, this creature would normally take the form of a farm animal before creating mischief.

properties.name: Dusty Shoemaker's Son
properties.description: Taffy, a shoemaker's son, entered a fairy circle on this mountain and danced for ten minutes before leaving. As he walked back to the village, Taffy realised he did not recognise any of the buildings, and on the site of his parents' cottage, an old farmhouse now stood. Taffy told his story to the farmer and then, as time caught up with him, turned to ash.

properties.name: Dancers
properties.description: A large number of fairies dancing in a field were spotted by a group of people intending to play football. The fairies moved away to another field, and when they realised the people were following, became invisible.
properties.location_name: Pencarreg (Dyfed) - Area close to a mountain

properties.name: Angry Fairies
properties.description: The owner of this former castle once refused the local fairies the right to dance at his wedding party. The upset little people conjured up a massive storm which then destroyed the building. The site is now home to a Gwrach-y-rhibyn.

properties.name: Manufacturers
properties.description: The fairies who lived within this mound would be happy to manufacture any item overnight if the correct raw materials and instructions were placed on nearby grass in the evening. Someone left a short piece of wood and requested a ship's mast be made, resulting in the fairies ceasing their helpfulness.
properties.location_name: Pennygown, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - Unidentified earthen mound in the area

properties.name: Singing Fairies
properties.description: Heard singing on the mountain and from the nearby lake, these fairies were very rarely seen.

properties.name: Fairy Funerals
properties.description: Fairy funeral processions were said to once have occurred here, always by the soft light of the moon. It is reportedly unlucky for mortals to gaze upon them.

properties.name: Wilkie
properties.description: Milk would be poured daily into a hole in this knoll, to appease the entity known as Wilkie. If anyone refused to donate their fair share, they risked clothing being stolen or their cattle dropping dead.
properties.location_name: Pierowall, Westray (Orkney) - Knoll in the area

properties.name: Fairy Fair
properties.description: A villager riding home spotted a large fair taking place on the hill, with many people dressed in colourful clothing. He decided to investigate further, but as he rode up to the site, the fair vanished. After riding through the area, the villager turned, and was once again able to see the event behind him.
properties.location_name: Pitminster (Somerset) - Blackdown Hill, between Pitminster and Churchinford (once known as Chestonford)

properties.name: Fairy Kingdom
properties.description: This rocky outcrop is said to be the last haunt of the fairy folk in Wales.

properties.name: Dancing fairies
properties.description: A schoolgirl would dance with fairies under a crab apple tree, initially believing them to be other children, but eventually accepting them as fairy folk. After the novelty wore off, she gave up dancing with the fairies, but this displeased them; they dislocated her leg.
properties.location_name: Pontypool (Gwent) - Trevethin (was known as Trefethin), exact area not known

properties.name: Naked Pixies
properties.description: Pixies in this neck of the woods would sit around their fires naked, and if seen by a human, the voyeur would be 'fairy led' - forced to dance and run in frenzy through woodland until sunrise.

properties.name: Mass Evacuation
properties.description: When the first church bell rang across this region, all the local fairies packed their bags and fled.

properties.name: The Hall Knocker
properties.description: This entity, now dismissed and contained under a stone in the local church, would help the local farmers with the more boring tasks.

properties.name: Fairy Feast
properties.description: A man travelling across the moorland came across a fairy celebration with the tiny attendees including a king and queen, servants, and a band. The fairies ignored the human observer, although when the king demanded the execution of a chef for forgetting the wine, the man spoke up and tried to grab the king; the entire party vanished, leaving only a little crushed grass.
properties.location_name: Preston (Lancashire) - Ribbleton Moor (area since urbanised), area known as Red Scar

properties.name: Funeral
properties.description: A funeral procession of little people was once seen in the grounds of this ruin.

properties.name: Little People
properties.description: It is thought the village is named after a type of fairy that once frequented the area.

properties.name: Fairy Cup
properties.description: A man named Hugh joined a fairy party and stole a glittering cup. Despite being chased, Hugh escaped; the fairy cup ended up with the MacLeods on the island.
properties.location_name: Raasay Island (Highland) - Exact location not known

properties.name: Kelpie Bridle
properties.description: A man travelling from Pitlochry to Nether Lochaber rested in this area and found a horse's bridle made of pure silver which burnt hot to the touch. A wise woman told the man the bridle belonged to a Kelpie and instructed him to hang the object in his home on a crook of rowan. The man did as instructed and was blessed with luck for the rest of his life.
properties.location_name: Rannoch (Highland) - Lochanna Cuile (location may have changed identity), near or on Rannoch Moor

properties.name: Eveling
properties.description: Eveling, King of the Fairies, along with his daughter Modron lived in the Roman ruins found here.

properties.name: Helpful Hob
properties.description: A domesticated fairy who took up residence here left when the owner insulted it by offering the creature a new set of clothing.
properties.location_name: Reeth (North Yorkshire) - Sturfitt Hall (aka Sturfit Hall) (no longer standing?)

properties.name: Lady of the Lake
properties.description: This beautiful woman sits on a stone on the bank of the lake, though she dives under the water if she becomes aware of anyone watching her.

properties.name: Entrance to Fairyland
properties.description: There was once a way into fairyland here - one man who took it disappeared for seven years! Another story says one man waited by the hill and grabbed the first fairy who emerged, forcing the entity to marry him. A nearby lake contains a floating island, from which fairy music can be heard.

properties.name: Coal Discovery
properties.description: The coal deposits in the valley are said to have been discovered by fairies after they killed a giant which was attacking their homes. The giant was assassinated as he slept, and the fairies set fire to his body; they realised that the ground continued to burn after their enemy's body had turned to ash.

properties.name: Music in the Air
properties.description: Fairies are said to produce the sweet music which occasionally drifts down from the mountain at night.

properties.name: Peg Powler
properties.description: This water entity would pull under anyone who swam too close to her home - witnesses would only see her green hair seconds before their friends who would be pulled under the water.
properties.location_name: River Tees (Durham) - Waters of the river

properties.name: White Horse
properties.description: Together with a fairy, this pale horse rises from the dangerous waters once a year. It has been known to drown people who come too close.

properties.name: Green Teeth
properties.description: The water entity which drowned children and the unwary in this area, named as Green Teeth, Jenny Long Arms, or Grindylow, was described as having 'skinny claws' and 'secret dart'.
properties.location_name: Rochdale (Greater Manchester) - Bodies of water around Heywood

properties.name: Flame Farewell
properties.description: A miller named Ralph was said to have encountered a group of fairies at this waterfall. When the fairies vanished, they left a brief burning light in their wake. Unfortunately, the dell was destroyed during a flood in 1838.

properties.name: Cure for Whooping Cough
properties.description: Any child taken into the caves at low tide would be cured by the little people (or little person, depending on the legend) who lived within.
properties.location_name: Runswick Bay (North Yorkshire) - Hob Holes - caves along the coastline

properties.name: Goblin Home
properties.description: Though no trace of the structure now remains, a barrow in this neighbourhood was once the home of goblins (or a close fairy family member).
properties.location_name: Ryme Intrinseca (Dorset) - Buckbarrow (current name of location unknown)

properties.name: Healing Waters
properties.description: The one time rendezvous point of the little folk, their magical powers ensured the water of the well cured the sick.

properties.name: Many Fairy
properties.description: A nineteenth century poem declared the area home to several fairy related entities, including Old Moss (the fairy queen), Old Pack and Matty Kew (who lived together), Old Hob, Old Baker, Blater of Old Tanme, Thrasher of Slack Cote, New Tame Fiend, Young Grange Bump (and possibly their parent, Bump), Old Delph Will, and Griffon Factory Marr.

properties.name: Blessing
properties.description: It is considered unlucky not to say 'good day' to the fairies as you cross the bridge. In recent years, people have begun to leave gifts and notes on a nearby tree for the fairies.

properties.name: Several Dancers in White Muslin
properties.description: Observed by a couple of young children, the dancers only remained visible for a few seconds before disappearing.

properties.name: Dancers
properties.description: A young man heading towards Whitby spotted a group of fairies dancing in a circle. The man joined them. At one point during the dance the fairies called out 'whip, whip!', and they pulled out and cracked small hunting whips. The man then called out the same words, which resulted in the fairies whipping him and giving chase as he fled towards Whitby.
properties.location_name: Scalby (North Yorkshire) - Moors in the area, heading north

properties.name: Gruagach Attack
properties.description: A long haired, fairy-like creature, the Gruagach in this area entertained itself by releasing cattle at night and could be heard laughing as farmers awoke to sort out the mess. The Gruagach killed one woman after she cursed its antics.
properties.location_name: Scorrybreac (aka Scorrybrec), Isle of Skye (Highland) - Cowshed in the area (likely no longer standing)

properties.name: Cro Sith
properties.description: The area where Clan Nicolson made their home was also the location of fairy cattle. The creatures were trapped and caught on the island after earth from a graveyard was scattered on the shore. Following the cattle's capture, a voice from the sea could be heard calling their names.
properties.location_name: Scorrybreac (aka Scorrybrec), Isle of Skye (Highland) - Shores

properties.name: Jinny On Boggard
properties.description: This little creature was like a small man or woman, though it had a face of a seal with long hair. It travelled on the water in a large pie dish. Another creature here, or possibly another form of the same one, had large tusks and swam in the water; it would only leave to feed. Finally, one piece of folklore has a supernatural steed that walks around on the surface of the water here.
properties.location_name: Scunthorpe (Lincolnshire) - River Trent on Humberside/Lincolnshire border, area known as Jenny Hurn bend

properties.name: Fairy Fort
properties.description: While constructing a new runway during the 1950s, the chief engineer was warned that the work would result in the destruction of a fairy fort - the location of the runway was changed, leaving the fort undisturbed.

properties.name: Rings
properties.description: An overgrown lane in the village (now a Sheffield suburb) was said to be home to fairies, with distinct fairy circles spotted in the grass. A headless woman also haunted the lane.
properties.location_name: Sheffield (South Yorkshire) - Dead Man's Lane (aka Dead Lane), Ecclesall (lane may have been renamed, but in Greystones area)

properties.name: Apports
properties.description: The island is renowned for its mischievous fairies, who take objects or mysteriously place other objects in strange places.

properties.name: Pixies
properties.description: This barrow was the home to pixies, who were said to have caused several accidents during the construction of the power station.
properties.location_name: Shurton (Somerset) - Wick Barrow, aka Pixies Mound (on the site of Hinkley Point power station, private property)

properties.name: Farm Hand
properties.description: Being a dab hand with corn thrashing, this fairy fellow was most welcome at the house, but left when accidentally insulted by the owner.

properties.name: Fairy Flag
properties.description: The Flag of the MacLeod's was given to them by a fairy that changed herself into a mortal and married into the family - it can only be used three times, but each time it is waved, it grants the MacLeods victory over a foe. Used twice before (1490 & 1580), the flag remained true to the fairy's words. The Fairy Bridge near the castle is considered inherently evil, and upsets animals.
properties.location_name: Sligachan, Isle of Skye (Highland) - Dunvegan Castle

properties.name: Phynnodderee
properties.description: The phynnodderee, also known as a fenodyree, started to help someone build their home but fled the area after a gift left for the fairy unbound it from the site.
properties.location_name: Snaefell (Isle of Man) - Tholt-e-Will (aka Tholt-y-Will), Snafield mountain

properties.name: Leaving Fairies
properties.description: The locals once played a trick on the fairies here - they promptly moved away in protest.
properties.location_name: Snizort, Isle of Skye (Highland) - Dun Borve

properties.name: Dancing Fairies
properties.description: This small lake on the north side of Snowdon is home to fairies and goblins who dance around the shoreline.

properties.name: Black Orb
properties.description: On a warm summer's evening, a witness heard flapping in their ears, like the sound of dragonfly wings. Turning their head, the witness spotted a black orb hovering in the room. As they approached, the orb darted into the garden through an open door.

properties.name: Little People
properties.description: The south facing side of the hill are said to be where the vast majority of the island's fairies and pixies live. It is also said that a giant is asleep under the hill, having been cursed by Merlin.
properties.location_name: South Barrule (Isle of Man) - South Barrule hill

properties.name: Fairy Fort
properties.description: This old hill fort once belonged to the fairies, although by stabbing the hill with a knife, one can hear the little people speak.

properties.name: Gnomes
properties.description: Mrs Rose told Sir Arthur Conan Doyle that she frequently spotted fairies and gnomes in the shrubbery. The gnomes would take the form of little old men who wore green and assumed the role of guardians to the fairies.

properties.name: Nanny Diamonds
properties.description: Little folk are still reputed to dance and cause mischief along this piece of road.
properties.location_name: Southwell, Portland (Dorset) - Road between Southwell and Cheyne

properties.name: Speech
properties.description: Fairies can be heard talking within the hill if the listener either runs around the hill nine times anticlockwise while the moon is waxing, or they stick a knife into the centre of the mound, or both.

properties.name: Robin Roundcap
properties.description: An elf took up residence here, putting out fires and spoiling food. He was banished to a nearby well by three members of the clergy.
properties.location_name: Spaldington (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Spaldington Hall (demolished 1838)

properties.name: Home
properties.description: Fairies were once thought to live around the knoll, which local legend says was also used as a site to burn witches if they survived their dunking at nearby Witch Lake.
properties.location_name: St Andrews (Fife) - Small knoll close to the Martyr's Monument, and nearby Witch Lake

properties.name: Fairy Cow
properties.description: A local farmer once kept a cow that was used by fairies after dark - the little folk were driven away, and the farmer's luck changed from good to bad.
properties.location_name: St Buryan (Cornwall) - Field called Park-an-Ventan, Bosfrancan (no longer there?)

properties.name: Fairy Feast
properties.description: The little people were once seen eating and having a party at this site.
properties.location_name: St Catherine's Point (Isle of Wight) - Puckaster Cove

properties.name: The Fairy Led
properties.description: The neighbourhood was the haunt of particularly pesky fairies who would lead men off the paths at night and let them wander aimlessly until dawn. One man claimed he had walked to the north pole, but of course, some people exaggerate.

properties.name: Blinded
properties.description: The fairies which were believed to live bathe here would strike anyone blind who spotted them. The area is also said to be home to spooky noises, although the source is unknown.

properties.name: Missing Wife
properties.description: The farmer's first wife was kidnapped by fairies, so he later remarried. A short time later, the first wife briefly reappeared and told the farmer that if swept the barn free of straw, then he would be able to grab her fairy horse and rescue her when she next rode past. The farmer shared the plan with his second wife, but filled with jealousy, she ensured that a piece of straw was left on the barn floor. The rescue failed and the first wife never returned.
properties.location_name: St John's (Isle of Man) - Ballaleece Farm

properties.name: Troll
properties.description: Trolls were said to once lurk around various places on the Isle, with place names indicating where they lived. Church bells were said to have driven them away.
properties.location_name: St Johns (Isle of Man) - Trollaby Lane

properties.name: Dancing People
properties.description: Traditionally the homes of fairies, this barrow is one of many in the area and the little people have been seen dancing around it.

properties.name: Gateway to Fairyland
properties.description: Legend names this site as an entrance to fairyland, with the little people being seen milling around the doorway. If one left a bowl of porridge by the doorway with unfinished knitting overnight, by the following day the bowl would be empty and the knitting complete.
properties.location_name: St Pierre du Bois (Guernsey) - Creux es Faies (megalithic tomb)

properties.name: Washing Folk
