properties.description: These fairies would arrive in the area at midnight to make use of the stream water to wash their clothing.

properties.name: Cake Baking Fairies
properties.description: The pixies here rewarded a man with a cake after he helped them repair a cooking implement.

properties.name: Dances
properties.description: This village was once said to be well known for its fairy rings and fairy dances.

properties.name: Soft Ringing
properties.description: The little people ring the church bells after covering them with dew, ensuring the soft ringing is seldom heard by mortals.

properties.name: Dozen Dancing Fairies
properties.description: Whilst walking one night, a gentleman watched a dozen doll-like figures dancing around in a ring. He reported them as being more like little ghosts than solid creatures, making no sound and wearing sparkling dresses, standing no more than three foot high. Fairies were once quite common (as common as fairy sightings are) in the meadows surrounding the town.

properties.name: Tiny Frolicsome People
properties.description: Appearing only at night, tiny people were often seen dancing and singing along Tavern Street, disappearing only after becoming aware that they were being watched.

properties.name: Urisk
properties.description: A newspaper article dated 1913 claimed that it had not many years had passed since a Urisk, a large hairy creature similar to a brownie, had appeared in the area.
properties.location_name: Strath Fillan (aka Strathfillan) (Perth and Kinross) - General area

properties.name: Brownie
properties.description: This invisible entity lived in the castle and was only detectible when it stole food from the pantry.

properties.name: Maiden
properties.description: A fairy maiden lived in the waters of this lake. A story also claimed a large town once stood here but it was swallowed by the lake (after the locals insulted Saint Patrick) and then used by the fairies to construct their kingdom. A slight variation says the fairies were once the townsfolk before they were submerged. Bells could occasionally be heard coming from the water.
properties.location_name: Swansea (West Glamorgan) - Crumlyn Lake (aka Llyn Crymlyn), now Crymlyn Bog

properties.name: Ball of Fire
properties.description: A man pursued a ball of fire that morphed into a shapeshifting 'horrid black thing'. Despite outrunning him, the shape settled near the exhausted man and started to shrink, so the man caught the entity in his hat. Intending to ask the entity where he could find buried treasure, the man began to daydream about what to do with his newfound wealth, which included leaving his wife for another woman; the creature disappeared.
properties.location_name: Swansea (West Glamorgan) - Mumbles, exact area not known

properties.name: Fairy Battle
properties.description: It was said that fairies fought openly above the skies of this hill during the potato famine. The hill is also said to be the burial place of Finnbheara, king of the Connacht fairies.
properties.location_name: Taum (County Galway) - Cnoc Meadha, aka Knockmagha, Knockma, or Knock Ma (hill)

properties.name: Built by Fairies
properties.description: It is said that the Queen of the fairies helped build this bridge by turning some of the water below into boulders.

properties.name: Dog Killer
properties.description: The Brownie that lurked around this area would split its time between the castle and the MacDonald house on Cara Island. The entity could be quite vicious, killing dogs that had been left in the house at night and slapping visitors who disrespected the castle, although it would also tidy the site and secure the cattle.
properties.location_name: Tayinloan (Argyll and Bute) - Old Largie Castle (demolished 1958)

properties.name: Fairy Fort
properties.description: This fort, like another in the area and so many in Ireland, was believed to be home to a troupe of fairies.

properties.name: Fairy Home
properties.description: This fort, like another in the area and so many in Ireland, was believed to be home to a troupe of fairies.

properties.name: Water Fairy
properties.description: A legend tells of a lake fairy that once emerged from Llyn y Fan Fach and married a local man. She eventually returned home to the lake after her husband hit her, but only after bearing him three sons; she later returned to teach them medical skills.
properties.location_name: The Black Mountain / Myddfai (Dyfed) - Llyn y Fan Fach mountain lake

properties.name: Blind the Elves!
properties.description: It was written that some fishermen would rub salt into their nets and sow salt into the water to blind the elves, who would otherwise ruin a good day's fishing.

properties.name: Well of Our Lady
properties.description: A local man stumbled across a party of goblins, only managing to elude the creatures by hiding in the well. Ever since that day, the holy well has been thought to repel evil.

properties.name: Unclothed Man
properties.description: Cattle left overnight were looked after by a Brownie that remained invisible to moral eyes. A seer investigated and reported spotting an unclothed man looking after the cows. To show his gratitude, the seer left a gift of tartan trousers and shoes; the Brownie put on the clothing and left the island.
properties.location_name: Tiree (Argyll and Bute) - Unknown farm on the west of the island

properties.name: Dwarven Miners
properties.description: Working deep beneath the mountain, the dwarfs would create items for mortals only if the price were right.

properties.name: Pixie Home
properties.description: One of the stories the tour guides share is that the cave was once inhabited by pixies.

properties.name: Dinky Men
properties.description: Named 'Dinky Men' by an older woman who lived in a cottage on the moor, these Cornish piskeys would help the woman and her grandchild when assistance was needed. Other visitors to the moor would be less fortunate; they would be pixie led into the bogs.

properties.name: Fairy Meet
properties.description: Fairies are said to make their presence known around this area once a year.

properties.name: Piskie Kidnapping
properties.description: A young local boy was taken one night by the fairies but found several days later alive and well. He said that a beautiful lady had taken him into a fantastic palace where he had fallen asleep, only to wake up to see his rescuers.
properties.location_name: Treonike (now St Allen, Truro) (Cornwall) - Nearby woodland

properties.name: Cliff Dancers
properties.description: Before the cliffs eroded away, fairies were said to dance of the edge so often that the grass never grew, and tiny footprints could be seen in the dirt.

properties.name: Way In
properties.description: Wayne Anthony, in Haunted Derbyshire and the Peak District, writes that hidden within the cave is an entrance to the fairy kingdom, though one needs to possess second sight to find it. A ghostly Roman centurion is said to stand guard at the mouth of the cave.

properties.name: Fairy Hill
properties.description: Sounds of music and partying can be heard coming from this mound, the little folk still in full party mode. One legend says that a local fiddler was paid handsomely in gold to perform for the fairies at one of their parties. He fiddled for ten hours, after which he returned home, only to find a hundred years had elapsed.
properties.location_name: Uig, Isle of Skye (Highland) - Sithean Beinne Bho'idhich - Fairy Glen

properties.name: Entrance to Fairy Land
properties.description: A small opening in the limestone here was thought to lead to fairyland, though it is possible for a slender person to crawl into the cave.

properties.name: Entrance to Fairyland
properties.description: Elidurus visited fairyland via an entrance located somewhere in the Vale. In the other land, horses and greyhounds were the same as our world, only much smaller, the sky always cloudy, and the fairies consumed only milk. Elidurus returned several times, but after trying to steal some gold for his mother, discovered that the doorway to fairyland had been once again hidden.
properties.location_name: Vale of Neath (West Glamorgan) - General area

properties.name: Sea Trows
properties.description: These water-based troll-like creatures were once believed to swim the waters here, occasionally coming onto land to rest. It is thought they dressed in seal's skin while swimming, pealing it off to resemble humans when needing to leave the water.
properties.location_name: Ve Skerries (Shetland) - Sea in the area

properties.name: Fairy Kitchen
properties.description: The scorch marks on these ruins led to some saying the site had been used by fairies as their kitchen.
properties.location_name: Vindolanda (aka Chesterholm) (Northumberland) - West of area, in ruins containing hypocausts

properties.name: Moved
properties.description: The fairies took a dislike to where the church was going to be built, so moved the foundation stones to the top of the hill where the church was finally built.

properties.name: Jenny Greenteeth
properties.description: Children were warned to stay away from a dangerous pit, as Jenny Greenteeth would drown them.
properties.location_name: Warrington (Cheshire) - Unknown pit in a field, Latchford

properties.name: Dancing Fairies
properties.description: The hill here is reportedly home to the little people, who spend the night-time hours dancing on the summit.

properties.name: Moved by Fairies
properties.description: The church was planned to be built elsewhere, however the local fairy population had other plans, and moved the building materials to the place the structure now stands.

properties.name: Fairy Loaves
properties.description: It was once believed that fossilised sea urchins that were found in a gravel pit here were loaves of bread made by fairies.

properties.name: Troll Home
properties.description: Several stories exist around this stone. One says it was thrown into location by an angry giant and became the home of a passing troll. Another says it was the home of a giant and his wife, while a third names the occupant as a dwarf named Trollid.

properties.name: Rabbit-sized Demon
properties.description: This sober witness stated that, while cycling, a small bipedal creature with horns ran out in front of him, travelling just as fast as the bicycle. It disappeared as it hopped on a grass verge.

properties.name: Pixie Party
properties.description: A human gatecrasher reported joining a pixie party that was occurring within this barrow - he soon ran off, not wanting to fall under a fairy spell. Another man was said to have picked up a golden coin each day from the barrow, left there for him by a fairy who liked him, although she stopped her act of generosity when the man told others of her. Finally, another story says there is treasure here, although it can never be removed.
properties.location_name: Wold Newton (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Willy Howe barrow

properties.name: Fairy Wishes
properties.description: By dropping a bent pin or money into this well, visitors to the well would appease the fairy which lived nearby, and maybe have their wish come true.

properties.name: Butter Makers
properties.description: Fairies and other little folk would use this area to churn their milk into butter.

properties.name: Bobby Griglans
properties.description: A man cutting gorse on the hill discovered a thirty centimetre long fairy. The man took the fairy home and allowed his children to play with it, who named the creature Bobby Griglans. After a few days, the fairy's parents came to take it back, referring to the fairy as 'Skillywidden'.
properties.location_name: Zennor (Cornwall) - Area referred to as Trendraen Hill, although likely to be Trendrine Hill

properties.name: Headless Hound
properties.description: This headless dog was said to have once belonged to a young giant who ran so fast that he decapitated the dog by pulling too sharply on the leash.

properties.name: Giant's Treasure
properties.description: The giant's treasure slipped from his grasp as he crossed the area, the hoard sinking into the ground and becoming covered in rocks. The giant had to leave the treasure behind, but it was written that a mortal man could recover the wealth if a fairy in the form of a chicken or an ape appeared; the man would have to reach out and grab the treasure without making a sound, and if he failed to do so, the fairy would never appear to him again.
properties.location_name: Addleborough (North Yorkshire) - Barrow in the area

properties.name: Sound of an Army
properties.description: Close to the hill, Miss Muriel Cobern heard dozens of horses galloping, as if an invisible army were passing. The sound suddenly stopped as she fled the area. The site is also said to be the resting place of a giant, who will awaken if the hill is run around seven times.
properties.location_name: Alton Barnes (Wiltshire) - Adam's Grave, aka Walker Hill, aka Walker's Hill, aka Walkers' Hill

properties.name: Thrown Stone
properties.description: Ireland's most southerly point, Fastnet Rock was thrown into position by a giant from Mount Gabriel (Schull).

properties.name: Idris
properties.description: Idris was said to be a wise giant, who may have had links with King Arthur. People would avoid the area at night, convinced that fairies would drive them mad. Another legend says that the site was the hunting ground of Gwyn ap Nudd (Ruler of the Otherworld) who searched the area with a pack of Cwn Annwn, looking for souls to take back home. Finally. Strange lights are said to appear around the summit for the first few days of a new year.

properties.name: Thrown Objects
properties.description: Before being removed by farmers, the standing stones which stood around the area were thought to have been thrown by giants engaged in combat.

properties.name: Blacksmith
properties.description: A giant blacksmith is said to appear if you call out his name while standing on this hill.

properties.name: Donnell of the Yellow Hair
properties.description: A stone here marked the grave of the giant Dombnall Dual-Buidhe, or Donnell of the Yellow Hair. A dolman once marked the spot but had been removed by 1897.
properties.location_name: Belmullet (County Mayo) - Stone on fortified hill, 4.5 miles southwest of town (current status not known)

properties.name: Fighting Giants
properties.description: A monolith found here features a larger-than-life handprint and footprint - it is said that the prints belong to two giants who fought over the love of a woman.

properties.name: Church Stones
properties.description: The Fell came into being when a giant accidentally dropped an apron full of rocks being used to build a church (to redeem himself from his life of sin and violence). A large ghostly figure wearing an apron is said to appear when the mist grows thick.

properties.name: Giant's Stone
properties.description: A giant who lived at Birmingham castle was killed when the Giant of Dudley threw a stone at him (launched from Dudley!). The rock also demolished the castle. The stone was erected as a memento, and the lane named after the War Stone used as a weapon. The rock is now on a plinth within the cemetery.

properties.name: Cursed Giant
properties.description: The chime was once a lush area, rich in vegetation and the hunting ground of a giant who feasted upon children. A holy man cursed the giant and the area, ensuring nothing would ever grow there again.
properties.location_name: Blackgang Chime (Isle of Wight) - Cave in the area (no longer present?)

properties.name: Magical Giant
properties.description: A giant who lived nearby was said to use this site once a year for magical rites and was happy for local people to watch.
properties.date: Said to have taken place every 01 August (when giant was alive)

properties.name: Goram and Vincent
properties.description: To prove themselves worthy of the lady Avona's love, the giant brothers Goram and Vincent raced to dig a ditch to drain a lake. Goram started work but fell asleep after digging the Hazel Brook Gorge, while Vincent dug the Avon Gorge that drained the unwanted lake, thus winning Avona's heart.

properties.name: Goram's Remains
properties.description: After losing a girl and a bet to his brother Vincent, the giant Goram threw himself into the Bristol Channel. There he petrified, his head and shoulders forming Flat Holm and Steep Holm.
properties.location_name: Bristol Channel (Somerset) - Flat Holm and Steep Holm

properties.name: Thrown
properties.description: A local legend says a large boulder that could once be found here was thrown into position by a giant. Another story says that twenty men moved the stone and tried to throw it at a giant. The stone has since been removed or broken up.
properties.location_name: Brockhampton Green (Dorset) - Large stone (no longer present)

properties.name: Fight
properties.description: The local giant fought another giant who wandered into his land. The local giant managed to kill the newcomer, who, it transpired, was his son.
properties.location_name: Bundoran (County Donegal) - Close to an unidentified stream in the area

properties.name: Mysterious Stones
properties.description: It is still argued to what extent this stone circle represents the heavenly bodies above, and what its ultimate purpose was. One legend says that the thirteen stones were giants who refused to become Christians - they were petrified for the sin.
properties.location_name: Callanish, Isle of Lewis (Outer Hebrides) - Callanish standing stones

properties.name: Coach
properties.description: A coach is reportedly heard when a member of the Hastings family is due to die. At the castle it was heard by John Boyle, the night his cousin the Marquis Hastings died. Other ghosts reported to haunt the castle include the second Marques of Bute (who walks through several walls), a faceless woman in a long skirt who is known as Sarah, and a three metre tall giant who walks around the park.

properties.name: John of Gaunt
properties.description: The giant John would often throw boulders at neighbouring giants, who would toss them back at him - hence, the hillside is covered in large rocks. The giant's remains are said to be buried somewhere on the site.
properties.location_name: Carn Brea (Cornwall) - Stones on nearby hill

properties.name: Crushed Friend
properties.description: A giant who once lived here (occasionally named as Holiburn of the Carn) was visited by a human friend. After a few games of quoits, the human had to leave. The giant patted his friend's head in a playful way, unfortunately forgetting his own strength - his fingers pieced the human's head, killing the man instantly. The giant died of a broken heart seven years later.

properties.name: Chalk Giant
properties.description: One story says this location is where a visiting Danish giant fell asleep, exhausted after killing dozens of local men. The remaining villagers took the opportunity to sneak up and cut off his head, drawing a chalk outline around the body to ward off other giants. The chalk figure is also considered a fertility symbol, and anyone who 'sleeps' on the figure is believed to be blessed (or cursed) with many children.

properties.name: Giant Stone
properties.description: Two origin myths surround this stone. One says the rock was thrown into position by an angry giant, while the other states the rock was rolled into position by villagers attempting to move it up the hill to use as a weapon against the giant.
properties.location_name: Cerne Abbas (Dorset) - Stone on Bockhampton Green

properties.name: Giant Grave
properties.description: The mound found here is said to be the grave of a giant who dropped dead after losing a game of 'throw the boulder the furthest'. Two stones nearby are said to mark the place where the giant and his (winning) friend stood; when the cockerel crows, the stones move.

properties.name: Thrown Stone
properties.description: The largest stone found here is said to have been thrown into position by a giant standing a couple of kilometres away.

properties.name: Giant's Droppings
properties.description: Angry at the town of Shrewsbury, a giant gathered a mound of earth and set off to dam up the River Severn. After the giant became lost, he approached a man heading home towards the town. The local lied and managed to convince the dim-witted villain it would take months to reach the town. The giant sighed and dumped the earth where he stood, forming the Wrekin.

properties.name: Thrown from Currabinny
properties.description: The boulder which can be found here has a granite plinth nearby, stating the rock was thrown into position by the giant of Currabinny.

properties.name: Prince Idwal
properties.description: While the exact details vary from storyteller to storyteller, the legends agree that a young man named Idwal was murdered at the lake and that birds now avoid flying over the water as a mark of respect. Some variations say Idwal can be heard calling during stormy weather. As a side note, a giant is said to be buried on the nearby mountain.
properties.location_name: Cwm Idwal (Clwyd) - Waters of the lake

properties.name: One Eye
properties.description: A one-eyed giant once resided in the mill, using the building to grind his human victims into bread. One of his prisoners, a young lad named Jack, managed to escape by plunging a knife into the Cyclops's eye, killing the creature. At least that was the story which was told to explain the long mound which really existed close to the mill.
properties.location_name: Dalton (North Yorkshire) - The Giant's Grave and mill (no longer standing)

properties.name: Sitting Giant
properties.description: A giant was said to sit here, using the nearby river to cool his feet and Dunster Castle as a drying rack for his clothing.
properties.location_name: Dunster (Somerset) - Grabbist Hill, the Giant's Chair

properties.name: Isir
properties.description: This natural dwelling was once home to a giant named Isir, who enjoyed the taste of human meat. His fate is unknown.
properties.location_name: Eamont Bridge (Cumbria) - Cave by the river

properties.name: Mount of Frogs
properties.description: Ider the Arthurian knight killed three giants on this hill, known at the time as the Mount of Frogs.

properties.name: Hugh Cesario
properties.description: This cave system was once the home to Hugh Cesario, a giant who either fought on the side of good, or took local men, women and farm animals back to the cave to snack on.

properties.name: Resting Giant
properties.description: The rocky outcrop known as Arthur's Seat was once used as a resting place for a passing giant. In 1836 a group of schoolboys discovered seventeen tiny coffins hidden in the area, each containing a small carved figure inside. Their purpose is unknown.

properties.name: Ella
properties.description: Legend says the town is named after Ella, a Danish giant who terrorised the area and reduced the city to the village it is now.

properties.name: Built by the Devil
properties.description: A local giant made a wager with Old Nick that that a bridge could not be built across the River Barle overnight. Old Nick rolled up his sleeves and promptly proved him wrong. The Devil then took ownership of the bridge, refusing to let anyone cross safely until a priest drove him away.
properties.location_name: Exmoor (Devon) - Tarr Bridge (also known as Tarr Steps), River Barle

properties.name: Finn's Bridge
properties.description: Finn McCool, a hero of Ireland, created this rocky causeway to enable a Scottish giant to cross the sea so they could fight. MacCool won the duel after dressing as a baby; the Scottish giant fled home, tearing up the bridge to prevent the Irish giant from following. Also, the ghost of a boy who drowned here in 1910 is still seen playing on the rocks - he wears a soaking wet sailor's suit and vanishes if spoken to.

properties.name: Dead Husband
properties.description: Sheela, a giant's wife, killed her husband after throwing their bed at him - he had argued with her and struck her shoulder with an axe. His body still lays under the tomb, and Sheela can be heard in times of national crisis calling out her husband's name. Another story says four treasure hunters arrived at the stone and started to dig beneath it. Before long, a cat appeared with a burning tail. The light blinded the men and they staggered off, one falling into the River Funshion and drowning.
properties.location_name: Glanworth (County Cork) - Labbacallee (aka The Hag's Bed), wedge tomb

properties.name: Raura Peens
properties.description: Raura Peens, the last fairy of the area, may have appeared to a local man at these stones during the nineteenth century. The fairy tried to lure the witness to her realm (or at least a cave under the area) but failed. Another story said the stones were once home to a giant named Heywood.

properties.name: Arrival of Hercules
properties.description: Mainland Britain was once ruled over by a giant known as Albion, who named the island after himself. Albion the giant was defeated by Hercules, who landed in upon these shores at this location.

properties.name: Overnight Construction
properties.description: The giant Moll Walbee, known also as Maud de Breos, built this castle in a single night!

properties.name: Giant Saxon Warriors
properties.description: Accounts of warrior ghosts seen at this sight date back hundreds of years, and during the 1950s (or 1920s, according to other sources) several Saxon graves were found around the area.
properties.location_name: Heydon (Cambridgeshire) - Heydon Ditch. Five foot high at its highest point, runs from Heydon to Fowlmere (3.5 miles in length)

properties.name: Goram
properties.description: Known as either Goram or Gorm, this giant was buried in the area.

properties.name: Bel's Grave
properties.description: The area is said to be named after a giant who died in the area - he tried to travel between Mountsorrel to Leicester in three leaps but failed.

properties.name: Gold Guardians
properties.description: This hill was formed when a giant buried his treasure, leaving a troupe of warrior fairies to protect it. They have been called into action at least once, appearing as a would-be treasure hunter arrived armed with pick and shovel.

properties.name: Devil's Cheese
properties.description: An old piece of folklore says the Devil would make his cheese using this stack of stones. The tor was created when Saint Tue engaged in a stone tossing contest with Uther, one of the local giants. One of Uther's stones missed the target; Saint Tue picked the rock up and an angel carried it to the top of the tor. Most of the giants promptly converted to Christianity.

properties.name: Home
properties.description: The hill, another home to fairies, also claimed to house the grave of a giant - anyone who laid within the hill would be granted incredible strength.

properties.name: Treasure and Death
properties.description: A giant buried his wife here, along with a golden circlet. Three brothers looking for the treasure overturned the capstone of the cromlech which once stood here. It is unclear whether they found the circlet, but legend says they all died suddenly soon after their dig.

properties.name: Moll's Stone
properties.description: Moll Wallbee, also known as Matilda de Braose and Maud de Braose, was said to be a giantess who married William de Braose, the Forth Lord of Bramber. While carrying stones in her apron (to rebuild a castle), one fell into her shoe. She picked it out and tossed it into the churchyard of Llowes, some three miles from where she stood.
properties.location_name: Llowes (Powys) - Moll Walbee's Stone (aka St. Meilig's Cross, on display in the church)

properties.name: Red Hand
properties.description: A giant warrior, Red Hand reportedly walks along the shoreline.
properties.location_name: Loch Morlich (Highland) - Shores of the loch

properties.name: Roman Legion
properties.description: This long dead legion is said to march across the moor, carrying glowing torches; they set off from Melandra, old roman fortification near Glossop. Another report states that the area is haunted by a giant grey man, standing at least 10 meters tall.

properties.name: Giant's Home
properties.description: This castle was said to be the home of Wade, the giant. He is also held accountable for a road and two large 'graves', marked by standing stones.

properties.name: Finn's Kitchen
properties.description: Created by the giant Finn McCool as a place for his cauldron, this set of standing stones remain intact ready for the next passing ogre...
properties.location_name: Machrie, Isle of Arran (Ayrshire) - Fingal's Cauldron Seat

properties.name: Table & Chair
properties.description: Legend states that this location was once home to a giant - the stones that remain were his table and sitting stone. Other reports say that Arthur used the site.

properties.name: Giant Grave
properties.description: The giant wife of St Michael's Mount was accidently killed when struck by a carelessly tossed hammer. The rock was lifted by her husband and her body placed beneath.

properties.name: Loser
properties.description: This earth mound is said to contain the body of a giant who lost a stone throwing contest against another of his ilk. The two stones are close by and are said to rotate when a cockerel is heard to crow.

properties.name: Giant's Grave
properties.description: This large stone on Merbach Hill is said to be the final resting place of a giant slain by the king.

properties.name: Buried Giant
properties.description: Running around this barrow seven times will result in the sleeping giant awaking.

properties.name: Mahon Mac Mahon
properties.description: Several giants lived in the cave, including Mahon Mac Mahon, who kidnapped children and forced them to work brass and iron. A blacksmith by the name of Robert Kelly managed to enter the cave and return one child to his parents - the child had been missing for seven years but had not aged a single day since disappearing.

properties.name: Hulac Warren
properties.description: Monsal Dale was once known as Demon's Dale, and a race of sadistic giants lived there. A shepherdess killed herself rather than be kidnapped by the giant's leader Hulac; the gods were so outraged by his actions that the giant was instantly turned in to the Warren Stone.

properties.name: Giant Quoit
properties.description: The pile of rocks is said to be the discarded remains of stones used by giants during a game of quoits.

properties.name: MacCoul Giants
properties.description: These two large warriors once lived in the bay, though now in shadow form only.

properties.name: Giant's Home
properties.description: This giant was said to eat to occasional child and pass time by throwing rocks at other giants who lived nearby.
properties.date: Unknown, but cottage ruins said to visible in early nineteenth century
properties.location_name: Nancledra (Cornwall) - Cottage (no longer standing) by the brook

properties.name: Robert FitzHamon
properties.description: The protective structure is haunted by the giant figure of FitzHamon, the castle's builder.

properties.name: Gorm
properties.description: A giant by the name of Gorm built this camp as his home.

properties.name: Returning Giant
properties.description: A local legend states that if you run around the barrow seven times, the giant buried within shall return.

properties.name: Staked Giant
properties.description: This sycamore grew from a stake used on the body of a giant. It is unclear whether this implies that if removed, the giant shall return.
properties.location_name: Offley Hay (Staffordshire) - Unidentified sycamore tree in the area

properties.name: Giant Shadow
properties.description: A black shadowy figure, standing two and a half metres tall with no discernible features, gave chase to a group of friends as they left the wood.
properties.location_name: Outer London: Havering (Greater London) - Frank's Wood, now known as 'the brickfields', near Moor Lane, Cranham

properties.name: Giant's Grave
properties.description: Large stones mark the place where a giant is buried - he is thought to be Sir Ewen (or Sir Ewain), a great boar hunter.

properties.name: Swelkie
properties.description: The whirlpool that forms here is thought to be created by the turning of a mill stone to grind salt, ensuring the sea remains salty. Some say the wheel is turned by a sea-witch, while others name the grinders as Fenia and Menia, two local giants.

properties.name: Wrath
properties.description: Wrath was a giant who lived in a cave with direct access to the sea. He would wait for a ship to wreck before taking the surviving sailors home to eat.
properties.location_name: Portreath (Cornwall) - Sea cave in the area

properties.name: Giant's Grave
properties.description: The Scottish giant Parrah Boug MacShagean built the dolmen at this site and challenged Fionn mac Cumhaill to combat. Fionn poisoned the nearby river and Parrah died from drinking the water. Parrah's body is said to lay in the nearby tomb. A legend says that if a visitor can manage to land three stones on to the dolmen, their wish shall be granted.
properties.location_name: Ravensdale (County Louth) - Proleek Dolmen and tomb

properties.name: Robin of Redesdale
properties.description: The statue formerly known as Robin of Redesdale was said to be that of a hunter, a giant who lived in the neighbourhood, poisoned by his brother.
properties.date: Statue no longer present (destroyed pre-nineteenth century?)
properties.location_name: Redesdale (Northumberland) - Statue which stood on around Watling Street area

properties.name: Grave
properties.description: A giant's grave is said to be within this wooded area. The area is said to take its name from a rock that sits on the edge of a nearby cliff, thrown there by the Devil during a competition.

properties.name: Giant Arthur
properties.description: One story associated with this Neolithic tomb has King Arthur finding a stone in his boot. The king picked the pebble out and threw it to one side, thus placing the stone where it now stands. Another legend says the stone walks down to the sea once a year for a quick drink. Finally, another story says the stone was split by a miller who was after a millstone - unable to lift it, the split stone has remained laying there since.
properties.date: 01 January (reoccurring) (walking stone legend said to occur)

properties.name: The Giant's Grave
properties.description: A mass grave in the churchyard reputedly contains a thighbone that measured over a metre in length.

properties.name: Coal Discovery
properties.description: The coal deposits in the valley are said to have been discovered by fairies after they killed a giant which was attacking their homes. The giant was assassinated as he slept, and the fairies set fire to his body; they realised that the ground continued to burn after their enemy's body had turned to ash.

properties.name: Green Teeth
properties.description: The water entity which drowned children and the unwary in this area, named as Green Teeth, Jenny Long Arms, or Grindylow, was described as having 'skinny claws' and 'secret dart'.
properties.location_name: Rochdale (Greater Manchester) - Bodies of water around Heywood

properties.name: Local Giant
properties.description: A giant terrorised this area for many years, bullying and killing the locals. The massive man was finally slain when, after a day of abhorrent behaviour, he fell asleep by a watermill - the miller ran outside and hacked him to death with an axe. Another version of the story, in which the giant was accidently knocked out by windmill sails, names the giant slayer as Sir Guy Dawnay, who just happened to be passing through the area.

properties.name: Onion
properties.description: A local giant named Onion tossed a stone that landed on the common, a mile away, leaving the impressions of his fingers upon it. Others have said that the stone was thrown by the Devil.
properties.location_name: Silchester (Hampshire) - Stone on the common, Hampshire/Berkshire Border

properties.name: Giant
properties.description: The summit of Mount Snowdon is rumoured to be the burial place of a giant banished by King Arthur.

properties.name: Giant and Bloody Rain
properties.description: Somerleyton was once the hunting area of a giant, who is said to briefly appear on the evening of 17 July. The giant once stumbled across a plot to murder him, so struck first and killed the would-be assassin. The assassin's blood is said to rain down from the sky on 19 November.

properties.name: Little People
properties.description: The south facing side of the hill are said to be where the vast majority of the island's fairies and pixies live. It is also said that a giant is asleep under the hill, having been cursed by Merlin.
properties.location_name: South Barrule (Isle of Man) - South Barrule hill

properties.name: Ascapart
properties.description: Ascapart the giant was beaten in a fight by Bevis of Hampton, after his club (a tree) became stuck in the mud. Instead of slaying the giant, Bevis appointed Ascapart his squire. When the giant finally died, he was buried under Bevis Mound.
properties.location_name: Southampton (Hampshire) - Bevis Mound and general area

properties.name: Stones
properties.description: The stone formation at the top of the hill were carried up by either Saint Agnes or a giant's wife. Another account says that Agnes managed to turn the Devil into stone, where he remains to this day.

properties.name: Cormoran
properties.description: The mount was once home to this legendary beast (named by some as a seven foot giant), thought to have a single eye. It crossed to the mainland at low tide, helping itself to cattle for its food.

properties.name: Cricket Bat
properties.description: Children would be told that this 350 centimetre tall stone was a giant's cricket bat, thrust into the earth after another giant refused to fetch the ball during their game. One story says the site is also haunted by an old woman manifesting on moonlit nights.
properties.location_name: St Peter Port (Guernsey) - La Longue Rocque (aka Grande Pierre) (standing stone)

properties.name: Wedding Party
properties.description: Dressed as a fiddler, the Devil convinced all the guests from a wedding to dance on a Sunday - for the sin, the entire party were turned to stone. Another creation story for the site says a passing giant dropped the contents of his shovel that formed the circle of stones.

properties.name: Giant's Gold
properties.description: Two giants stored their gold in a large, locked chest under the castle. One giant managed to drop the key in the castle moat, where it is said to remain to this day. The chest must be approached with the key in hand, to do so otherwise invites an attack from a huge raven.

properties.name: Giant
properties.description: Tom Hickathrift, the Cambridge giant and hero, is said to have been buried here; his grave marked by a stone eight foot long.

properties.name: Battle
properties.description: Several giants once met on the hill and proceeded to fight. The large stones which are scattered around the area are the leftover missiles that the giants tossed at each other (in reality they are the remains of a hillfort).

properties.name: Gog and Magog
properties.description: Gog and Magog were giants (or one giant, depending on the source) who once lived in this area before Brutus, leader of the Trojans, fought them. Brutus either spared their lives and used them to guard his palace or drowned them in the sea.

properties.name: Jack's Grave
properties.description: These stones mark the final battle between Jack the giant killer and one of his mortal foes - they managed to kill each other in the fight. The body of either Jack or the giant lies under one of the stones.

properties.name: Cursed Motorway
properties.description: A week after a motorway construction team disturbed the graves of eighteen men, some two metres tall, two of the men suffered fatal heart attacks and a night watchman dropped dead at his post. People protesting the construction of the motorway pronounced the incidents 'the curse of the giants'.
properties.location_name: Twyford (Hampshire) - Twyford Down (now area of the M3)

properties.name: Saxi and Herman
properties.description: Saxi and Herman, two giants who were brothers, once lived on the island. They both fell in love with a mermaid who said she would marry whoever would swim to the North Pole with her. Both brothers began the trip, but neither returned.

properties.name: Giant Grave
properties.description: A local giant, murdered by another large fellow, is said to be buried here. A storm is reputed to blow up if the grave is disturbed.

properties.name: Arthur's Enemies
properties.description: A family of giants once lived at Red Castle, until killed by King Arthur and his knights. A nearby brook ran red with blood for three days after the fight.

properties.name: Troll Home
properties.description: Several stories exist around this stone. One says it was thrown into location by an angry giant and became the home of a passing troll. Another says it was the home of a giant and his wife, while a third names the occupant as a dwarf named Trollid.

properties.name: Thrown Stone
properties.description: Battlegore was the landing site of a stone thrown by a giant from West Quantoxhead, competing with the Devil. Old Nick's rock fell short, resulting in his banishment from the area.

properties.name: Long Man of Wilmington
properties.description: This tall hill giant, cut into the soft chalk base, stands around seventy metres in height and is thought to represent any one of several legendary figures. A phantom black dog is also reputed to lurk above the figure, and a local legend says that Arthur fought one of his battles nearby.

properties.name: Romans
properties.description: Phantom soldiers have been reported in this area, while legend says this Iron Age hillfort was built by a race of giants.
properties.location_name: Winterborne St Martin (Dorset) - Maiden Castle (hill fort)

properties.name: Giant Ammunition
properties.description: The massive granite boulders which covered the area were thought to have been summoned by giants, who could call them out of the earth. As a side note, the tin works in the hamlet was once believed to have predated the biblical flood.

properties.name: Headless Hound
properties.description: This headless dog was said to have once belonged to a young giant who ran so fast that he decapitated the dog by pulling too sharply on the leash.

properties.name: Coach with Headless Driver
properties.description: Racked with guilt after deliberately running over his daughter's lover in 1780, this man now continues to drive his coach and horses down the lane, though he has lost his head since death.
properties.location_name: Abington (Northamptonshire) - Pig Lane (lane may no longer exist or has been renamed?)

properties.name: Black Dog
properties.description: Charles Walton (the man murdered in Lower Quinton (see Quinton database entry for further information)) claimed to have seen a phantom black dog for several successive nights when he was younger - on the last night, instead of the dog, he saw a ghostly headless woman. He soon heard that his sister had died at roughly the same time as the last sighting.

properties.name: Headless Man
properties.description: Wayne Anthony, in Haunted Derbyshire and the Peak District, writes that the bridge is said to be haunted by two ghosts. A headless figure is sometimes seen standing on the bridge, while another ghost is seen leaping off the edge into the River Henmore.

properties.name: Twelve Pallbearers
properties.description: These twelve men carrying a coffin are said to be headless. The coffin is also empty, the space reserved for any witnesses to the spectral sight.
properties.location_name: Ashford in the Water (Derbyshire) - Shady Lane, near Thornbridge Hall

properties.name: Mrs Towndrow
properties.description: The headless entity spotted in this location was believed to be the Mrs Towndrow who was murdered in 1841 by her husband John shortly before he killed himself. A local legend states that of you thrice circumambulate an empty stone coffin found in the churchyard before sitting in it, you can hear the restless dead.

properties.name: Figure in Black
properties.description: This shadow of a shambling headless person reportedly stalks the churchyard here.

properties.name: Duke of Suffolk
properties.description: Father of Lady Jane, Henry Grey, Duke of Suffolk, had his head lopped off and now haunts this building (headless, of course).

properties.name: Headless Highwayman
properties.description: This decapitated spectral bandit remains lurking in the shadows of his old ambush area. One version of the story says he remains on his horse.
properties.location_name: Atwick (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Roads in the area

properties.name: Phantom Coach
properties.description: The coach departs the Lions Gate and is driven along the local streets until it reaches the B1383. As normal, the coach driver is said to be headless.
properties.location_name: Audley End (Essex) - Audley End Road & Chestnut Avenue.

properties.name: Burial
properties.description: The largest man made mound in Europe, no one can say for sure why the hill was built, though one group has alleged that it was the only way the builders knew of immobilising a great evil. The ghost of King Sil is also said to ride around the base of the mound on moonlit nights, which is likely to be connected to the belief that a horse and rider of solid gold are contained within. Finally, another ghost, this time headless, is said to haunt the base.

properties.name: Phantom Coach
properties.description: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution.

properties.name: Headless Coachman
properties.description: A headless coachman would drive his vehicle pulled by six decapitated horses around the neighbourhood.

properties.name: Headless Figure
properties.description: A phantom man who haunted the area was banished into the lake by a local priest. At some point during the banishment, the ghost lost his head.

properties.name: Headless Horseman
properties.description: A robber escaping from a house rode into a thin piece of wire tightly pulled between the gateposts here, losing his head in the process. His decapitated shade is either seen or heard at Halloween, still on horseback.
properties.location_name: Ballymena (County Antrim) - Road leading to the White Gates, Crebilly Road area

properties.name: Old Blunderhazard
properties.description: Once per year, just before Christmas, a ghostly member of the Blennerhassett family leaves the village in a coach pulled by headless horses. The phantom would travel to Hassett's Tower in Norwich before returning home before sunrise.

properties.name: Headless Man
properties.description: Having hanged himself, this headless gentleman is reported to still drive his horse and trap around the neighbourhood. One witness heard a horse passing close to her late at night, but nothing could be seen.
properties.date: Unknown, but may have been heard 17 October 2009, around 22:30h

properties.name: Monk
properties.description: An area of the village was said to be haunted by a headless monk.
properties.location_name: Bawnboy (County Cavan) - Exact area not known

properties.name: Headless Horseman
properties.description: Christmas Eve is marked by the arrival of a coach driven by a headless horseman and pulled by headless horses, which travel down the driveway and vanish as they arrive by the front door. Close by, an oak known as Nelson's Tree is reputed to have been used as gallows and is haunted by a woman in white, although it is not clear whether she was one of the tree's victims or mourns the loss of a loved one. In the hall itself, on a wall within in a bedroom cupboard, there is the imprint of the Devil's hoof branded into solid brick. Finally, another tale says there is a window at the hall which always opens itself, even if locked shut.

properties.name: Headless Woman
properties.description: While the smell of lavender is often reported, without any apparent source, the headless ghost, dressed in blue silk, has not been observed for years.

properties.name: Headless Hound
properties.description: It is said that if this large creature follows you, death is not far behind.
properties.location_name: Beetham (Cumbria) - Between village and Milnthrope, and surrounding countryside

properties.name: Phantom Coach
properties.description: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution.

properties.name: Eoghann a' Chin Bhig
properties.description: Eoghann, while living, tried to kill his father - he was decapitated for his efforts. Now he appears before personal disaster or a death in the family.
properties.date: Prior to disaster in the MacLean family
properties.location_name: Ben Buie, Isle of Mull (Argyll and Bute) - General area

properties.name: Headless Monk
properties.description: Once based at Dodnash Priory that once stood nearby, this monk now haunts the road between two villages. A single stone remains at the site of the priory, and it is said treasure is concealed underneath.
properties.location_name: Bentley (Suffolk) - Bridge on road linking Brantham and Bentley

properties.name: Jocelyn Percy
properties.description: Sir Jocelyn has been reported driving his coach and four headless horses around the streets of this otherwise quiet neighbourhood. Another version of the legend says he flies above the street, pausing briefly over an unnamed house.
properties.location_name: Beverley (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Roads in the village

properties.name: Laughing
properties.description: The soft laughter of an invisible female has been heard around the bar, which is unrelated to the headless horseman who lurks outside.

properties.name: Head
properties.description: A free floating decapitated head was seen around the manor house, coinciding with the execution of someone at Parkhurst Prison. The ghost of Charles I is also reported to haunt the manor, as is a monk seen standing along a path near the coach house.

properties.name: Headless Hound
properties.description: Dragging a chain, this decapitated dog crosses the churchyard.

properties.name: Headless Men
properties.description: Two people passing the trump reported seeing several phantom headless men. Treasure is said to be hidden here.

properties.name: Decapitated Rider
properties.description: This unlucky jockey was decapitated by a low hanging branch - he continues to ride the route that killed him.
properties.location_name: Blandford Forum (Dorset) - Edge of Cranborne Chase, Iwerne Steepleton

properties.name: Sheep
properties.description: A herd of phantom, ruminant animals are reputed to walk along this street. Some say the sheep are to be headless, while another version says there is in fact only one sheep (complete with head).
properties.location_name: Blandford Forum (Dorset) - Sheep Market Hill and/or Damory Street

properties.name: Hunt
properties.description: Thought to be part of a phantom hunt, these ghostly dogs have been heard running and yelping through woodland. It is also thought that a headless shuck haunts the area, though it is less sure whether the entity is connected to the hunt pack.
properties.location_name: Blandford Forum (Dorset) - Woods in the area

properties.name: Anne Boleyn
properties.description: To mark the anniversary of her death, a coach pulled by four headless horses pulls up on the driveway, with a decapitated Anne on a seat, her head in her lap. After Anne's headless white ghost arrives, it is said then to climb out, before inspecting each room of the Hall (the place of her birth and childhood). On the same date, Sir Thomas Boleyn is said to drive a team of headless horses in this area, cursed to cross twelve Norfolk bridges (including those at Aylsham, Coltishall and Wroxham).

properties.name: Duelling Knights with Fiery Swords
properties.description: Returning to this realm once a year, the knights are said to be Sir Thomas Boleyn (father of Anne) and Sir Thomas Paston. Their shades battle with burning swords, and Lady Anne watches from a nearby carriage, pulled by headless horses.
properties.location_name: Blofield (Norfolk) - Path leading from Blofield church to St Michael's at Braydeston

properties.name: John Bibby
properties.description: Seen once a year, Bibby rides his coach with his decapitated head in his lap.

properties.name: Headless Coachman
properties.description: A witness is said to have encountered a phantom coach and horses, driven by a headless coachman. The entity vanished just after passing the viewer.

properties.name: Headless Woman
properties.description: A local folk belief placed a headless woman haunting a bushy piece of land.
properties.location_name: Bottesford Beck (Leicestershire) - Close to bridge over the river

properties.name: Mrs Short
properties.description: The ghost of Mrs Short (called the Queen of Hell during her lifetime on account of her furious temper) was said to emerge from the hall's gates in a coach pulled by headless horses. Another report says her ghost was seen in night cap and gown running around the area. Some versions of the tale state the phantom in the coach is a Mr Fitzgerald.
properties.date: Unknown, but said to appear on the stroke of midnight
properties.location_name: Boulge (Suffolk) - Boulge Hall (no longer standing) and general area

properties.name: Roman Battle
properties.description: Headless horses can be seen, and fierce fighting can be heard - the area was the site of a battle between Roman legionnaires and the Britons.

properties.name: Headless Woman
properties.description: A second headless woman was said to haunt another road in this area.
properties.location_name: Bowshaw (Derbyshire) - Unknown road (led to Hullat Hall (likely no longer exists))

properties.name: Headless Woman
properties.description: This decapitated entity moves through the area moaning and crying, according to a local rhyme.

properties.name: Sir Thomas Boleyn Drives Headless Horses
properties.description: The father of Anne Boleyn is said to be cursed with driving his coach over forty (or eleven) Norfolk bridges. One of the passing points is Breckles Hall, where the spectre once scared a poacher to death (another version states that the poacher was taken by the Devil because of his constant law breaking). Locals say that anyone who views the coach is dragged down to hell.

properties.name: Father John
properties.description: One of several spectres alleged to haunt (or have haunted) this location, this figure is said to be benevolent in nature. A former maid has also been seen onsite (she was hanged for theft), a headless man wanders around, and a woman who looks like she originates from the Elizabethan days. Finally, Sir Goddard has been seen walking around - he was sawn in half by angry villagers when it was discovered he ate babies!

properties.name: Headless Lady
properties.description: A phantom funeral procession arrives at the church, though for whom it represents is a mystery. Fields on either side of the church are said to be haunted by a decapitated woman, carrying her head under arm.
properties.location_name: Bretforton (Hereford & Worcester) - Church, and surrounding area

properties.name: White Calf
properties.description: The phantom white calf (also known as the Lackey Causey calf), sometimes headless, was said to emerge from a tunnel and attempt to lure people into the water, though the creature's entrance may no longer exist.
properties.location_name: Brigg (Lincolnshire) - Between Brigg and Wrawby, near a stream

properties.name: Footsteps
properties.description: The sound of disembodied footsteps is said to haunt this hotel, with a phantom headless monk and ghostly little girl also present.

properties.name: Headless Figure
properties.description: This entity is reputed to just stand motionless in the field. Just like a scarecrow.
properties.location_name: Brightwalton (Berkshire) - Field near the Marquis of Granby pub

properties.name: Decapitated Horseman
properties.description: Ashton Court is best known for its ghostly headless horseman, though there are also reports of grey ladies and a phantom hound.

properties.name: Headless Nun
properties.description: This pub is reputedly haunted by one or more headless nuns. Mild poltergeist phenomenon has also been reported in the building, as has the sighting of a ghostly little girl.

properties.name: Figure with Child
properties.description: A national tabloid carried some highly suspect video created by a builder who claimed he had filmed a headless phantom carrying a child.

properties.name: Headless Horseman
properties.description: Students have reported seeing a headless horseman patrolling this area late at night.
properties.location_name: Bristol (Somerset) - University of Bristol and Tyndall, Avenue area

properties.name: Swerving Coach
properties.description: This coach and four is said to charge at oncoming traffic, causing accidents and chaos. Some have also reported a headless huntsman in the same zone. A phantom horseman is also said to appear on moonless nights, and finally the area is home to a 'bounding' ghost, a figure that runs ahead of people, only to vanish without trace.
properties.date: Horseman at midnight on moonless nights, others unknown
properties.location_name: Brockley (Somerset) - Road leading to Brockley Combe

properties.name: Dullahan
properties.description: Riding headless on a black horse, the Dullahan are said to be bearers of ill-omen. The entity carries his severed, decaying head in his right hand. Wherever the Dullahan stops, it is said that a mortal will shortly die on that spot.
properties.location_name: Bryansford (County Down) - Hill between Bryansford and Moneyscalp

properties.name: Cwn Annwn
properties.description: A driver passing through Buckholt in 1961 reported seeing a large black hound, believed to be Cwn Annwn, a spectral hound of Annwn, although some people believe it to be a ghostly donkey. More recently, although no date has been mentioned, the hound has been seen in a nearby cottage which is also haunted by two phantom people (one short, one headless). Finally, a witness encountered the face of a beautiful woman along the road. The witness apologised and stepped to one side before realising that there was no one else present.
properties.date: 1961 onwards (hound), face sighting possibly mid twentieth century

properties.name: Sir Thomas
properties.description: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven (or twelve, or forty) bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution.

properties.name: Headless Horse
properties.description: The result of a Danish skirmish over a thousand years ago, this decapitated horse runs along the lanes surrounding the area.
properties.location_name: Burnham Green (Hertfordshire) - Roads in the area

properties.name: Headless Airman
properties.description: An airman has been observed standing around with no head - it is thought that he was decapitated as he tried to bail prior to crash landing.

properties.name: Headless Shadow
properties.description: One witness described this entity as a black and grey shadow without a head. Thankfully, the ghost is more likely to manifest phantom footsteps along the corridors.
properties.location_name: Buxton (Derbyshire) - Newhaven Inn, between Buxton and Ashbourne

properties.name: Phantom Coach
properties.description: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution.

properties.name: Coach Circling the Courtyard
properties.description: Warning about the encroaching death of a family member, a horse drawn hearse driven by a headless horseman passes through the gates of the castle and circles the courtyard seven times.
properties.date: Warning of a death in the family
properties.location_name: Caister-On-Sea (Norfolk) - Caister Castle, one mile west of Caister-On-Sea

properties.name: Walter Calverley
properties.description: A temporary fit of madness resulted in this Elizabethan gentleman killing two of his children and stabbing his wife (who escaped by feigning death). His regretful spirit rides the countryside on a headless horse, although one source says that it had been laid. Poltergeist-like activities have also been reported at the house.
properties.location_name: Calverley (West Yorkshire) - Calverley House (currently called Old Hall?), surrounding countryside

properties.name: Headless Horseman
properties.description: Galloping across the parkland under the light of the moon, this rider could be searching for his missing appendage. Some also say that the area is haunted by a coven of witches.

properties.name: Headless Horseman
properties.description: Emerging from a deep pit near to Cantley, this headless spirit on horseback rides off towards the nearby village.

properties.name: Headless Man
properties.description: Several entities were reported in the Cumberland News to haunt this station, including a headless man on platform eight, another man who points towards the Undercroft, and a woman in a veil who travels along corridors.

properties.name: Headless Woman
properties.description: This site was said to be haunted by a murdered woman whose headless ghost walked the area, although any witness accounts appear to be scarce.

properties.name: Headless Figure
properties.description: A tall headless figure is said to haunt the valley during the winter months.

properties.name: Headless Figure
properties.description: A headless figure wearing an old RAF uniform was reputedly seen holding their helmet under their arm.
properties.location_name: Catterick (North Yorkshire) - Officers' Mess, Marne Barracks

properties.name: Headless Monk
properties.description: A water ride at the park was moved after staff reported encountering a headless monk, amongst other paranormal phenomena. The foundations of the ride were said to have intruded on an ancient burial site. The park has been accused of holding a publicity stunt, having used another ghost story previously to promote its 'Saw' ride.

properties.name: Royalist
properties.description: A headless Royalist soldier is said to haunt the building where he lost his head after a cannon ball hit him during his stay. The site is also reputed to be haunted by a grey lady.

properties.name: Lord Shaftesbury
properties.description: The first Lord Shaftesbury is driven in his coach by a headless horseman around this area. The horseman has his head under one arm, which still wears his hat!

properties.name: Headless Horse
properties.description: A woman and her daughter reported seeing a headless horse in a field close to Junction 29.

properties.name: Trotting
properties.description: The clip-clop of phantom hooves has been reported in this area, normally accompanied by a strange pale mist which (it is said) sometimes transforms into a horse with a headless rider.

properties.name: Sound of a Horse
properties.description: The driver of a horse and cart and a passenger were left shaken after encountering the sounds of a horse which passed them, and although nothing could be seen, the sounds were accompanied by a cold gust of wind. A murder had occurred along the road a few years previous, where a woman on horseback was decapitated by a former lover.

properties.name: Couple
properties.description: One person claimed to have seen a man and woman walking on the bridge before they vanished. A short time later the same witness watched a lady in grey clothing flee from the direction of the castle, again vanishing, but reappearing by the river, headless.

properties.name: Treasure Guardian
properties.description: A decapitated female ghost was reported to stand guard over a hidden hoard of gold near the tower of this building.

properties.name: Headless Nun
properties.description: A witness reported that, while taking a short cut across the area at night, he spotted a figure dressed as a nun, but with the head missing. The figure stopped and vanished in front of the witness. The witness later told a friend who said that his brother has also encountered the entity.
properties.location_name: Colchester (Essex) - Area close to Birch Brook, no longer accessible

properties.name: James Parnell, Quaker Martyr
properties.description: James Parnell, Quaker was arrested in 1652 for the heinous crime of being 'idle and disorderly' after upsetting a local priest. Parnell was imprisoned in Colchester Castle, where folklore says he was placed in a small room and forced to climb a rope each time he wished to eat. After a few months of this punishing routine, Parnell eventually slipped during his daily climb and suffered injuries which killed him. Parnell's ghost was said to haunt the dungeon (although it is worth noting that he was imprisoned in a crevice in the castle wall). There is a story that a foolhardy man once stayed the night in the dungeon as a wager, only to emerge the following morning a gibbering wreck. Another tale states that the castle is haunted by a headless nun, although her story is not known.

properties.name: Phantom Coach
properties.description: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution. A headless Black Shuck is also reputed to cross this bridge once per night.

properties.name: Headless Hound
properties.description: The black shuck which walks this area is instantly recognisable as the creature is missing a head.

properties.name: Sir George Sydenham
properties.description: Sir George is reported to ride his headless horse up and down the hills near the village. Combe Sydenham is also a place where the Wild Hunt has been reported, always heard as locals were too afraid to look (and rightly so!).

properties.name: Headless Woman
properties.description: The terrifying shade of the headless woman walks the hill near the gateway of where this castle stands. The same ghost may also be responsible for the lights that flit across the ruins at night, or they could be caused by phantasmal Royalists that used the castle in 1643. The sound of a galloping phantom horse has been heard at the bottom of the castle hill. A cloaked figure with a brown floppy hat was observed by a local man in 2009 while he was picking worms for fishing. The figure was in the middle of a field but vanished after the witness took his eyes off the man for a few seconds.
properties.date: Woman and horse unknown, male figure seen 15 May 2009, around 01:30h

properties.name: Headless Piper
properties.description: How this phantom headless piper can pipe without a head has yet to be answered.

properties.name: Lady Snagge
properties.description: While riding her horse, Lady Snagge was decapitated by a thin piece of rope pulled across the road by robbers who plundered her body before leaving it. Snagge's ghost is still reported as it repeats her final journey, sometimes with head intact, and sometimes without.

properties.name: Horse
properties.description: A headless horse is reputed to gallop along in this area.

properties.name: Possibly Mary
properties.description: The headless woman reported in the castle grounds could be Mary Queen of Scots, though her relationship with the building appears to be minimal. Workmen on the site claimed to have seen a figure in the ruins and heard voices. A visitor alone in the caponier during the 1990s could hear a woman singing, although another visitor nearby failed to hear anything. Another story says that a Historic Scotland work crew could hear the sounds of a banquet emerging from the hall but could see no one there.

properties.name: Green Lady
properties.description: This ghostly tall woman, wearing a green dress, is said to search the graveyard for the remains of her Royalist husband. The second myth from this church concerns a great treasure that is said to have been buried here along with human remains from a Civil War battle. Unfortunately, the hoard is protected by a headless phantom that intervenes if anyone comes too close to discovering it.

properties.name: Hearse
properties.description: Some accidents occurring along this road have been blamed on the phantom black coach pulled by headless horses that runs wild and driverless.
properties.location_name: Cutcombe (Somerset) - A396, between village and Timberscombe

properties.name: Changing Entity
properties.description: The large black dog once said to haunt this area could also take the form of a flaming headless man, a headless woman, a white cat, or a rabbit.
properties.location_name: Darlington (Durham) - Glassensikes (stream or river) and Harewood Hill area

properties.name: Old Pinkney
properties.description: The manor was haunted by an entity which wore a red cap and went under the nickname Old Pinkney. A well under the house was known as Pinkney's Well and was never used after dusk. The surrounding area was also said to be haunted by a headless man.
properties.location_name: Darlington (Durham) - Old manor house (no longer standing?), Blackwell

properties.name: Clibbon
properties.description: Found guilty of highway robbery, Clibbon was tied to the back of a horse and dragged around the town until he was dead. His screams are sometimes heard. The same area is also home to a headless horse, though it may not be the same creature associated with the highwayman.

properties.name: Decapitated Man
properties.description: A headless gentleman walks around this road.

properties.name: Black Monk
properties.description: One of the corridors in this building, built on the site of a friary, is haunted by a monk dressed in black robes. Other witnesses claim to have seen more than one monk here, sometimes headless. Another shade is that of a Victorian gentleman, who may have killed himself on site.
properties.location_name: Derby (Derbyshire) - Friary Hotel, currently the Friar Gate Hotel

properties.name: Lady in Blue
properties.description: Around 400 years old, the Jacobean House is home to a headless coachman who drives his carriage through a former archway of the building. Also seen is a lady in blue, who descends the main staircase, while another man (believed to have committed suicide) has also been spotted on the upper floors of the building.
properties.location_name: Derby (Derbyshire) - The Haus, once known as the Jacobean House

properties.name: Runaway Horses
properties.description: It is said eight hundred men died at the Battle of Roundway Hill in 1643, after a battlefield manoeuvre which trapped cavalry in a slopped area. The men and horses were slaughtered, and since the battle ghostly horses have been seen galloping around the area. A headless ghost was once also said to walk the area, possibly linked to a barrow on the site, as is the phantom hound which drags a chain from its neck.
properties.location_name: Devizes (Wiltshire) - Oliver's Castle, aka Roundway Castle, aka Rundaway Castle - Bloody Ditch

properties.name: Wish Hounds
properties.description: The local shucks, known as wish hounds, reportedly stalk these roads. One of them is said to be headless.
properties.location_name: Ditchling (Sussex) - Road between town & Westmeston, and nearby downs

properties.name: Mr Cobbe
properties.description: The ghostly Mr Cobbe drives his coach drawn by six headless horses close to the park. One version of the story says he is no longer seen, but the phantom's presence is marked by the sound of a gate opening and shutting.
properties.location_name: Donabate (County Dublin) - Newbridge House and park

properties.name: Black Coach
properties.description: A farmer returning home encountered a coach pulled by four headless horses, which pulled away from the Court's driveway at breakneck speed. The man later learned that Viscount Doneraile died just before his encounter. The ghost of Lady Casteltown has been reported within the building, as has her husband.
properties.location_name: Doneraile (County Cork) - Area outside Doneraile Court

properties.name: Coach in the House
properties.description: One owner of the property won a large amount of money when he drove a horse and coach up the stairs inside the house - his victorious ghost reportedly re-enacts the antic. A separate entity is that of a headless grey woman, a member of the Trenchard family who slit her own throat, while a priest is said to haunt the gatehouse (where he was held prisoner).

properties.name: Headless Woman
properties.description: The PIT (Paranormal Investigation Team) group claimed to have photographed two entities within the museum, Judge George Jeffreys and Mary Anning. The latter was headless in her picture.

properties.name: Rider
properties.description: A decapitated rider on horseback follows a road that no longer exists - the figure crosses the gravestones and vanishes through a hedge.

properties.name: Headless Drummer
properties.description: The headless drummer boy is said to date from the Napoleonic period. A lady in red and figure in blue have also been reported, while the tunnels under the castle are haunted by Second World War soldiers and operatives, perhaps escapees from Dunkirk - one figure in khaki and with a blurred face was seen walking purposefully along a corridor in 2013. The Arthurian hero Sir Gawain is reportedly buried here.

properties.name: Headless Woman
properties.description: Not much more is known about the phantom headless woman who haunts the junction.
properties.location_name: Dronfield (Derbyshire) - Place 'where three roads meet' between village and Coal Ashon

properties.name: Headless Horseman
properties.description: This decapitated figure rides along the road once darkness falls, and is thought to be an ill omen for any witness, or one of their family.
properties.location_name: Dublin (County Dublin) - Roper's Rest, leading to Love Lane

properties.name: Archdeacon Bulkeley?
properties.description: Said to either be driven by or containing Archdeacon Bulkeley, a coach drawn by six headless horses containing two passengers together with two footmen, drives through this area of Dublin. Anyone who spots the coach is said to die within 366 days.
properties.location_name: Dublin (County Dublin) - Area around Old Bawn Road and the Dodder

properties.name: Matching Band
properties.description: During the Easter Rising of 1916, the family and staff at Corkagh House heard a band playing and the sound of marching feet heading towards the house. Those present thought they would be attacked but the sounds suddenly stopped and nothing could be seen. More recently, the park is reputedly home to a headless horseman and/or the sound of horses hooves' heard along an area known as the 'Devils Passageway'.
properties.location_name: Dublin (County Dublin) - Corkagh Estate (now Corkagh Park)

properties.name: Invaders
properties.description: Men who tried to invade the castle were executed on site and their headless remains buried within the grounds. Sometimes their spirits are said to wander.

properties.name: Buck Jones
properties.description: Riding headless on the back of a white horse, this phantom is named as Buck Jones, former City Sheriff (though the man died in a debtor's prison).

properties.name: Headless Rider
properties.description: This decapitated male wearing a flowing cape passes through the village on an off-white horse.

properties.name: Headless Dorothy
properties.description: A story says that young Dorothy was accidentally decapitated by her father as he tried to save her from a group of drunken Roundheads. Her headless form is now reported to drift aimlessly around the village. The local pub is named after her sad tale.

properties.name: Squire Fulford
properties.description: Driving a coach pulled by four headless horsemen, the squire passes though the village only at certain times of the year.

properties.name: Headless Soldier
properties.description: A barmaid was left disturbed after encountering a headless man in the bar area. The decapitated figure wore a Civil War uniform.
properties.location_name: Dunstable (Bedfordshire) - Black Horse public house (no longer operational)

properties.name: Coach
properties.description: Seen galloping towards the hall, this coach is pulled by four black, headless horses. One legend says the coach contains a coffin.

properties.name: Annie Chapman
properties.description: Annie Chapman was murdered by Jack the Ripper at 29 Hanbury Street, and it was said that she haunted the area where she was killed, her phantom accompanied by a man. On one occasion during the 1930s, a witness could hear her murder, but could see nothing at the site. A phantom, headless body in the area is also said to be Annie. 29 Hanbury Street has since been demolished.
properties.date: 1930s, reputed to be more common in the autumn

properties.name: Headless Airman
properties.description: Seen parachuting down from the skies, this decapitated airman is thought to date from the Second World War.
properties.location_name: East Cowes (Isle of Wight) - Skies above area

properties.name: Headless Shuck
properties.description: Manifesting on dark stormy nights, this shuck brings death to those who see him within a year.
properties.location_name: East Runton (Norfolk) - Runton Heath, between East Runton and Aylmerton

properties.name: Headless Duke
properties.description: The Duke of Norfolk, who spent much of his time and money here, lost his head after upsetting Queen Elizabeth and now ascends the staircase of the great hall. Outside the buildings, a monk has been seen flitting between the courtyards of Washhouse and Masters.

properties.name: Priest with Black Hair
properties.description: This holy man has been observed standing over the tomb of a former bishop, maybe paying his last respects? A headless figure has been reported at least once in the building.
properties.location_name: EC3 (Greater London) - St Magnus the Martyr Church, Fish Street Hill

properties.name: Stretcher
properties.description: A guard on duty reported seeing a group of people in old uniforms walking towards the entrance to the Tower carrying a stretcher containing a headless body. The figures faded away just before they reached the soldier.
properties.location_name: EC3 (Greater London) - Tower of London, main entrance

properties.name: Figures
properties.description: A shocked sentry was found repeating 'Man in Cloak' - after he regained his senses, the guard claimed to have been approached by a headless cloaked figure, the sight of which caused the sentry to panic and run to find help.
properties.location_name: EC3 (Greater London) - Tower of London, Water Lane

properties.name: Headless Woman with Child
