During the mid-twentieth century a team of workmen camping on the grounds heard the bell pealing, even though it had been removed several years previously.
properties.location_name: Watton (East Riding of Yorkshire) - Watton Abbey

properties.name: Headless Man
properties.description: The discovery of human remains near the bridge in the mid-twentieth century resulted in a ghostly apparition appearing for a few weeks after the incident. Earlier in the twentieth century, a criminal spotted a well-dressed young man on the bridge. As the criminal tried to grab the man, the young potential victim vanished.

properties.name: Rising Woman
properties.description: Several guards stationed at the barracks reported the ghost of a headless woman, which rose from the ground in front of them wearing a striped dress. The figure then set off towards the park.
properties.location_name: Wellington (Somerset) - Barracks, and St James Park

properties.name: Headless Horse
properties.description: The headless horse which runs along this road was said to have been a casualty during the civil war.

properties.name: Dragon Slayer
properties.description: A dragon which was given the locals a hard time was killed by an ex-soldier and his dog - the dog leapt onto the creature's face, while the man decapitated the wyrm.
properties.location_name: West Clandon (Surrey) - Exact location unknown, but woodcarving in local church

properties.name: Headless Driver
properties.description: A ghostly coach pulled by horses and driven by a headless coachman reportedly travels along this lane. The coachman was said to have been decapitated during a highway robbery.
properties.location_name: West Lulworth (Dorset) - Lane along Coach Lane Gates (gates may no longer stand)

properties.name: Headless Horseman
properties.description: A headless horseman is reported to haunt Westcott. In 2007, one man walking between Westcott and Dorking was followed by something unseen which pushed its way through thick bushes and made a trotting sound.
properties.location_name: Westcott (Surrey) - Road between Westcott and Dorking

properties.name: Woman in the Bedroom
properties.description: The ghost of a woman in one of the bedrooms is said to guarantee a sleepless night. There are also stories of a headless person who silently walks the manor house at night.

properties.name: Headless Woman
properties.description: This entity wanders the village roads, less her head.

properties.name: Coach and Four
properties.description: It was once said that a ghostly coach pulled by four horses and driven by a headless figure would pass through the village and vanish into the church wall.

properties.name: Headless Man
properties.description: This male entity has the removed item securely grasped under his arm.

properties.name: Petrified Snakes
properties.description: The abbey was besieged by snakes, until St Hilda turned up and drove them all off the edge of the cliff, where they turned to stone on the beach below. Her ghost is also reported to still gaze from the windows of the abbey. A few people have heard a choir singing in the empty building and one local legend says a team of headless horses pulling a hearse was once spotted by a man and boy walking in the area. Finally, treasure hidden in the abbey's grounds is protected by a headless figure in white.
properties.date: Snakes unknown, St Hilda's ghost during summer months 10:00h - 12:00h

properties.name: Headless Woman
properties.description: A local legend says that, during the mid-nineteenth century, this woman was decapitated with a scythe after a jealous former boyfriend became overly upset when he discovered her engagement to another. The entity is said to haunt the staircase and, according to one story, once seen by a party of six people staying at the building.

properties.name: Many Headless
properties.description: A story which was told in the late nineteenth century says that a large hole once existed here. Occasionally at midnight headless horses driven by headless men would emerge.

properties.name: Headless Bride
properties.description: The decapitated ghost caused one witness to faint as it ascended the staircase and headed towards the viewer. A local legend states that the ghostly woman and her lover both committed suicide the day before she was due to be married to another man. The building has a history of phantom footsteps and doors that open themselves.
properties.date: Twentieth century, particularly active around Christmas

properties.name: Monk
properties.description: The headless ghost which was said to haunt the ruins was thought to be a monk. There was a local myth that a tunnel connected the manor to the church, and that the headless spirit would vanish into the concealed entrance.

properties.name: Monk-Like Figure
properties.description: One occupant of this property twice spotted a small monk-like figure wearing brown run across the landing from a bedroom to their parent's room. On another occasion, the witness awoke to see a projection of light on the wall showing a dozen decapitated people pleading and speaking French.
properties.location_name: Withington (Gloucestershire) - Terrace house (now demolished), Hill Street

properties.name: Headless Horseman
properties.description: This decapitated figure on the back of a stallion still rides around the area where Crowden Hall once stood.
properties.location_name: Woodhead (Derbyshire) - Area where Crowden Hall once stood (demolished 1937)

properties.name: Headless Horses
properties.description: Rumoured to be a battleground between Britons and Romans, the sounds of battle, accompanied by the site of headless horses, have been reported. The battle was said to be so intense, the valley filled with blood.
properties.location_name: Woodmanton (Wiltshire) - Location known as Patty's Bottom

properties.name: Coach
properties.description: A coach which manifested here was said to glow from the windows and lamps with a light as bright as day. Witness accounts varied to whether it was pulled by invisible horses or headless horses.
properties.date: Unknown, likely to be pre-twentieth century

properties.name: Headless Man
properties.description: This headless ghost disappeared soon after a decapitated skeleton was discovered in the wall of the farmhouse and laid to rest.

properties.name: Phantom Coach
properties.description: A ghostly coach and four passes over this bridge once a year. It is said to be driven by a headless Sir Thomas Boleyn and is one of eleven bridges that he passes over on the night of his daughter Anne's execution.

properties.name: Headless Dog
properties.description: The site of a murder which took place many years ago was said to have become home to a headless black dog.
properties.location_name: Yeaton (Shropshire) - Road between village and Baschurch

properties.name: Headless Woman
properties.description: A wealthy woman was murdered for her money, and her body hidden a short distance from her house in Bishopthorpe. It was several days before the corpse was discovered, and it is said that the state of decay was so bad that her head had become separate from her torso. Her headless ghost haunted the area where her body lay for years after the event.

properties.name: Thomas Percy
properties.description: Local folklore says that a decapitated Thomas Percy, the Seventh Earl of Northumberland, fumbles around the churchyard searching for his severed head. Thomas and head separated after upsetting Elizabeth I; the earl attempted and failed to raise an army against his queen and was executed for this error of judgement. More recently, a ghostly figure in a long gown (possibly a nun) has been observed on the site.
properties.location_name: York (North Yorkshire) - Holy Trinity Church, Goodramgate

properties.name: Evil Hill
properties.description: The area was once thought to be a place of evil - no birds would sing in the area, and it was thought haunted by a headless hound. A headless man was also reported in the region.

properties.name: Bottomless Pit
properties.description: This small body of water is said to be a one way ticket to Hell for anyone foolish enough to try to swim to the bottom.
properties.location_name: Albury (Hertfordshire) - Halls Garden Pond near the church

properties.name: Hell Gate
properties.description: Dundermot Mound is reported to be the location of a secret entrance to hell - occasionally it opens, releasing demons who take any witnesses straight to the underworld.

properties.name: Sir Thomas Boleyn Drives Headless Horses
properties.description: The father of Anne Boleyn is said to be cursed with driving his coach over forty (or eleven) Norfolk bridges. One of the passing points is Breckles Hall, where the spectre once scared a poacher to death (another version states that the poacher was taken by the Devil because of his constant law breaking). Locals say that anyone who views the coach is dragged down to hell.

properties.name: Misty Figures
properties.description: A couple walking from Thornton Watlass spotted a few misty figures moving around the wood. It is said that the Devil would use the woods as a hiding place.

properties.name: Devil's Chimney
properties.description: This limestone outcrop is said to lead straight to hell.

properties.name: Hobgoblin Builders
properties.description: Warlock Sir Hugo de Giffard is said to have made a pact with the Devil, and in return had an army of Hobgoblins build this castle for him. A staircase in the hall was filled with earth as it was believed it led straight to hell.
properties.date: Still present, although only subterranean 'Goblin Hall remains'

properties.name: Urisk
properties.description: Evidence that shows this part fairy, part human hybrid still to be in the area occasionally turns up. A barrow known locally as the 'Mound of the Dead' is thought to be an entrance to Hell.

properties.name: Suicidal Screams
properties.description: A man once killed himself by leaping off the cliffs at Gwithian after he found his home burnt to the ground and his sister missing. As stormy weather approaches, the man's screams can once more be heard as they bounce off the steep cliff sides at the area known as Hell's Mouth.
properties.date: Weather Dependent: Prior to a storm

properties.name: Escape Route
properties.description: While combing her hair nearby, Saint Menfre was confronted by the Devil. She threw her comb at him, striking with such force he dug Lundy Hole in his haste to return to hell.

properties.name: Vanishing Leapfroggers
properties.description: A stone marks the spot where the Devil played a game of leapfrog with three local lads - when they jumped over his back, a hole to hell opened, and they were never seen again.
properties.location_name: Marston Moretaine (Bedfordshire) - Devil's Stone, also known as Devil's Jump Stone

properties.name: Laird Beardie
properties.description: Beardie played cards against the Devil and lost - the fateful game is now replayed once a year, though it is warned that anyone watching the game will also travel to hell with the Laird at the end of the match.

properties.name: Padfeet
properties.description: A gorge or hillside chasm was home to the neighbourhood's demonic hounds.
properties.location_name: Saddleworth (Greater London) - Gorge, was known as Hell Mouth

properties.name: Hell Hole
properties.description: An entrance to the lower world was said to be located somewhere in Sheffield.
properties.location_name: Sheffield (South Yorkshire) - Somewhere between Neepsend Bicarage and Rutland Road

properties.name: Dando
properties.description: Dando was a monk and a bit of a rogue - he enjoyed hunting on a Sunday. One Sabbath he was out hunting for hare when the Devil appeared and grabbed all the game Dando had hanging from his horse. Old Nick ran, Dando in hot pursuit - unfortunately, the monk lost his balance on the bank of the Tiddy and fell in. The waters turned to steam, and Dando was taken to hell.
properties.location_name: St Germans (Cornwall) - Erth Hill, near the rivers of Tiddy and Lynher

properties.name: A Woman Scorned
properties.description: Scorned by a knight, a woman invited the warrior and his new love to meet here. The woman murdered the couple, and as a storm developed, the Devil appeared and took the murderess into a nearby hole (presumably to hell).

properties.name: Marshy Bells
properties.description: The Devil once appeared in the village, stealing the church bells and sinking them into the nearby marsh. Bubbles in the marsh were said to be the result of the bells continuing to sink into the bottomless pit.
properties.location_name: Tunstall (Norfolk) - Church, and marsh known as Bell Hole (and Hell Hole)

properties.name: Hellsmouth
properties.description: A muddy pool located near the farm was thought to be an entrance to hell, from where ghosts and ghouls could come and go as they pleased.

properties.name: Golden Cradle
properties.description: The hidden golden cradle was said to have once rocked the children of kings. The small loch in the area is said to fill some people with dread, and if you run around it nine times recanting a certain spell, a hand shall rise and drag you in.
properties.location_name: Abernethy (Perth and Kinross) - Castle Law (aka Castlelaw)

properties.name: Iron Chest
properties.description: Protecting their hidden hoard of money, if anyone comes too close to discovering this secret stash a white figure appears and cries out 'That's mine!'.

properties.name: Giant's Treasure
properties.description: The giant's treasure slipped from his grasp as he crossed the area, the hoard sinking into the ground and becoming covered in rocks. The giant had to leave the treasure behind, but it was written that a mortal man could recover the wealth if a fairy in the form of a chicken or an ape appeared; the man would have to reach out and grab the treasure without making a sound, and if he failed to do so, the fairy would never appear to him again.
properties.location_name: Addleborough (North Yorkshire) - Barrow in the area

properties.name: Cockerel
properties.description: A demonic cockerel stands guard over a hidden treasure hoard within the hill - the last man to discover it was eaten by the bird.
properties.location_name: Alcocks Arbour (Warwickshire) - Area around the hill

properties.name: Devil's Trackway
properties.description: This causeway was said to have been constructed by the devil. There are also reports of buried treasure in the area.

properties.name: Dragon Stone
properties.description: A farmer watched in horror as his horses and plough were sucked underground in the middle of a field - a few seconds later a large dragon emerged and flew off. The stone that remains today is said to cover the dragon's treasure. Some believe the dragon was in fact Satan.
properties.location_name: Anwick (Lincolnshire) - Drake Stone, currently in the churchyard

properties.name: Golden Idol
properties.description: Hunted for by many a treasure seeker, still yet to be discovered, a large statue made from solid gold is supposed to be hidden in the lands here.

properties.name: Clashing Blades
properties.description: A member of the Ghost Club and his partner reported being surrounded by the sounds of men fighting, sword clashing with sword, and the moans of the dying. There are also tales of a golden coffin and stolen treasure buried in the area.

properties.name: Pointing Figure
properties.description: After discovering 175 Anglo-Saxon coins with their metal detectors in the woodland, Barrie Thomson and John Reeves said they had experienced unexplained happenings during their search. Thomson also reported multiple dreams of a white figure who pointed towards the treasure.

properties.name: Horse and Rider
properties.description: This stone is carved with scenes which could depict an ancient battle. One legend says that at certain times of the year a horseman manifests and gallops around it. Another story states that a man named Michael will one day find two pots of gold under the stone.

properties.name: Burial
properties.description: The largest man made mound in Europe, no one can say for sure why the hill was built, though one group has alleged that it was the only way the builders knew of immobilising a great evil. The ghost of King Sil is also said to ride around the base of the mound on moonlit nights, which is likely to be connected to the belief that a horse and rider of solid gold are contained within. Finally, another ghost, this time headless, is said to haunt the base.

properties.name: Free Money
properties.description: Lucky people are said to be able to find money which has been left by the fairies, but if they failed to add a coin of their own to the hoard they had found, everything would slip away.
properties.location_name: Bamburgh (Northumberland) - Rocky outcrop on which castle is built

properties.name: Money Stone
properties.description: A rock in the centre of the road was known locally as the Money Stone and according to legend was used to mark the location of buried treasure. People had tried to dig up the hoard but were always scared off by a supernatural presence.

properties.name: Tall Woman
properties.description: The spirit that harassed a family in the unnamed building only ceased manifesting and creating loud bangs when they discovered a large sum of hidden money and paid off her debts.

properties.name: The Glass Tower
properties.description: As well as the final resting place of King Arthur, Merlin is said to have kept the Thirteen Treasures of Britain within his glass tower that stood on Bardsey Island. These treasures were: 1) Dyrnwyn - the sword's blade would burst into flame when wielded by royalty, 2) The Hamper of Gwyddno Garanhir - the hamper would multiply its contents by five-score, 3) The Horn of Bran - this drinking beaker would provide whatever the user wanted to whet their lips, 4) The Chariot of Morgan the Wealthy - extremely fast magical travel, 5) The Halter of Clyno Eiddyn - any horse dreamt of would appear haltered in the morning, 6) The Knife of Llawfronedd the Horseman - would carve the meat at a meal for twenty four men, 7) The Cauldron of Diwrnach - would only boil water for a brave man, 8) The Whetstone of Tudwal Tudglyd - when used to sharpen the sword of a hero, any strike made by the weapon would be fatal, 9) The Coat of Padarn Redcoat - would only fit anyone of noble blood, 10) The Crock of Rhygenydd - would serve any food the user wanted, 11) The Dish of Rhygenydd - would serve any food the user desired, 12) The Chessboard of Gwenddolau - would play itself once set up, & 13) The Mantle of Arthur - would make the wearer invisible.
properties.location_name: Bardsey Island (Gwynedd) - Exact location not known

properties.name: Cromwell's Wealth
properties.description: A chest full of treasure is said to have been hidden by Oliver Cromwell in this area. It has also been said that people have reported feeling something brush against them while on one of the hills.
properties.location_name: Bartlow (Cambridgeshire) - Bartlow Hills, aka Three Hills

properties.name: Bags of Gold
properties.description: The ghost of a former miller would hold down a flagstone, under which several bags of gold were said to be hidden. The hoard was only said to be accessible at the stroke of midnight during a full moon, when the phantom miller would take his ghostly horses to a pond to water.
properties.location_name: Bearley (was Bearly) (Warwickshire) - Windmill (current status not known)

properties.name: Helpful Fairies
properties.description: The little people here are traditionally helpful in nature, and once fixed a plough for a farmer. When archaeologists tried to dig up the barrow in 1850, a storm blew up and drove them away. A golden coffin is said to be buried in the area.

properties.name: Red Caps
properties.description: In an unnamed field in this area treasure is reputedly buried. Two fairy-like entities wearing red caps are said to occasionally appear, searching for the hoard.
properties.location_name: Beelsby (Lincolnshire) - Unnamed field in the area

properties.name: Golden Plough
properties.description: This barrow was once thought to contain a golden plough.

properties.name: Headless Monk
properties.description: Once based at Dodnash Priory that once stood nearby, this monk now haunts the road between two villages. A single stone remains at the site of the priory, and it is said treasure is concealed underneath.
properties.location_name: Bentley (Suffolk) - Bridge on road linking Brantham and Bentley

properties.name: Golden Coffin
properties.description: A golden coffin is reputed to be buried in the area. Another legend says that if you walk around the hill seven times while swearing, the Devil will appear to grant you a single wish.
properties.location_name: Berwick St John (Wiltshire) - Winklebury Camp, also known as Winkelbury Camp

properties.name: Williams' Treasure
properties.description: Archbishop Williams appeared twice after his death to the female owner of a shop built where his house once stood. Williams instructed the woman to dig in a certain place in her garden, but she refused. Many years later, a father and son digging in the garden stumbled upon Williams' hidden hoard.
properties.location_name: Bethesda (Gwynedd) - Site of a house once owned by Dr John Williams

properties.name: Coffin
properties.description: A golden coffin is said to be buried in this long barrow.

properties.name: Hording Lizard
properties.description: A dragon was said to reside in a tumulus, protecting its hoard.
properties.location_name: Bilsdale (North Yorkshire) - Exact location not known

properties.name: Headless Men
properties.description: Two people passing the trump reported seeing several phantom headless men. Treasure is said to be hidden here.

properties.name: Normal Treasure
properties.description: It is said that treasure remains under these barrows, though there is no evidence to support this.
properties.location_name: Bloxworth (Dorset) - Barrows on private field (currently in varying states of damage)

properties.name: Golden Coffin
properties.description: Protected by a flock of birds, two golden coffins were believed to be buried under the cairns.

properties.name: Treasure
properties.description: Who Uncle Ned was is unknown, but his name is remembered as a local legend says he buried his treasure somewhere near the rock which bears his name.
properties.location_name: Bolenowe Carn Moor (Cornwall) - Uncle Ned's Rock

properties.name: Romans hiding Treasure
properties.description: Murdered by an angry mob, these Romans all died before any could reveal where they stashed their gold. While their ghosts are now said to show the location of the treasure, those who see them die before they can share the secret.

properties.name: Never-ending Flow
properties.description: The harbour was created when a warlock attempted to recover lost treasure from a well - the well's guardian was angered and caused the water to ceaselessly flow.

properties.name: Treasure
properties.description: Treasure was said to have been buried under a tree which once stood here. The only way to uproot the tree and recover the hoard was to chain twelve white oxen to the tree and have the creatures uproot it. Unfortunately, the tree was chopped down, so the treasure remains hidden.
properties.location_name: Brading (Isle of Wight) - Tree (no longer standing) in the area once known as Centurion's Copse

properties.name: Treasure Hunter
properties.description: The area is reportedly haunted by the shade of an Edwardian woman looking for a hoard of treasure she hid while living in a building along the road. She is said to be more likely to be seen by dogs, which react by howling for no apparent reason, rather than people.
properties.location_name: Braishfield (Hampshire) - Dark Lane, and the surrounding area

properties.name: Demon Bird
properties.description: A large black bird was once said to protect a pile of hidden gold within the castle grounds. The hoard could not be moved unless the bones of Lord Beauchamp, former owner of the gold, were in possession of the treasure hunter.

properties.name: Gold Guard
properties.description: Protecting hidden treasure, this shade once attacked a man who tried to take all the gold - he died soon after of a wasting disease. It is not known whether the spectral woman who has also been seen here is the same ghost, although most legends say she does guard the hoard, which in turn is said to be haunted by the wailing of its former owners. Another legend says that whoever falls asleep here may wake in the past or future.

properties.name: Secret Cavern
properties.description: An old tradition said that there was a secret cavern under the caves which led to a chest of treasure and another exit located somewhere on the other side of the Severn. The floor of the cave was thoroughly dug in 1877 in an effort to find the hidden entrance, but the search found nothing.

properties.name: Hoard
properties.description: A local legend says that treasure is (or was) concealed in this area, though the details are vague.

properties.name: Black Monk
properties.description: Many believe that this ghostly monk in black has returned to watch over a pile of treasure which was hidden during his lifetime. The figure has been spotted several times over the last two hundred years, most recently around Christmas 1948 when he was seen walking down the church aisle.

properties.name: Golden Gates
properties.description: Local legend once said a secret tunnel ran between these two sites, with golden gates set within.
properties.location_name: Bromholm (Norfolk) - Tunnel between Bromholm Priory and Gimingham hall

properties.name: Excalibur
properties.description: The lake is another contender for the claim for being the resting place for King Arthur's sword. Another legend says the lake is home to a treasure that can only be retrieved by chain made by seventh generation blacksmith that is pulled by a team of twin horses, twin oxen, and twin youths.
properties.location_name: Broomlee Lough (also referred to as Bromley Lake) (Northumberland) - Waters of the lake

properties.name: Golden Table
properties.description: A deep well near the hillfort was said to contain a golden table - a recovery attempt caused a minor earth tremor, so work was stopped.
properties.location_name: Buckland Newton (Dorset) - Dungeon Hill Hillfort (private land)

properties.name: Sir Henry Paget
properties.description: The ghost of Sir Henry, 1st Marquess of Anglesey, is one of many phantoms claimed to haunt this site. Other spooks include participants from the English Civil War and a white (or grey) female form that stands on the moat bridge. Another piece of folklore claims the Knights Templar concealed a large portion of their treasure somewhere on the site, including the Holy Grail.

properties.name: Goblet of Gold
properties.description: A goblet of gold (or it could be filled with gold) is hidden in this area. Thomas the Rhymer declared that the treasure could only be found during a full moon by two brothers, one of whom would kill the other.
properties.location_name: Carden Den (Fife) - Exact area not known

properties.name: White Lady
properties.description: A 'new' building on the site of thirteenth century ruins, the White Lady once haunted the older area, and now has set up shop in the replacement castle. The woman is said to have died of a broken heart after her son drowned in a local pond. There is also reported to be a buried hoard of treasure here, zealously protected by either three eagles or an entity who confuses any potential gold thieves, while an ethereal cavalier walks the castle grounds.

properties.name: Little People
properties.description: Once a popular place for pixies to frequent and dance, they have not been seen in recent years. They are said to be protecting a concealed hoard of gold. A large rock in the area was said to feature a tiny footprint.

properties.name: Treasure Under A Stone
properties.description: A man had a dream that if he went to the mountain on Monday morning, he would see a crow sitting on a stone, and under this stone would be treasure that once belonged to a Norwegian prince. Unfortunately, the man was impatient and set off early, arriving Sunday evening to find hundreds of crows, each sitting on a different rock.
properties.date: Said to have occurred early nineteenth century
properties.location_name: Carn Glas (Highland) - Rock on the mountain

properties.name: King Gerennius
properties.description: King Gerennius was thought to have been buried here within a ship of gold that used silver oars, though when the barrow was opened in 1855, no ship was found.

properties.name: Gold Coffin
properties.description: The barrow was once thought to contain a golden coffin, though when excavated in 1933, no coffin was discovered.

properties.name: Fairy Treasure
properties.description: This rocky pile was once said to be home to fairies and witches, and it concealed a massive wealth of treasure within.
properties.location_name: Castel (Guernsey) - Rocky outcrop in the parish of Ste Marie du Castel

properties.name: Horses
properties.description: A man who tried to break into this barrow was driven mad by the sound of horses - each time the robber reached into the hole he had made, the sound of horses could be heard, though the creatures could not be seen. The man eventually lost his sight and hearing, dying three months later.
properties.location_name: Challacombe (Devon) - Broken Barrow (exact location not known)

properties.name: Pan
properties.description: A pan made from brass and filled with gold and silver was said to be concealed beneath this barrow. One treasure hunter was said to have been driven insane by phantom horses which followed him around the area.

properties.name: Concealed Treasure
properties.description: This stone, made of sand and gravel, is said to be unmovable, which makes acquiring the treasure buried beneath pretty damn hard to reach.
properties.location_name: Chertsey (Surrey) - Large stone on St Anne's Hill, was known as the Devil's Stone

properties.name: Coffin
properties.description: Nine barrows can be found here, but only one is said to contain a golden coffin.

properties.name: Mermaid
properties.description: This mermaid was so upset at the way she was treated by onlookers, she drove to the bottom of the pool and never returned. It was thought that she guarded a mass of treasure.

properties.name: Non-Removable Treasure
properties.description: It was once believed that a golden coffin was contained within the barrow, and any attempt to remove it would fail.
properties.location_name: Chiseldon (Wiltshire) - Barrow along Gypsy Lane (southwest of village)

properties.name: Dwarf
properties.description: This little man is said to stand guard over a fabulous wealth, once concealed from greedy men.

properties.name: White Woman
properties.description: A white female phantom is said to walk this area. It is unclear whether the ghost is that of an old woman who once lived in a nearby cave and watched over a hoard of treasure.
properties.location_name: Churchill (Oxfordshire) - Crossroads close to Lyneham Longbarrow

properties.name: Calf
properties.description: A golden calf is said to be buried under a hill in the area, possibly under a barrow.

properties.name: Horse & Rider
properties.description: A member of this Ardrum House family died and, as requested in his will, was buried with his favourite horse. A rumour started that a the burial included small amount of gold, and driven by desperation, three local men attempted to dig up the grave. As they started work, they heard the rack of a whip, and the air filled with the noise of hooves bearing down upon them. Nothing could be seen so the men fled.
properties.location_name: Cloghroe (County Cork) - Concealed grave a few hundred metres from Ardrum House (no longer standing)

properties.name: Wealthy Farmer
properties.description: The ghost of a farmer whose wealth was never discovered is said to still be around the area, waiting for a person willing to follow him to the hidden money.
properties.location_name: Clopton (Suffolk) - Farm next to Clopton Hall

properties.name: Smuggler's Gold
properties.description: This stash of gold was hidden by a smuggler in an area which came to be known as the Golden Rock. It was said that the hoard was protected by the ghost of a murdered servant.
properties.location_name: Cobh (County Cork) - Spike Island - Golden Rock

properties.name: Treasure Guardian
properties.description: A decapitated female ghost was reported to stand guard over a hidden hoard of gold near the tower of this building.

properties.name: Golden Calf
properties.description: Buried under this Iron Age hillfort is reputed to be a golden calf.

properties.name: Pot of Gold
properties.description: One folktale from this area states that a farmer dreamt of the exact location where a pot of gold had been buried on his land. Rather than dig it up, the farmer told a family member who waited until nightfall and dug on the spot without the farmer knowing. Finding the gold, the thief fled. Simultaneously, the farmer had a stroke which left him unable to speak.
properties.location_name: Coppeen (County Cork) - Unidentified Farm in the area

properties.name: Treasure
properties.description: This area is said to contain hidden wealth.
properties.location_name: Copythorne (Hampshire) - Barrow Hill (aka Money Hills), Stonyford

properties.name: Kettle Gold
properties.description: This well was fed from three springs, one curing deafness, the second blindness, the third lameness, although the price of removing an ailment was gold. An entity administered the area, storing donations in a kettle which was buried under what was known as the Kettle Stone. Anyone who tried to remove the stone and steal the gold would die a terrible death soon after.
properties.location_name: Corgarff (Moray) - Well at the base of a steep hill (unsure if still present)

properties.name: Rustling Woman
properties.description: Dressed in dark clothing, this feminine shade protects a hoard of buried treasure. The silk of the ghost's dress can normally be heard in the wind. One field in the area is said to be impossible to cultivate.
properties.location_name: Cornwood (Devon) - Fardel Hall (also called Fardell Hall), area between hall and nearby bridge

properties.name: Unlit
properties.description: The site was once believed to be haunted by a ghost which would extinguish a light in a certain room. Another legend said that a spot close to the house contained buried treasure.

properties.name: Seven Year Treasure
properties.description: If one were to walk along this coastline before dawn every day for seven years, on the last day of the period, one would find enough treasure to ensure they lived comfortably for the rest of their life.

properties.name: Man in the Golden Chair
properties.description: A secret tunnel near here was once believed to lead to a room full of gold and jewels, looked after by an old man who waited in a golden chair to hand over the wealth to whoever found it first. A piper and his dog were once sent in to investigate the tunnel - a short time later, the dog came out terrified and the man never seen again.

properties.name: Green Lady
properties.description: This ghostly tall woman, wearing a green dress, is said to search the graveyard for the remains of her Royalist husband. The second myth from this church concerns a great treasure that is said to have been buried here along with human remains from a Civil War battle. Unfortunately, the hoard is protected by a headless phantom that intervenes if anyone comes too close to discovering it.

properties.name: Treasure Protector
properties.description: Little is known about this ghost, other than he is said to protect a hoard of treasure, and twelve clergymen failed to exorcise him. Maybe the clergymen were more successful than they believed - the legend dates to at least 1893, but in April 2008 a hoard of golden coins were discovered in farmland in the village.
properties.location_name: Dallinghoo (Suffolk) - High gate post - exact location not known

properties.name: Dazzling Woman
properties.description: A woman bathed in dazzling white was seen close to these ruins. A myth says that a golden carriage is buried somewhere nearby.
properties.location_name: Darchet (Berkshire) - Old Monastery between Darchet and Old Windsor

properties.name: Guardian Monster
properties.description: Protecting a hoard of treasure, this beast has teamed up with a raven which calls out if anyone comes too close to the gold. The beast will then wait until a rope is lowered into the pit and then sever it when the treasure hunter is climbing down. The body would always be found outside the hole in the morning.

properties.name: Golden Coffin
properties.description: This barrow was once believed to have contained a coffin of solid gold - excavations have never found such an item.

properties.name: Hidden Cave
properties.description: A secret entrance on the hill leads to a hidden cave which contains treasure, so the story goes.
properties.location_name: Delvin (County Westmeath) - Unknown hill in the area, (off the road to Ballinlough?)

properties.name: Horse and Rider
properties.description: A figure on horseback has been observed in the distance, holding a shield and sword. Perhaps connected to this entity, the sounds of a horse charging through woodland have been reported. There may be an additional relationship to the nearby Denbury Hill Iron Age hill fort which folklore says contains golden treasure.
properties.location_name: Denbury Down (Devon) - Woodland in the area

properties.name: White Spotted Dog
properties.description: A secret room in the moat was said to contain nine kegs of gold protected by a large black dog with a white spot. The only way to escape with the gold was to stab the dog three times with a dagger to the white spot.

properties.name: Buried Boulder
properties.description: This large stone is said to have buried itself, and underneath is hidden treasure.
properties.location_name: Digby (Lincolnshire) - Exact location unknown - somewhere on the edge of the fen, near the Dorrington boundary

properties.name: Flying Beast
properties.description: To guard the treasure buried under both hills, this beast flies between the two locations each night.
properties.location_name: Dolbury (aka Dolebury) (Devon) - Exe valley between Dolbury Hill & Cadbury Hill

properties.name: Treasure Guardians
properties.description: A team of mermaids and mermen are reported to guard a hidden cave full of treasure.
properties.location_name: Duncansby Head (Highland) - Cave system off coast

properties.name: Golden Cradle
properties.description: With its location now covered now covered by trees, this mythical treasure is unlikely to be found any time soon.

properties.name: Sir Guy the Seeker
properties.description: Sir Guy's spirit cannot be laid to rest until he rediscovers the maiden that he once stumbled upon while hiding in these ruins from a storm. Another ghost on site is the Earl of Lancaster, executed on the site (though the executioner was so incompetent it took almost a dozen blows to remove the Earl's head). Finally, a woman lies enchanted somewhere in the ruins, and will awaken when the 'right knight' blows his horn. It is thought that the same woman is responsible for the shining stones which could once be found in the area, which were considered part of her hidden treasure to be awarded to the person who saved her.

properties.name: Chair
properties.description: A golden chair is reputed to sit at the bottom of this barrow.

properties.name: Golden Coffin
properties.description: This is another location where a golden coffin is reputed to be buried.

properties.name: Cycling Through
properties.description: The guides of the underground passages, normally open to the public, tell of a phantom cyclist who passes through the area. There is also rumoured to be hidden treasure somewhere in the tunnels.

properties.name: Polish Gentleman
properties.description: After he died, the noble from Poland left a great deal of money to friends and relatives, but the hidden funds could not be found. Though his shade was seen shortly after death, no one has had to courage to face the ghost and ask where his treasure is concealed.
properties.location_name: Fearn (Highland) - Area between Fearn and Nigg, exact location not known

properties.name: Soldier
properties.description: An unknown amount of gold hidden somewhere in this area is guarded by a ghostly soldier.
properties.location_name: Feenish Island (County Galway) - Area around castle

properties.name: Gold Protector
properties.description: The castle is haunted by a spirit that protects a hidden pot of gold. If the treasure is removed from the castle, it is said that the ghost will not rest until it has returned the pot to the hiding place.

properties.name: Coffin
properties.description: This is another location where a silver coffin is reputed to be buried.

properties.name: Storm
properties.description: A group of men tried to remove this stone to reach the mythical treasure beneath. A storm blew up, the wind, thunder and lightning driving them away.

properties.name: The Spittal Hill Tut
properties.description: Nicknamed the Spittal Hill Tut, this ghostly entity would take the form of a small horse. The entity grabbed one man passing through the area on horseback, almost crushing him to death. Some believed the Tut existed to protect some treasure concealed beneath the hill.

properties.name: Silence
properties.description: A chest of gold remains hidden in this area and can only be discovered by treasure hunters who remain completely silent while searching for the hoard.

properties.name: Golden Plough
properties.description: This mound was once thought to contain treasure in the form of a golden plough.

properties.name: Pot of Gold
properties.description: A woman named Molly is said to have had a dream about finding gold at this location. The story ends either with Molly being struck by lightning as she lifts the stone, a ghost scaring her away, a ghost asking her husband to name five local churches (which he fails to do), or Molly recovering the gold.

properties.name: Hidden Hoard
properties.description: These two rows of standing stones are thought to contain hidden treasures.
properties.location_name: Gatehouse of Fleet (Dumfries and Galloway) - Cairnholy Cairns

properties.name: Dead Husband
properties.description: Sheela, a giant's wife, killed her husband after throwing their bed at him - he had argued with her and struck her shoulder with an axe. His body still lays under the tomb, and Sheela can be heard in times of national crisis calling out her husband's name. Another story says four treasure hunters arrived at the stone and started to dig beneath it. Before long, a cat appeared with a burning tail. The light blinded the men and they staggered off, one falling into the River Funshion and drowning.
properties.location_name: Glanworth (County Cork) - Labbacallee (aka The Hag's Bed), wedge tomb

properties.name: White Horse
properties.description: It was once said that a phantom white horse haunted a spot at the hall where a former owner had concealed a cache of riches.

properties.name: Coach
properties.description: A golden coach was thought to be buried under the hill.

properties.name: Waiting Wife
properties.description: Lady de Blenkinsopp is said to wait for her husband to return from the crusades, where he went after a disagreement over gold. Some reports state that the treasure is still hidden on the site.

properties.name: Twenty-four Carat Gold Table
properties.description: A table of solid gold is said to be hidden somewhere in or under this castle, protected by a gruesome dwarf.

properties.name: Golden Calf
properties.description: A secret passage was said to run between these two structures, and within was hidden a calf sculptured from gold.
properties.location_name: Gresham (Norfolk) - Between Gresham Castle (no longer standing) and Beeson Abbey

properties.name: Black Monk
properties.description: The monk that is said to appear at these ruins once a year has been rarely seen since the 1960s. A large chest of gold is also said to be buried in the area, watched over by a raven (or the monk, depending on the story source).
properties.date: January - first new moon of the year (reoccurring)

properties.name: Wealthy Dragon
properties.description: It is thought that the wyrm which established its home in the hill brought with him a great deal of treasure.
properties.location_name: Gunnarton Fell (Northumberland) - Gunnarton Castle, aka Money Hill

properties.name: Avery's Treasure
properties.description: A buccaneer named Avery was said to have buried several chests of treasure in this area before sailing away, never to return. A Mr John Knill failed to find any treasure after searching the area in 1770.
properties.location_name: Gunwalloe (Cornwall) - Within the sandbanks (or Kennack cove)

properties.name: Coffin
properties.description: Another barrow that is said to contain a gold or silver coffin.

properties.name: Silver
properties.description: The new owner of a house forced open a room that had remained locked for several years due to a rumoured ghost. While no ghosts were discovered, two solid silver pieces of plate were found in old boxes that fell apart when touched.

properties.name: Soldiers with Mules
properties.description: A group of Roman soldiers with pack animals slowly make their way up these steps. Some believe that the ghostly posse will lead anyone who tries to follow them to gold.
properties.location_name: Harlech (Gwynedd) - Roman Steps leading up the Rhinog Fawr mountains

properties.name: Treasure
properties.description: A piece of folklore likely to go back to at least the eighteenth century says that treasure is buried somewhere around this area, although its source and value is unstated.
properties.location_name: Harmby (North Yorkshire) - Around the area of All Saint's Chapel

properties.name: William of Soulis
properties.description: Murdered by peasants who had grown tired of his wicked ways, William now returns to rendezvous with a wizard deep under the castle ruins. It is said a phantom Mary Queen of Scots haunts the building, though there is no evidence she ever visited. Another entity is also reported here - starved to death in the dungeon, the ghost of Sir Alex Ramsey now walks the castle grounds. In addition to the ghosts, some believe there is hidden treasure under the ruins of the castle, but the Devil watches over it - when people have tried to dig for the hoard in the past, violent storms have driven them away.
properties.date: Every seven years, last heard 1885 (exact date unknown) (reoccurring)

properties.name: Gold
properties.description: A clay pot containing gold is said to be buried under Simon's Barrow.

properties.name: Ringing Road
properties.description: A dip in the road would ring whenever a cart moved across it. Two men excavated the spot and discovered a secret doorway. They decided to stop for dinner, but as they left the spot, a storm blew up and filled the hole.

properties.name: Treasure Light
properties.description: Marking the spot of buried treasure, this flaming marker has a habit of self-extinguishing before anyone comes too close.

properties.name: Treasure Hunter
properties.description: The jutting rock is said to have treasure concealed beneath it, though it is now protected by the ghost of a man who tried to remove the stone to find the hidden wealth - his body was found crushed under the stone.
properties.location_name: Higher Combe (Somerset) - The Caratacus Stone, just outside the village

properties.name: Table
properties.description: This well is said to contain a table made either of silver or gold that no one has yet managed to recover.

properties.name: Secret Passage
properties.description: A piece of local folklore said the castle and Holmesfield Hall were connected by a secret passage. Contained within the passage sat an iron box filled with treasure, but it was protected by a cockerel which would crow if approached.

properties.name: A Voice Asks for a Spade
properties.description: Supposed to house hidden gold, the street is haunted by a voice that, on nights of a full moon, tells any listeners to 'pick up your spade and follow me'. To date, nobody has accepted the challenge.

properties.name: Soldier?
properties.description: An area covered in rocks which could have been part of a tumulus was said to contain gold and the bodies of soldiers. It was said to be haunted.
properties.location_name: Hyde (Gloucestershire) - Unnamed stony knoll in area (likely to be no longer present)

properties.name: Cromwell's Treasure
properties.description: A local legend said that Cromwell had concealed a wealth of treasure within one of the barrows.

properties.name: Invisible Stone
properties.description: Even though only a handful of stones can now be seen, it was reported that there were once twelve stones, but only eleven could be seen. If anyone could find the twelfth, they would have been rewarded with riches.

properties.name: Headless Figure
properties.description: The barrow is said to be either haunted or protected (or both?) by a headless figure. At least one barrow in the village is said to contain a solid gold (or silver) coffin, though when the barrow was dug up in the early twentieth century, nothing was found.
properties.location_name: Inkpen (Berkshire) - Barrow located on Sadler's Farm

properties.name: Gold Dorothy
properties.description: A woman named Dorothy from Durness had a dream about discovering gold under a stone. She dug in the area but found nothing. Twelve months later she had the same dream, and taking a friend, dug at the same spot again. They found nothing. Another twelve months later, the same dream, and she dug a third time. She found nothing, but she did gain the nickname Gold Dorothy. She never dreamed about the treasure again.
properties.location_name: Inshore (Highland) - Unknown stone in the area

properties.name: Monks
properties.description: While the Cowell's building stood here (a monastery was formally on the site), a few employees reported observing an old monk. Two women, on separate occasions, were supposed to have fainted after seeing the figure, and at least one man had fled during a night shift. Phantom footsteps were also heard in the factory, and perhaps more recently by a cleaner at work in the shopping centre, with the sound accompanied by jangling keys. A local legend says that hundreds of years ago, when the monastery was under attack, the occupants hid their treasure down a well on the site - it was never recovered and remains to this day.
properties.location_name: Ipswich (Suffolk) - WS Cowell's, now the site of the Buttermarket Centre

properties.name: Two Headed Dog
properties.description: A pool in the area is said to contain treasure, though unfortunately it is guarded by a large hound with two heads. A man who managed to drain the pool of all its contents was visited by the hound, resulting in him reversing his actions and replacing the water.

properties.name: Bull
properties.description: A pot of gold hidden in the area was protected by a bull which appeared whenever treasure hunters came to the area. Legend said that the only person who could claim the money as his own had the name Foley.

properties.name: Protective Voice
properties.description: A hoard of treasure is reported to be hidden under the bridge, though if anyone comes too close to discovering it, a voice talks them into leaving the scene empty handed.

properties.name: Coffin
properties.description: A golden coffin is reputed to be buried under this barrow.

properties.name: Pendragon
properties.description: Uther Pendragon, the father of King Arthur, resided here until he and a hundred of his men were poisoned by the Saxon invaders. Some believe Pendragon's ghost remains here. Another ghost, that of Sir Hugh de Morville, haunts the building as punishment for murdering Thomas Becket. Finally, a hoard of treasure hidden here is protected by a phantom black hen that replaces the soil of any hole dug just as quick as it is removed!

properties.name: Cooper
properties.description: Cooper was a highwayman who, so successful at robbing the rich, died a wealthy man. Cooper was buried with his treasure along the road he committed many of his crimes, and the place post-mortem he continues to dwell.
properties.location_name: Lacey Green (Buckinghamshire) - Coopers Grave (area of road), two stones at the junction of a bridleway

properties.name: Guardians
properties.description: Strange shadowy entities once stood guard over the burial chambers, cursing anyone who removed treasure or relics. In one instance, the entities followed home a man who removed a skull and tore around his home like a whirlwind. The man returned the skull the following day, but even then, the phantoms remained, until the man was thrown from his horse and killed a few months later.
properties.location_name: Lagg, Isle of Arran (Ayrshire) - Torrylin Cairn

properties.name: Gold
properties.description: A golden hoard is said to be concealed around this area. It is unclear whether this treasure relates to the phantom Romans said to walk the area.

properties.name: Gold Guardians
properties.description: This hill was formed when a giant buried his treasure, leaving a troupe of warrior fairies to protect it. They have been called into action at least once, appearing as a would-be treasure hunter arrived armed with pick and shovel.

properties.name: Katie Neevie's Hoord
properties.description: A local rhyme reads 'Between Dillerhill and Crossford, there lies Katie Neevie's hoord'. It is thought that the hoard takes the form of gold concealed within a kettle, a boot and a bull hide.
properties.location_name: Lesmahagow (Lanarkshire) - A very large stone hidden on a farm in the area

properties.name: Knight
properties.description: A knight in gold armour and a silver coffin are said to be concealed under this fort. The fort was once believed to have created by Old Nick, one of the mounds of dirt he left behind while digging the nearby 'Devil's Dyke'.
properties.location_name: Lewes (Sussex) - Mount Caburn - iron age hillfort

properties.name: Golden Warrior
properties.description: While a local legend said that a warrior clad in gold was buried here; when excavated in 1924, gold and silver objects were found (but no golden warrior).

properties.name: Macduff's Castle
properties.description: Traces of a castle thought to have belonged to Macduff exist in the village, and an old legend says that before the castle was ransacked, the treasure was thrown into a lake.
properties.location_name: Lindores (Fife) - Body of water nicknamed Sillar Lake (likely to be currently known as Lindores Loch)

properties.name: Headless Horseman
properties.description: Holding his head under one arm, this horseman was said to have manifested when a small group of men attempted to dig up the barrow while looking for treasure (reputed to be a golden image).
properties.location_name: Litchfield (Hampshire) - Angledown Barrow (aka Angle Down Barrow)

properties.name: No Gold
properties.description: It was thought that a golden hoard was concealed under the stone, though searches have found nothing.

properties.name: Chest of Wealth
properties.description: This chest of fabulous treasure is buried somewhere in the area and is protected by a large black raven. This bird once told a would-be treasure hunter that the wealth belongs to one man and one man alone.

properties.name: Solid Gold
properties.description: Hidden under the Grey Mare is reputedly a coffin made from solid gold. There is also a local legend that connects the barrow to a phantom horse that runs around the area.
properties.location_name: Littlebredy (aka Little Bredy) (Dorset) - The Grey Mare & Her Colts (long barrow)

properties.name: Pirates
properties.description: After concealing a hoard of gold and other treasures, these ghostly pirates still protect their stolen wealth.

properties.name: Druid
properties.description: A farmer who thought the prehistoric monument contained treasure entered the chamber, only to come face-to-face with a pale ghostly druid. The farmer fled, but later returned and realised that the figure was just a tall white stone.

properties.name: Harpist
properties.description: The sound of a harp that can be heard at the stretch of the river known as 'Pool of the Harper' is thought to belong to a performer who drowned there. The banks of the river, which stretches for some 75 miles, are said to be home to a couple of phantom women. A woman in green wearing emeralds is said to protect a hidden treasure (details of which are sadly lacking), while a woman dressed from head to toe in black drifts around looking for something (or someone).

properties.name: Basking Beast
properties.description: This creature, with a head like that of a horse and a single hump, rested ten metres from the shoreline, before slowing sinking beneath the water. A legend says that the Jacobites once hid a collection of treasure in the waters of the loch, where it remains.
properties.location_name: Loch Arkaig (Highland) - Waters of the loch

properties.name: John's Treasure
properties.description: The stagnant pool of water was reputed to be the place where King John's wealth was lost. One report stated that some of the treasure was recovered when the area was partly drained.
properties.location_name: Long Sutton (Lincolnshire) - King John's Hole, pool of water halfway between town and King's Lynn (may no longer be present)

properties.name: Golden Horseshoes
properties.description: A horse shod with golden shoes is said to be buried in this area.
properties.location_name: Longstock (Hampshire) - Wooded area on the downs known as Money Bunt

properties.name: Money Coffin
properties.description: A local myth says that one thousand guineas are buried on this site, concealed within a coffin.

properties.name: Turning Stone
properties.description: This hunk of rock rotates three times on hearing a cockerel crowing in the morning. The treasure under the stone should be accessible during this time but hampered by the fact that only one particular cockerel will animate the rock.

properties.name: Secret Tunnel
properties.description: A secret tunnel said to exist between Friars Lough and the village contained treasure hidden by monks.

properties.name: Edmond Roe O'Kennedy
properties.description: Edmond was murdered in the sixteenth century before telling anyone where he had concealed his hidden treasure. Since his death, Edmond's shade has appeared to visitors at the ruined site, blood leaking from a large slit in his throat.

properties.name: Fire Breathing Hound
properties.description: This abomination with cloven hooves protects a hidden hoard of treasure somewhere near this house.

properties.name: Ruined Treasure
properties.description: This barrow was destroyed after treasure hunters failed to find evidence of a burial chamber. Stories of a valuable hoard located in the area still exist.
properties.location_name: Marden (Wiltshire) - Area where Hatfield Barrow once stood

properties.name: Meg
properties.description: Meg of Meldon had a reputation for being a ruthless miser, a canny businesswoman, and a witch. Her ghost was said to travel from the village to her former home, resting for seven years every seven years. Her spirit was once seen in the form of a little dog close to a bridge but was more likely to take the form of a pretty woman or flickering lights of many colours. Another part of her legend says that Meg hid her treasure around the area, within wells, in ceilings, and close to Meldon tower.
properties.date: After dark, seven years on, seven years off
properties.location_name: Meldon (Northumberland) - Between Meldon and Hartington Hall

properties.name: No Treasure
properties.description: James Gillies has three dreams which involved discovering a hoard of treasure buried under a stone. Alas, he told several people of his dream, and when he finally found time to go and look under the stone, all he found was a hole and evidence that the hidden treasure had recently been taken.
properties.location_name: Meldon (Northumberland) - Unnamed field containing standing stone

properties.name: Gentle Music
properties.description: Three children reported hearing a battle coming from outside the castle and seeing a knight on horseback who charged at them. Inside the castle, muffled sixteenth century music has been heard, while there are persistent rumours of buried treasure somewhere in the grounds (which can only be found after running nonstop around the fort nine time and quickly entering the mysterious door which magically opens!).

properties.name: Coffin
properties.description: One of these barrows (there are three) is said to contain a golden coffin. A storm is said to blow in if anyone starts to dig for it. Some sources mention that headless phantom coffin bearers haunt the area, which may only be visible to the D'Urberville family.

properties.name: Gold
properties.description: The barrow here was said to have contained a crock of gold.

properties.name: Highwayman
properties.description: A phantom highwayman would ride around this pit before heading towards Callow Spong (where he would vanish). The pit itself was said to be the home of the Devil, who dragged one man down into its waters after he tried to fish out a chest of coins.
properties.location_name: Moulton St Mary (Norfolk) - Callow (or Caller) Pit

properties.name: Giant's Gold
properties.description: One myth says that alongside the giant's body is a golden hoard. The last time anyone tried to dig it up, a phantom bull could be heard, scaring the diggers away.
properties.location_name: Murroe (County Limerick) - Garranbane, aka Giant's Grave

properties.name: Looks Like Stormy Weather
properties.description: When a man tried to find the hidden hoard of gold contained within the manmade mound, a huge storm blew up scaring the (would-be) thief away.

properties.name: Guiding Light
properties.description: An unearthly ball of light is said to periodically appear on the hill to lead treasure hunters to a mass of treasure buried somewhere here. However, once the digging site is reached, a violent storm will spring up and drive the would-be raiders away. The hill is another contender for the final resting place of Boudicca.

properties.name: Blind Peter
properties.description: On Halloween three misty women appear and walk along the ruin's walls. The sounds of bells can also be heard on this night. Another presence is Blind Peter. He is less choosey on the date on which he appears and just wanders around the ruins of the abbey. Finally, there is a legend that a secret underground passage was sealed after the last man to enter it emerged shortly after, screaming 'block it up!' before dropping dead - the passage may be connected to the tales of treasure buried on site.

properties.name: Old Lady in a Black Dress
properties.description: The earliest ghost sighting here dates to 1820 when a few prisoners were scared 'half to death' by something indescribable. In 1844 the Devil, dancing away on the walls, was seen by two witnesses. More recently, staff at the castle museum have reported watching an old lady wearing black float around the grounds. It is unclear if this sighting relates to the old ghost story of Martha Alden, who was executed for murdering her husband in 1807 and took to haunting the castle hill. Finally, there are rumours that the hill on which the castle stands conceals the grave of an old king, together with his hoard of silver and gold.

properties.name: Golden Coffin
properties.description: The long barrow was said to conceal a coffin made from gold. Nothing was found when the stones were exposed, and now the site has been (badly) covered up.

properties.name: Golden Coffin
properties.description: A barrow in this area was thought to contain a golden coffin. When treasure hunters tried to dig into the barrow, their tools broke and were prevented from completing their task. The barrow was also said to contain a nest of vipers.

properties.name: Could be Cromwell
properties.description: The jury is out to whether this shade is that of Oliver Cromwell, who is responsible for destroying the building, or that of a former family member looking for lost treasure (in the form of a golden calf). In addition, the ruins are also home to several phantom monks and a Puritan on horseback that gallops up the driveway only to vanish near the church.

properties.name: Madame Hannah Beswick
properties.description: Though the building no longer stands, the ghost of Beswick was also said to haunt her former lands, watching over the gold that she had buried to protect it from thieves and soldiers. The shade reportedly stands tall, wearing a black silk dress and frilly cap, although sometimes she was headless. According to legend, her presence grows stronger every seventh anniversary of her death (in 1758), and she was also seen on clear nights. During the 1950s it was claimed that 35 Ferranti workers had seen her on different occasions.
properties.date: Weather Dependent: clear nights. Last seen 1950s
properties.location_name: Oldham (Greater Manchester) - Birchen Bower (building no longer stands, but replaced by Ferranti Works), Bower Lane, Hollinwood

properties.name: Golden Coffin
properties.description: This is another location where a golden coffin is reputed to be buried.

properties.name: Targets
properties.description: Three tumuli here were said to have been used by Robin Hood for target practice. The area is also reputedly home to a phantom procession of soldiers, and when treasure hunters tried to raid the tumuli, the dirt is said to have replaced itself as quick as it was removed.

properties.name: De Mandeville
properties.description: Dying in an accident while trying to bury his treasure (or by suicide by drowning himself in the moat), Sir Geoffrey De Mandeville has returned to watch over his fortune, dressed in his knightly armour.
properties.location_name: Outer London: Enfield (Greater London) - Camlet Moat, Enfield Chase

properties.name: Sunken Treasure
properties.description: A ship laden with treasure is reported to have been lost in the time of Queen Elizabeth.

properties.name: No Chest
properties.description: Mother Shipton was said to have prophesied that when the hill was cut open, a black chest filled with money would be found. Alas, when the hill was cut into by railway workers in 1851, no treasure was discovered.

properties.name: Man
properties.description: The ghost of a man appeared to a farm labourer several times. The labourer finally listened to the entity who told the man to go to Neath and dig at a certain location. The labourer did as instructed and found enough gold to construct a row of cottages.

properties.name: Gold
properties.description: One of the barrows in this region is said to contain a fantastic golden coffin.

properties.name: Chest of Gold
properties.description: Every attempt to find this hoard of gold is met with failure; it is protected by a hen capable of extinguishing candles and, when the hen fails, violent storms which rise from nowhere.
properties.location_name: Reeth (North Yorkshire) - Maiden's Castle (Iron Age fort)

properties.name: White Woman
properties.description: This cave is haunted by a greedy woman who entered it looking for hidden treasure - a sudden storm blew up, trapping and drowning her.
properties.location_name: Rhosili (West Glamorgan) - Paviland Cave, between village and Port Eynon

properties.name: King Arthur
properties.description: Beneath the keep of the castle, deep underground, is a secret room where King Arthur rests until Britain needs him. A young drummer who was sent down a tunnel to find the hideout vanished without trace, though his phantom drumming can still be heard underground. Finally, the castle's Gold Hole Tower had associated folklore which said gold was concealed underneath, although no evidence of this has ever been uncovered.

properties.name: Ghost's Treasure
properties.description: A tailor's wife was asked by a ghost to cast some treasure, gained during the phantom's lifetime by nefarious means, into the river so it could rest easy. The woman did as asked, but tossed the treasure upstream rather than downstream, condemning the ghost to remain earthbound. The ghost created a strong wind which whisked the woman high into the air and she was later found unconscious along a lane.
properties.location_name: River Ogmore (South Glamorgan) - Exact location not known

properties.name: Hidden Gold
properties.description: Under these manmade hills is a fortune in gold - alas, it is impossible to recover, as any hole dug quickly refills itself by supernatural means.
properties.location_name: Robin Hood's Butts (Somerset) - Exact location unknown

properties.name: Golden Coffin
properties.description: This is another location where a golden coffin is reputed to be buried.

properties.name: Guarded Hoard
properties.description: Magically concealed, the treasure hidden here only stands a chance of discovery when the moon is full. However, if anyone comes to close to discovering the hoard, a phantom horse appears and charges the treasure hunter.

properties.name: Golden Table
properties.description: A golden table is reputed to be at the bottom of a well on the hill.

properties.name: Haunted Hoard
properties.description: Somewhere around an ash tree in the area known as Lidgett's Gap is said to be hidden treasure, though it is protected by a boggard.
properties.location_name: Scawby-cum-Sturton (Lincolnshire) - Corner formed by A18 and A15, between Scawby and Sturton, known as Lidgett's Gap

properties.name: Hidden Treasure
properties.description: An old poem states that 'Atween the wat grund and the dry, the gowd o' Tamleuchar doth lie'.

properties.name: Greed isn't Good
properties.description: The ghost of a recently deceased woman appeared to a farmer and asked him to dig up her hidden treasure - he could keep all the silver but had to give all the gold to her niece. The farmer dug up the treasure and kept it all to himself. The woman returned and haunted him day and night until he died soon after, her ghost witnessed by neighbours just prior to the farmer's body being found on his horse.
properties.location_name: Sexhow (North Yorkshire) - Unnamed farm in the area

properties.name: Monk
properties.description: When this man died, he was the only one to know the true location of the Abbey's hidden treasure. He now returns to ensure it remains hidden. It is worth noting that the ghost 'walks' on his knees - one assumes this is because of a change in ground level. Several people claimed to have seen a phantom man running around the ruins at night; some say that this was the ghost of King Edward.

properties.name: Arthur's Victory
properties.description: This hillfort has been named by some as the location where King Arthur defeated the Saxons. Several phantasmic events also surround the Rings, including the sighting of a ghostly warrior on horseback, a spectral woman wearing a black dress, and an ugly dwarf-like entity which peers into the windows of parked cars. Finally, there are stories of a golden coffin buried in the area.
properties.date: Battle in sixth century(?), entities in late twentieth century

properties.name: Coffin
properties.description: This barrow is another that was rumoured to contain a golden coffin.
properties.location_name: Shepton Mallet (Somerset) - Barrow on Beacon Hill

properties.name: Knocking Man
properties.description: The tapping sounds that are said to emerge from this mound come from a man hidden inside, rapping on his treasure chest to ensure it is still full. Other reports say that the ghostly figure walks up to the knoll and taps on the grass before vanishing.

properties.name: Treasure Guardian
properties.description: This large monolith's treasure could be seen in the hole left as the stone danced around its field.
properties.date: Stone still present, said to dance when a full moon falls upon Midsummer Night

properties.name: Chest of Gold
properties.description: Protected by a magical black bird, the chest can only be pulled from the ground by a dozen white oxen.
properties.location_name: Shorwell (Isle of Wight) - Elm tree in an unnamed wood near the area

properties.name: Tunnel
properties.description: A well in the village was said to have contained a tunnel that led to Sidbury hill. Somewhere within the tunnel is concealed a golden chair.

properties.name: Hidden Treasure
properties.description: Accidents or sudden death is said to have prevented treasure hunters from finding the hoard hidden somewhere here. Even if people have come close to finding it, strange noises have caused them to flee.
properties.location_name: Slad (Gloucestershire) - Area known as Pan's Wood (may no longer exist or has been renamed)

properties.name: Crinoline Lady
properties.description: The opening of a secret tunnel between a house and nearby church triggers a ghostly woman to leave the house and walk towards the Handley Monument where she vanishes. One story says anyone brave enough to ask her about hidden treasure would be given the directions to a fantastic hoard.
properties.date: Early twentieth century, when a secret passageway is opened
properties.location_name: Sleaford (Lincolnshire) - Boston Road towards Handley Monument

properties.name: Old Lady with Gold
properties.description: A maid venturing into the basement at night encountered a phantom elderly lady who instructed her to reach into an opening in the cellar wall. The maid did as instructed and pulled out a bag of gold and the deeds to the house. The maid, unsurprising, retired.
properties.date: Unknown - likely to be pre twentieth century
properties.location_name: South Shields (Tyne and Wear) - House along Thrift Street (no longer standing)

properties.name: Devil's Treasure
properties.description: A local legend states that the door handle of the church is all that remains of a treasure chest discovered by three (or two) men at the bottom of Callow Pit. As the men tried to take the chest away with them, Old Nick appeared and pulled at the other end - the men managed to escape with only one handle from the chest.
properties.location_name: Southwood (Norfolk) - Old church and Callow Pit

properties.name: Woman in Silk
properties.description: A phantom woman dressed in black silk was said to haunt this building, and anyone brave enough to follow her would be led to a vast amount of money. The loud rustling of her dress would normally alert anyone nearby to her presence.

properties.name: Haunted Woman
properties.description: A housekeeper who lived in this cottage after the death of her employer became haunted by his ghost. A prayer meeting was held in the cottage during which the ghost manifested to the housekeeper and revealed a hidden bag of coins, instructing the lady to take them and discard the loot into the nearby River Ogmore.
properties.location_name: St Donats (South Glamorgan) - Unnamed cottage near the castle

properties.name: Devil's Gold
properties.description: The stone was once thought to have gold concealed underneath, hidden by the Devil. However, when moved to make way for a new road, nothing was found.
properties.location_name: Staple Fitzpaine (Somerset) - Devil's Stone, near the church

properties.name: Accessed by Dreams?
properties.description: Folklore says that gold and silver had been found in the area after the searchers had had dreams and visions.

properties.name: Shuck with a Monk's Body and Dog's Head
properties.description: This strange entry in the 'shuck' category stands guard over a hidden hoard of gold. A legend states that St Felix instructed a monk and a dog to guard the buried treasure, and over the years the entities merged into a single presence.

properties.name: Highwayman
properties.description: A highwayman, some say Dick Turpin himself, hid some of his takings in this deserted medieval village. He died before managing to retrieve the money, and now he rides towards the village on horseback.

properties.name: Curse
properties.description: Treasure believed (by some) to be concealed in the area around the Iron Age hillfort has yet to be fully discovered - death and serious accidents have befallen all those to have tried. Another legend says that the treasure is protected by a dragon.

properties.name: Treasure Guardian
properties.description: Legend says this house was haunted - the farmer's wife met the ghost on the stairs, who told her that a great deal of wealth was concealed under the water pump. After she had found it, the ghost never troubled anyone again.

properties.name: There's Gold in Them Hills
properties.description: A chest of gold was said to have been buried somewhere around the summit of the hill.

properties.name: John Alcock
properties.description: Highwayman John Alcock stashed much of his ill-gotten gains in a cave under this hill, in a chest protected by three locks and guarded by a cockerel. The bird will only let treasure hunters pass if they possessed one of Alcock's bones. Another legend says the Devil created the hill; out picking nuts on Devil's Nutting Day (21 September), Old Nick stumbled across the Virgin Mary who ordered him to drop the nuts. The Devil did, thus creating the hill.
properties.location_name: Temple Grafton (Warwickshire) - Alcocks Arbour (aka Rolls Wood Hill), Croft Lane

properties.name: Calf
properties.description: In the nearby woodland, a golden calf is reputedly buried.

properties.name: Concealed House
properties.description: A local story states that a mansion lies buried under the hill, filled with wealth beyond dreams. Another story says that the hill was created by the Devil, created by mud falling from his shoe. Walking around the hill seven times at midnight is said to summon him.

properties.name: Gold
properties.description: Folklore says a bull's skin filled with gold was buried in this area.
properties.location_name: Thimbleby (North Yorkshire) - Farmstead in the area

properties.name: Coins
properties.description: A serving girl had a dream that treasure was buried in a field on the farm where she worked. She dug on the area and found many coins, which she distributed to her friends and neighbours. When the lord of the manor heard about the discovery, he declared the treasure belonged to him. The girl fled the neighbourhood, terrified of the lord's reaction once he realised that she had given most of the coins away.

properties.name: Serpent or Ghost
properties.description: The treasure in this small body of water is protected by either an eel-like serpent or the ghost of a servant, or possibly both.

properties.name: Hidden Gold
properties.description: An Upsall man had a dream that if he travelled to London Bridge, he would become rich. Arriving at the bridge, he met another man who said that he had also had a dream in which he had travelled to Upsall and dug up treasure by a bush near the castle but did not know where Upsall was located.
